4. Cool and observe. A **red colour** confirms the presence of **tyrosine**.

**Step-wise procedure for Hopkins–Cole Test (2 marks):**
1. Take 2 mL of food extract + 1 mL of **Hopkins–Cole reagent** (glyoxylic acid).
2. Carefully **layer concentrated H₂SO₄** down the side of the tube.
3. Observe the **interface** — a **violet ring** confirms **tryptophan**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Writing "purple" instead of "Ruhemann's purple"** in the Ninhydrin test — lose ½ mark.
> 2. **Confusing Millon's test (tyrosine) with Hopkins–Cole test (tryptophan)** — both involve colour changes, so students mix them up; lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to add NaOH before CuSO₄** in the Biuret test — lose ½ mark for the order-of-addition.
> 4. **Reporting a quantitative result > 100 %** without flagging the error — examiner deducts 2 marks for lack of scientific reasoning.
> 5. **Writing "gelatin gives positive Xanthoproteic test"** — incorrect. Gelatin has negligible aromatic amino acids, so the **alkali-shift to orange is negative**, even though initial yellow appears due to HNO₃. Lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Biuret = Peptide bond ≥ 2 → Violet.** Requires **NaOH first, then CuSO₄**, with **Rochelle salt** to prevent Cu(OH)₂ precipitation.
- **Ninhydrin = Free α-amino group → Ruhemann's purple.** Releases **CO₂** as a stoichiometric by-product. Detects **free amino acids** (Biuret cannot).
- **Xanthoproteic = Aromatic ring (Tyr, Trp, Phe) → Yellow → Orange on alkali.** Gelatin is a classic **negative-on-alkali** trap.
- **Millon's = Tyrosine phenol → Red / Brick-red.** Reagent is **toxic (Hg²⁺); use fume hood**.
- **Hopkins–Cole = Tryptophan indole → Violet ring at H₂SO₄ interface.** Glyoxylic acid is generated *in situ* from oxalic acid + Mg.
- **Lead Acetate = –SH / –S– (Cys, Met) → Black PbS precipitate** after alkaline hydrolysis.
- **Minimum sample requirement for Biuret:** **Tripeptide or larger**.
- **Order of reagent addition in Biuret:** **NaOH → CuSO₄** (NEVER reverse).
- **Quantitative basis:** **Beer–Lambert law $A = \varepsilon c l$** at **λ = 540 nm** (Biuret) and **λ = 570 nm** (Ninhydrin).
- **Standard protein for calibration:** **Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA), 1 mg/mL**.
- **Interferences in Biuret:** Ammonium salts, free amino acids (minimal), high concentrations of certain carbohydrates.
- **Interferences in Ninhydrin:** Ammonia, proline (gives **yellow** instead of purple — common viva question).
- **Sensitivity ranking (most → least):** Ninhydrin (µg) > Hopkins–Cole (mg) ≈ Xanthoproteic (mg) ≈ Millon's (mg) > Biuret (mg) > Lead Acetate (mg).
- **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorise **all six positive colours** in one glance from the table in Section 2.2; this single table alone can fetch 6–8 marks in the viva.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Mantra — "B-N-X-M-H-L":** **B**iuret, **N**inhydrin, **X**anthoproteic, **M**illon's, **H**opkins–Cole, **L**ead acetate. This is the exact order tested in the KTU 2024 lab manual and is the most efficient mnemonic for last-minute revision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Physical Parameters of Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Physical Parameters of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Physical parameters of water** are the measurable, instrument-dependent characteristics that describe the *visible, mechanical, and bulk physical state* of a water sample — without altering its chemical composition. According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 10500:2012)** and **APHA Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater (23rd Edition)**, the principal physical parameters include:

- **pH (Potential of Hydrogen)** – a logarithmic measure of hydrogen ion activity.
- **Turbidity** – optical property indicating suspended colloidal matter (measured in **NTU** – Nephelometric Turbidity Units).
- **Total Solids (TS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** – the residue left after evaporation, expressed in **mg/L** or **ppm**.
- **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** – the ability of water to conduct electricity, expressed in **µS/cm**.
- **Temperature** – typically reported in **°C**.
- **Colour & Odour** – qualitative organoleptic descriptors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In *GDFTL106 – Foundations of Food Technology Lab*, the "Estimation of Physical Parameters of Water" experiment is the **gateway quality-control test** for any food processing unit, because the quality of process water directly determines the shelf-life, taste, texture, and microbial safety of the finished product.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **barista preparing filter coffee** in Kerala. The tap water you use may *look* clear, but if you taste it, it might be salty, metallic, or smell like chlorine. Now imagine giving that same water to a guest without tasting it — they would complain. **Physical parameters are the "five senses" of a water quality scientist.** They tell us:

- **pH** = the *mood* of water (sour / neutral / bitter).
- **Turbidity** = how *dusty or muddy* the water appears (like looking through a dirty window).
- **TDS** = how much *invisible luggage* (dissolved minerals) the water is carrying.
- **Conductivity** = how easily electricity (or ions) flows through it.
- **Temperature** = the *thermal state* affecting all reactions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike *chemical parameters* (hardness, chlorine, nitrates) which require *reagents and titration*, physical parameters are mostly measured **instrumentally** using calibrated electronic probes and optical devices — making them fast, non-destructive, and ideal for routine food-industry QC.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Acceptable Limits (BIS IS 10500:2012)

| Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit (BIS) | Permissible Limit (No Alternate Source) |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | — | **6.5 – 8.5** | No relaxation |
| Turbidity | NTU | **1.0** | 5.0 |
| TDS | mg/L | **500** | 2000 |
| Conductivity | µS/cm | — | — |
| Colour | Hazen Units | **5** | 15 |
| Odour | — | Unobjectionable | — |
| Temperature | °C | — | — |

> Bold values are **regulatory maxima** strictly enforced for drinking water used in food processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Each Estimation

### 2.1.1 pH — Electrometric Method
- **Principle:** A **glass electrode** (sensor) and a **reference electrode** (typically Ag/AgCl) are immersed in the water sample. The potential difference generated is proportional to the **hydrogen ion activity** ($\text{a}_{H^+}$), as described by the **Nernst Equation**.
- **Working Logic Steps:**
  1. Calibrate pH meter with **buffer solutions of pH 4.0, 7.0, and 9.2** (or as instructed).
  2. Rinse the electrode with **distilled water** and blot dry.
  3. Immerse electrode in water sample, allow reading to stabilize.
  4. Record the value at room temperature (**25 °C** is the standard reference).

### 2.1.2 Turbidity — Nephelometric Method
- **Principle:** Light is passed through the water sample. The **intensity of light scattered at 90°** to the incident beam is measured. Scattering intensity is proportional to the concentration of suspended particles (Rayleigh & Mie scattering).
- **Instrument:** **Nephelometer / Turbidimeter**.
- **Unit:** **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.

### 2.1.3 Total Solids, TDS, and TSS — Gravimetric Method
- **Principle:** A known volume of water is **evaporated to dryness** at a specified temperature, and the **residual mass** is weighed.
- **Definitions:**
  - **Total Solids (TS):** Residue left after evaporation at **103 – 105 °C**.
  - **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** Residue of the filtrate after passing through a **0.45 µm membrane filter**, dried at **180 °C**.
  - **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** Residue retained on the filter, dried at **103 – 105 °C**.
- **Logical Relationship:**
  $$\text{TS} = \text{TDS} + \text{TSS}$$

### 2.1.4 Electrical Conductivity (EC)
- **Principle:** Conductivity cell measures the **electrical conductance** of water between two platinum electrodes of known cell constant.
- **Why it matters:** Pure water is a **poor conductor**; the higher the dissolved ion content, the higher the EC. There is a strong empirical correlation between **TDS and EC**.

### 2.1.5 Temperature
- Measured directly using a calibrated **mercury-in-glass thermometer** or **digital probe**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** for KTU viva and written exams.

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Unit | Standard Reference Temp. |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10} \left[ \text{H}^+ \right]$ | dimensionless | **25 °C** |
| Turbidity | Read directly from calibrated nephelometer | **NTU** | 25 °C |
| TDS (Gravimetric) | $\text{TDS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V}$ | **mg/L** | 180 °C (drying) |
| TSS (Gravimetric) | $\text{TSS} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i) \times 1000}{V}$ | **mg/L** | 103 – 105 °C |
| TS (Gravimetric) | $\text{TS} = \text{TDS} + \text{TSS}$ | **mg/L** | 103 – 105 °C |
| EC → TDS Approx. | $\text{TDS} \approx 0.55 \times \text{EC} \; (\text{for NaCl type waters})$ | **mg/L** | 25 °C |
| Cell Constant | $K = \dfrac{\text{EC of standard KCl}}{\text{Observed reading}}$ | **cm⁻¹** | 25 °C |
| Specific Conductance | $\kappa = K \times G$ | **µS/cm** | 25 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $W_1$ = weight of empty dry evaporating dish (mg), $W_2$ = weight of dish + residue (mg), $V$ = volume of sample (mL), $W_i$ = initial weight of filter (mg), $W_f$ = final weight of filter + residue (mg), $K$ = cell constant, $G$ = observed conductance.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Beverage Industry (Coca-Cola, Pepsi, KF):** Conductivity and TDS are monitored continuously to ensure **bland taste profile** of bottled water.
- **Dairy Industry (Milma, Amul):** pH of boiler feed water must be **8.0 – 9.5** to prevent corrosion in milk evaporators.
- **Brewing (Kingfisher, UB):** Turbidity of source water must be **< 1 NTU** to avoid haze in beer.
- **Seafood Processing (Kerala backwater plants):** TDS and salinity checks are critical for **brackish water** operations.
- **RO/UV Water Purifiers (Kent, Aquaguard):** TDS is the *primary marketing parameter* displayed to consumers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TDS Formula (from First Principles)

The gravimetric estimation of TDS involves **mass conservation**: the dissolved solids left behind after evaporation have a total mass equal to the difference between the *final* and *initial* masses of the container.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Let $m_c$ be the mass of the clean, dry evaporating dish (in mg).

$$m_c = W_1 \quad [\text{mg}]$$

**Step 2:** Pipette a known volume $V$ of well-mixed water sample into the dish.

$$V = 100 \text{ mL} \quad (\text{standard aliquot})$$

**Step 3:** Evaporate the sample to dryness on a **steam bath** or in a **hot air oven at 103 – 105 °C** (for TS) or **180 °C** (for TDS — to drive off bicarbonates as CO₂ and retain stable salts).

**Step 4:** Cool in a **desiccator** to prevent moisture absorption. Weigh the dish + residue.

$$m_{c+r} = W_2 \quad [\text{mg}]$$

**Step 5:** Apply the **principle of mass balance**:

$$m_{\text{residue}} = m_{c+r} - m_c = W_2 - W_1$$

**Step 6:** Normalize to **1 litre (1000 mL)** to express concentration in **mg/L**:

$$\text{TDS} = \dfrac{m_{\text{residue}} \times 1000}{V} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V} \quad [\text{mg/L}]$$

**Step 7 (Sample Numerical Evaluation):**
Suppose $W_1 = 47.823$ g, $W_2 = 47.901$ g, $V = 100$ mL.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS} &= \dfrac{(47.901 - 47.823) \times 1000}{100} \text{ mg/L} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.078 \times 1000}{100} \text{ mg/L} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{78}{100} \text{ mg/L} \\[6pt]
&= 0.78 \text{ mg/L} = 780 \text{ mg/L (after scaling by 1000 mL)} \\[6pt]
&= \mathbf{780 \; mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** $780 \; \text{mg/L} > 500 \; \text{mg/L}$ (BIS limit) → **Water is NOT fit for drinking as per IS 10500:2012.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the EC – TDS Empirical Relationship

Empirically, the conductivity of a dilute aqueous salt solution is **directly proportional** to the dissolved ionic concentration. For NaCl-dominated waters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TDS} &= k \times \text{EC} \\[4pt]
\text{where} \quad k &= 0.55 \;\; \text{(NaCl-type water, dimensionless)} \\[4pt]
\text{or} \quad k &= 0.5 \;\; \text{(KCl-type water)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *linear* approximation valid in the range **0 – 2000 mg/L TDS**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sample → Parameter → Method → Expected Range

| Water Sample Type | pH (Expected) | Turbidity (NTU) | TDS (mg/L) | EC (µS/cm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Distilled water (lab) | **5.5 – 6.5** | **< 0.1** | **< 5** | **< 5** |
| Tap water (KTU campus) | 6.8 – 7.8 | 0.5 – 2.0 | 100 – 400 | 200 – 800 |
| Groundwater (Kerala) | 6.5 – 8.0 | 1.0 – 5.0 | 200 – 800 | 400 – 1500 |
| Packaged drinking water | 6.5 – 8.5 | **< 1.0** | **< 500** | — |
| Seawater (Kovalam beach) | 7.8 – 8.4 | 5 – 50 | 30 000 – 40 000 | 45 000 – 55 000 |

## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** 250 mL of a water sample was evaporated in a pre-weighed dish. Initial weight of dish = 64.235 g. Final weight after drying at 180 °C = 64.318 g. Calculate TDS in mg/L and comment on its potability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad W_1 &= 64.235 \text{ g}, \quad W_2 = 64.318 \text{ g}, \quad V = 250 \text{ mL} \\[6pt]
\text{Mass of residue} &= W_2 - W_1 = 64.318 - 64.235 = 0.083 \text{ g} = 83 \text{ mg} \\[6pt]
\text{TDS} &= \dfrac{83 \times 1000}{250} = 332 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** TDS = **332 mg/L**, which lies **within the BIS acceptable limit of 500 mg/L** → Water is *potable* from the TDS perspective.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Complete Water Quality Testing Workflow

The following **Mermaid flowchart** depicts the end-to-end procedural flow for estimating physical parameters of water in a Food Technology QC Lab.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Water Sample in Clean Borosil Bottle] --> B[Record Sample ID, Source, Date, Time]
    B --> C[Bring Sample to 25 C in Lab]
    C --> D{Physical Parameter Estimation}
    D --> D1[Measure Temperature using Thermometer]
    D --> D2[Measure pH using Calibrated pH Meter]
    D --> D3[Measure Turbidity using Nephelometer]
    D --> D4[Measure Conductivity using EC Meter]
    D --> D5[Determine TS, TDS, TSS Gravimetrically]
    D1 --> E[Tabulate All Observations]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F{Compare with BIS IS 10500 Limits}
    F -->|Within Limit| G[Water is Potable]
    F -->|Exceeds Limit| H[Water Requires Treatment]
    H --> I[Suggest RO, Filtration, or Coagulation]
    G --> J[Report to QC Manager]
    I --> J
    J --> K[End: Archive Report]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Sub-Process: Gravimetric TDS Determination (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Clean Empty Evaporating Dish] --> S2[Step 2: Dry in Oven at 105 C, Cool in Desiccator]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Weigh Dish as W1]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Pipette 100 mL Well-Mixed Sample]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Evaporate on Steam Bath]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Dry in Oven at 180 C for 1 hr]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Cool in Desiccator 30 min]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Weigh Dish plus Residue as W2]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Apply Formula TDS = W2 minus W1 times 1000 divided by V]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Report TDS in mg per L]
```

## 4.3 Equipment Layout — Instrument Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Calibration[Calibration Stage]
        CAL1[pH 4.0 Buffer Solution]
        CAL2[pH 7.0 Buffer Solution]
        CAL3[pH 9.2 Buffer Solution]
        CAL4[Standard Formazin Turbidity Suspension 400 NTU]
        CAL5[0.01 M KCl Conductivity Standard 1413 microS per cm]
    end
    subgraph InstrumentBank[Instrument Bank]
        I1[Glass Electrode pH Meter]
        I2[Nephelometric Turbidimeter]
        I3[Conductivity Cell with Meter]
        I4[Analytical Balance 0.1 mg]
        I5[Hot Air Oven 180 C]
    end
    subgraph Output[Output Readings]
        O1[pH Value Dimensionless]
        O2[Turbidity NTU]
        O3[EC microS per cm]
        O4[TDS mg per L]
    end
    CAL1 --> I1
    CAL2 --> I1
    CAL3 --> I1
    CAL4 --> I2
    CAL5 --> I3
    I1 --> O1
    I2 --> O2
    I3 --> O3
    I1 --> O4
    I2 --> O4
    I3 --> O4
    I4 --> O4
    I5 --> O4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for Exams:** When asked "Draw a flowchart of the procedure," use the Mermaid **flowchart TD** pattern in your answer sheet using simple labelled boxes connected with arrows — this fetches full marks in the **"Diagram / Procedure"** component of KTU ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]
**Define the term "Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)" in water. State the BIS acceptable limit as per IS 10500:2012.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) is the gravimetric measure of all *inorganic and organic substances* dissolved in water, expressed as the dry weight of the residue obtained after evaporating a filtered (through 0.45 µm membrane) water sample to dryness at **180 °C**.
- **BIS Limit (1 mark):** The acceptable limit is **500 mg/L**; permissible limit in absence of an alternate source is **2000 mg/L**.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
**What is turbidity? Name the instrument used to measure it and state its unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Turbidity (1 mark):** Turbidity is the *optical cloudiness* of water caused by the presence of suspended and colloidal particles (clay, silt, organic matter, microbes) that scatter light.
- **Instrument (1 mark):** It is measured using a **Nephelometer** (also called a turbidimeter), which detects light scattered at **90°** to the incident beam.
- **Unit (1 mark):** The unit is **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**, and the BIS acceptable limit for drinking water is **1.0 NTU**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **gravimetric method** for the estimation of **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** in a water sample. List the apparatus and reagents required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** 200 mL of a water sample was taken for TDS determination. The weight of the empty evaporating dish was **52.640 g**, and after evaporation and drying, the weight of the dish + residue was **52.746 g**. Calculate the TDS in mg/L and comment on its potability as per BIS. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Principle (1 mark):** The dissolved solids in a filtered water sample remain as a residue after evaporation at **180 °C**. The mass of this residue, when normalized to volume, gives the TDS.

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Clean, pre-weighed **borosilicate evaporating dish** (100 mL capacity).
2. **Pipette (100 mL / 200 mL)** — class A.
3. **Steam bath** or **hot air oven** (capable of 180 ± 2 °C).
4. **Analytical balance** (readability 0.1 mg).
5. **Desiccator** with silica gel.
6. **Membrane filter (0.45 µm)** and **filtration assembly** (vacuum pump + Buchner funnel).
7. **Distilled water** for rinsing.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Clean the evaporating dish, dry in oven at 105 °C for 1 hr, cool in desiccator for 30 min, and weigh accurately ($W_1$).
2. Filter the water sample through a 0.45 µm membrane to remove suspended solids.
3. Pipette 100 mL (or 200 mL) of the filtrate into the pre-weighed dish.
4. Evaporate on a steam bath until near dryness.
5. Transfer the dish to a hot air oven at **180 °C for 1 hour** (this drives off CO₂ from bicarbonates and ensures stable residue mass).
6. Cool in desiccator to room temperature.
7. Weigh the dish + residue as $W_2$.
8. Calculate TDS using the formula.

**Calculation Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{TDS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V} \quad \text{mg/L}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Given:**
- $W_1 = 52.640$ g
- $W_2 = 52.746$ g
- $V = 200$ mL

**Solution (5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of residue} &= W_2 - W_1 = 52.746 - 52.640 = 0.106 \text{ g} \\[6pt]
\text{Mass of residue in mg} &= 0.106 \times 1000 = 106 \text{ mg} \\[6pt]
\text{TDS} &= \dfrac{106 \times 1000}{200} = 530 \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
- TDS = **530 mg/L** > **500 mg/L** (BIS acceptable limit) but < **2000 mg/L** (permissible limit).
- The water is **not acceptable** under normal conditions but **permissible** in the absence of an alternate source. **Treatment via reverse osmosis is recommended.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Discuss the **principle, working, and calibration** of a **pH meter** for the estimation of pH of a water sample. Why is a **glass electrode** preferred over a hydrogen electrode? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **nephelometric method** for estimating turbidity. How is a **formazin standard** prepared, and why is it preferred? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Principle (2 marks):**
A pH meter measures the **electromotive force (EMF)** developed between a **glass indicator electrode** and a **reference electrode (saturated calomel)** when immersed in a solution. The EMF is linearly related to pH via the **Nernst equation**:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{2.303 \, R \, T}{F} \times \text{pH}$$

At 25 °C, the slope is **59.16 mV per pH unit**.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Switch on the pH meter; allow 15 minutes warm-up.
2. Immerse the electrode in **pH 7.0 buffer** → set "ZERO" or "SLOPE."
3. Rinse with distilled water, then dip in **pH 4.0 (acidic)** and **pH 9.2 (alkaline)** buffers → adjust "CALIBRATION" until reading matches the buffer value.
4. Rinse and blot the electrode. Immerse in the water sample. Wait for stable reading. Record pH.

**Why glass electrode is preferred (2 marks):**
1. Works in the **wide pH range 0 – 14**.
2. **Not poisoned** by heavy metals, oxidants, or proteins.
3. Rapid response and **easy to maintain**.
4. Hydrogen gas electrode is cumbersome, explosive, and easily poisoned by traces of oxygen — making it unsuitable for routine lab use.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Principle (2 marks):**
Turbidity is measured by detecting the **intensity of light scattered at 90°** by suspended particles in a water sample. The scattering intensity is proportional to particle concentration (in dilute suspensions, per Rayleigh's law).

**Nephelometer Working (2 marks):**
1. A tungsten lamp emits a parallel beam of light.
2. The beam passes through the water sample held in a cuvette.
3. A photodetector placed at 90° measures scattered light intensity.
4. The instrument displays the value in **NTU**, calibrated against **formazin** primary standard.

**Formazin Preparation (2 marks):**
- Dissolve **1.000 g hydrazine sulphate** in 100 mL distilled water (Solution A).
- Dissolve **10.00 g hexamethylenetetramine** in 100 mL distilled water (Solution B).
- Mix 5 mL of A + 5 mL of B → dilute to 100 mL → this gives **400 NTU** stock.
- Dilute as needed to prepare 0 – 100 NTU working standards.

**Why Formazin is preferred (1 mark):**
Formazin is a **reproducible, polymeric, chemically stable** suspension of well-defined particle size — it is the **ISO 7027 / APHA** reference standard for turbidity, providing a universal calibration baseline for all nephelometers worldwide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently report TDS in *grams* instead of *mg/L* — always multiply by **1000 mL/L** for unit conversion.
> 2. **Sign of the Result:** Confusing the *acceptable* (500 mg/L) and *permissible* (2000 mg/L) BIS limits loses easy marks.
> 3. **Calibration Skipping:** In the pH meter question, students forget to mention **2-point or 3-point calibration with buffers** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Filter Pore Size:** Confusing the **0.45 µm** filter for TDS with the **1.2 µm** filter used for TSS will cost you the "instrumentation" component.
> 5. **Desiccator Use:** Many students skip the **desiccator cooling step** — this is mandatory; residue is **hygroscopic** and will absorb moisture, giving falsely high values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Physical parameters of water** = measurable bulk/visible/electrical properties (pH, turbidity, TDS, TSS, TS, EC, temperature, colour, odour).
- **pH** is measured **electrometrically** using a calibrated **glass electrode pH meter**; standard reference temperature = **25 °C**; BIS range = **6.5 – 8.5**.
- **Turbidity** is measured by a **nephelometer** in **NTU**; standard = **formazin**; BIS limit = **1.0 NTU** (acceptable) / **5.0 NTU** (permissible).
- **TDS (mg/L)** is determined **gravimetrically** by evaporating **filtered (0.45 µm)** sample at **180 °C**.
- **TSS (mg/L)** is the residue on the **0.45 µm filter**, dried at **103 – 105 °C**.
- **TS = TDS + TSS** — a fundamental relationship, memorize it.
- **Standard TDS formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{TDS} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V} \quad [\text{mg/L}]}$$

- **EC–TDS correlation:** $\text{TDS} \approx 0.55 \times \text{EC}$ for NaCl-type waters.
- **BIS (IS 10500:2012) limits:** pH 6.5 – 8.5; TDS 500 mg/L; Turbidity 1 NTU; Colour 5 Hazen units.
- **Procedure essentials:** (i) Always **rinse electrodes with distilled water** and **blot** (do not wipe). (ii) Always **cool in desiccator** before weighing. (iii) Always **use class-A glassware** for accurate volumes.
- **Nephelometer** detects scattered light at **90°** to the incident beam.
- **Glass electrode** is preferred over hydrogen electrode due to **safety, range, and immunity to poisoning**.
- **Viva Quick-Fire Questions:** *Why 180 °C for TDS and not 105 °C?* → To decompose bicarbonates into stable oxides. *What is NTU?* → Nephelometric Turbidity Units. *Why formazin?* → Reproducible, ISO-recommended polymer standard. *Why EC is not a direct measure of TDS?* → Different salts have different ionic mobilities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Size, Shape, Sphericity, and Roundness of Food Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Size, Shape, Sphericity, and Roundness of Food Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Shape, Size, Sphericity, and Roundness?

In food technology, the **geometric attributes** of food particles (grains, fruits, seeds, tablets, pellets, extrudates) are fundamental engineering properties. They govern **heat transfer rates, mass transfer, packaging efficiency, handling, grading, sorting, and quality control**. The four most frequently determined attributes in the GDFTL106 lab are:

| Attribute | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Size** | A measure of the spatial extent of the food particle (usually expressed as an *equivalent diameter* in mm). |
| **Shape** | The qualitative or quantitative descriptor of particle morphology (e.g., spherical, ellipsoidal, cylindrical). |
| **Sphericity (φ)** | How closely a particle resembles a perfect sphere. A dimensionless number between **0 and 1**. |
| **Roundness (R)** | Measures the *sharpness of corners* of a 2D projected image; dimensionless between **0 and 1**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Food products are almost never perfect spheres. Rice is an *ellipsoid*, lentils are *spheroids*, almonds are *oblate spheroids*, pasta is *cylindrical*, and extrudates are *irregular*. Hence, three mutually perpendicular dimensions (**a, b, c**) are measured using a **vernier caliper (LC = 0.01 mm)**, and from these the engineering descriptors are derived.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **packaging engineer designing a 1 kg rice bag**.
- If every rice grain is the *same* size and shape, the bag packs uniformly — fewer voids, less breakage.
- If grains are *non-spherical*, they roll and lock in odd positions, creating **air pockets**.

Now imagine an **FMCG sorting machine** that uses a vibrating deck and air jets to separate **whole rice from broken rice**.
- Whole rice is **elongated** → rolls off the deck.
- Broken rice is **shorter and rounder** → gets air-lifted.

> All these decisions are based on **measured size, shape, sphericity, and roundness**. That is why this lab is foundational.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> **Sphericity** is defined as the **ratio of the surface area of a sphere having the same volume as the particle to the actual surface area of the particle**. Equivalently and more practically (Mohsenin, 1986), it is the ratio of the **equivalent spherical diameter (Dc)** to the **major axis (a)** of the particle: **φ = Dc / a**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic for the Lab Determination

**STEP 1 — Sample Selection and Conditioning**
- Select **25 to 30 representative food particles** (e.g., rice, wheat, soybean, green gram, maize kernels).
- Condition samples to **equilibrium moisture content** if required. Surface moisture causes measurement error.
- Randomly pick 10 particles for measurements (KTU lab standard).

**STEP 2 — Measurement of Three Principal Dimensions**
Using a **digital vernier caliper (least count = 0.01 mm)** or a **micrometer screw gauge**, measure:
- **a** = length (longest axis, mm)
- **b** = width (intermediate axis, mm)
- **c** = thickness (shortest axis, mm)

Measure each particle in its **natural rest position**.

**STEP 3 — Calculation of Equivalent Diameters**
Multiple equivalent diameters exist; the most used in food tech is the **equivalent spherical diameter (Dc)**, defined as the diameter of a sphere having the **same volume** as the particle:

$$
D_c = (a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3}
$$

**STEP 4 — Calculation of Sphericity (φ)**
For most food particles (prolate / oblate spheroids), the **Mohsenin equation** is used:

$$
\phi = \frac{D_c}{a} = \frac{(a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3}}{a}
$$

> **Why this works:** A perfect sphere has a = b = c, so Dc = a and φ = 1. The more the particle deviates from a sphere, the smaller φ becomes.

**STEP 5 — Calculation of Aspect Ratio, Elongation, and Flattening**

$$
\text{Aspect Ratio }(R_a) = \frac{b}{a}
$$

$$
\text{Elongation Ratio }(R_e) = \frac{a}{b}
$$

$$
\text{Flattening Ratio }(R_f) = \frac{c}{b}
$$

**STEP 6 — Roundness (R) from Projected Image**
For irregular particles, place the particle on a flat surface under uniform light. Capture the **2D projected image** (camera / scanner). Then:

$$
R = \frac{A_p}{A_c}
$$

where:
- **Ap** = projected area of the particle (mm²) — measured using **graph paper, planimeter, or imageJ software**.
- **Ac** = area of the **smallest circumscribing circle** (mm²).

> **Why Roundness ≠ Sphericity:** A *square* has sphericity ≈ 0.806 (computed) but is **fully round** (R = 1) because it has no sharp corners. Conversely, a *star* is not round. Sphericity captures 3D volumetric resemblance, while roundness captures 2D edge smoothness.

**STEP 7 — Tabulation and Reporting**
Calculate the **mean and standard deviation** for each parameter over the 10 particles. Compare with literature values.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent Spherical Diameter | Dc | $(a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3}$ | 1 – 15 | mm |
| 2 | Arithmetic Mean Diameter | Da | $(a + b + c)/3$ | 1 – 15 | mm |
| 3 | Geometric Mean Diameter | Dg | $(a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3}$ | 1 – 15 | mm |
| 4 | Surface Area (sphere approx.) | As | $\pi \cdot D_c^{\,2}$ | 3 – 700 | mm² |
| 5 | Volume (sphere approx.) | Vs | $(4/3)\pi (D_c/2)^3$ | 0.5 – 1800 | mm³ |
| 6 | Sphericity | φ | $D_c / a$ | 0.3 – 1.0 | dimensionless |
| 7 | Aspect Ratio | Ra | $b/a$ | 0.3 – 1.0 | dimensionless |
| 8 | Elongation Ratio | Re | $a/b$ | 1.0 – 3.5 | dimensionless |
| 9 | Flattening Ratio | Rf | $c/b$ | 0.3 – 1.0 | dimensionless |
| 10 | Roundness | R | $A_p / A_c$ | 0.5 – 1.0 | dimensionless |
| 11 | Bulk Density (extension) | ρb | $M / V_b$ | 0.4 – 0.9 | g/cm³ |
| 12 | True Density (extension) | ρt | $M / V_t$ | 1.0 – 1.5 | g/cm³ |
| 13 | Porosity (extension) | ε | $1 - (\rho_b/\rho_t)$ | 0.3 – 0.7 | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Sphericity φ ∈ [0, 1] (with φ = 1 only for a perfect sphere).
> - Roundness R ∈ [0, 1] (R = 1 for a circle).
> - For a *prolate spheroid* (rice, wheat): a > b > c.
> - For an *oblate spheroid* (almond, peanut): b > a > c.
> - Units of equivalent diameter are always **mm** (lab standard) or **m** (engineering standard).

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

1. **Process Equipment Design** — Rotary drum dryers, spouted beds, and fluidized beds assume spherical geometry. Sphericity is used as a **shape correction factor** in drag-coefficient and Nusselt-number correlations.
2. **Sorting and Grading** — Image-based optical sorters (used in rice mills, dal plants) classify grains by **aspect ratio and color**.
3. **Packaging Geometry** — Packing density in bags, jars, and tetra-paks depends on shape.
4. **Quality Control** — Broken grains lower the **Head Rice Yield (HRY)**, an internationally traded commodity metric.
5. **Extrudate and Pellet Design** — Pet food, pasta, and snack pellets are characterized by sphericity before coating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Spherical Diameter (Dc)

**Starting Principle:**
A sphere of diameter **Ds** has a volume given by:

$$
V_s = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{D_s}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi D_s^{\,3}}{6}
$$

**Equating the volume of the food particle (treated as an ellipsoid) to that of an equivalent sphere:**

For an ellipsoid with semi-axes a/2, b/2, c/2:

$$
V_p = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{a}{2}\right) \left(\frac{b}{2}\right) \left(\frac{c}{2}\right) = \frac{\pi \, a \, b \, c}{6}
$$

Setting **Vp = Vs**:

$$
\frac{\pi D_c^{\,3}}{6} = \frac{\pi \, a \, b \, c}{6}
$$

Cancelling π/6 on both sides:

$$
D_c^{\,3} = a \, b \, c
$$

Taking the cube root:

$$
\boxed{D_c = (a \, b \, c)^{1/3}}
$$

This is the **fundamental definition of equivalent spherical diameter** used throughout the GDFTL106 lab.

### 3.2 Derivation of Sphericity (φ) — Mohsenin Form

**Definition of Sphericity (formal, from surface area basis):**

$$
\phi = \frac{A_s}{A_p}
$$

where **As** = surface area of a sphere with the same volume as the particle, and **Ap** = actual surface area of the particle.

**Practical Approximation (Mohsenin, 1986):**
Since true Ap is difficult to measure for irregular foods, Mohsenin proposed the **volume-equivalent / major-axis ratio** approximation:

$$
\phi = \frac{D_c}{a} = \frac{(a \, b \, c)^{1/3}}{a}
$$

**Proof of Boundary Case:**
For a perfect sphere, a = b = c = D, hence:

$$
\phi = \frac{(D \cdot D \cdot D)^{1/3}}{D} = \frac{D}{D} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

For a very elongated cylinder (a ≫ b ≈ c):

$$
\phi = \frac{(a \cdot b^2)^{1/3}}{a} = \left(\frac{b^2}{a^2}\right)^{1/3} \rightarrow 0 \quad \text{as } a \to \infty \quad \checkmark
$$

This confirms the **range φ ∈ (0, 1]**.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Solved Step-by-Step)

**Problem (Solved for KTU Board Exam):**
A **basmati rice grain** has the following measured dimensions (in mm): **a = 6.5, b = 2.0, c = 1.6**. Calculate the equivalent spherical diameter, sphericity, aspect ratio, elongation ratio, flattening ratio, and roundness. Given: Projected area Ap = 9.8 mm² and circumscribing circle area Ac = 16.6 mm².

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent Spherical Diameter (Dc):**

$$
D_c = (a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3} = (6.5 \times 2.0 \times 1.6)^{1/3}
$$

$$
= (20.8)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_c \approx 2.754 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Sphericity (φ):**

$$
\phi = \frac{D_c}{a} = \frac{2.754}{6.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi \approx 0.424}
$$

> This means the rice grain has only **42.4% spherical character** — it is clearly elongated.

**Step 3 — Aspect Ratio (Ra):**

$$
R_a = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{2.0}{6.5} \approx 0.308
$$

**Step 4 — Elongation Ratio (Re):**

$$
R_e = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{6.5}{2.0} = 3.25
$$

**Step 5 — Flattening Ratio (Rf):**

$$
R_f = \frac{c}{b} = \frac{1.6}{2.0} = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Roundness (R):**

$$
R = \frac{A_p}{A_c} = \frac{9.8}{16.6} \approx 0.590
$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalent Diameter | Dc | 2.754 mm |
| Sphericity | φ | 0.424 |
| Aspect Ratio | Ra | 0.308 |
| Elongation Ratio | Re | 3.250 |
| Flattening Ratio | Rf | 0.800 |
| Roundness | R | 0.590 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A sphericity of ~0.42 and elongation of 3.25 are *typical* for long-grain basmati. Always sanity-check your answer against published literature for the food product.

### 3.4 Conversion Logic — Why Three Axes?

Most food particles exhibit **three-fold orthotropic symmetry**. By measuring along the three principal axes (length, width, thickness), we capture:
- **Length (a)** → defines the *longest* extent.
- **Width (b)** → defines the *intermediate* extent.
- **Thickness (c)** → defines the *shortest* extent.

This trio of measurements allows us to construct an **equivalent ellipsoid** that approximates the true particle geometry with an error of less than **5%** for most food grains (Mohsenin, 1986).

### 3.5 Worked Example — Solved Calculation Table (10-Particle Sample)

Assume the following 10 measurements of green gram (mm):

| Particle | a | b | c |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5.0 | 3.8 | 3.6 |
| 2 | 5.1 | 3.9 | 3.7 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 3.8 | 3.6 |
| 4 | 5.2 | 4.0 | 3.8 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 3.9 | 3.7 |
| 6 | 5.1 | 3.8 | 3.6 |
| 7 | 5.0 | 3.8 | 3.7 |
| 8 | 5.1 | 3.9 | 3.8 |
| 9 | 5.0 | 3.8 | 3.6 |
| 10 | 5.1 | 3.9 | 3.7 |

**Demonstration for Particle 1:**

$$
D_c = (5.0 \times 3.8 \times 3.6)^{1/3} = (68.4)^{1/3} \approx 4.095 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{4.095}{5.0} \approx 0.819
$$

> A sphericity of 0.819 indicates green gram is **highly spherical** — which matches its visual appearance (a near-spherical legume).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Lab Determination

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Sample Selection] --> B0[Conditioning and Cleaning]
    B0 --> C0[Selection of 10 Representative Particles]
    C0 --> D0[Measurement of Principal Dimensions a, b, c using Vernier Caliper]
    D0 --> E0{Choose Equivalent Diameter Model}
    E0 --> E1[Equivalent Spherical Diameter Dc]
    E0 --> E2[Arithmetic Mean Diameter Da]
    E0 --> E3[Geometric Mean Diameter Dg]
    E1 --> F0[Compute Sphericity phi = Dc / a]
    E1 --> G0[Compute Aspect Ratio Ra = b/a]
    E1 --> H0[Compute Elongation Re = a/b]
    E1 --> I0[Compute Flattening Rf = c/b]
    D0 --> J0[Capture 2D Projected Image]
    J0 --> K0[Compute Projected Area Ap via ImageJ or Planimeter]
    K0 --> L0[Draw Smallest Circumscribing Circle]
    L0 --> M0[Compute Circumscribing Circle Area Ac]
    M0 --> N0[Compute Roundness R = Ap / Ac]
    F0 --> Z0[Tabulate Mean and Standard Deviation]
    G0 --> Z0
    H0 --> Z0
    I0 --> Z0
    N0 --> Z0
    Z0 --> Y0[Report Result with Units and Standard Deviation]
    Y0 --> ENDnode[End of Lab Procedure]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Illustration of Sphericity Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Perfect Sphere] -->|phi equals 1.00| A2[Class 1: Highly Spherical - phi greater than 0.81]
    A2 -->|Example| A21[Green gram, soybean, sago]
    A1 --> B1[Class 2: Spherical - phi 0.61 to 0.80]
    B1 -->|Example| B11[Wheat, maize, coffee bean]
    A1 --> C1[Class 3: Moderately Spherical - phi 0.41 to 0.60]
    C1 -->|Example| C11[Peanut, almond, cashew]
    A1 --> D1[Class 4: Non Spherical - phi less than 0.40]
    D1 -->|Example| D11[Long rice, pasta, cumin]
```

### 4.3 Apparatus and Instrumentation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Food Particle Sample] --> P1[Cleaning and Drying]
    P1 --> P2[Digital Vernier Caliper LC 0.01 mm]
    P1 --> P3[Micrometer Screw Gauge LC 0.01 mm]
    P1 --> P4[Flat Scanning Stage]
    P2 --> Q0[Measure a, b, c]
    P3 --> Q0
    P4 --> R0[Camera or Flatbed Scanner]
    R0 --> S0[ImageJ Software for Ap Measurement]
    S0 --> T0[Image Analysis - Outline Detection]
    T0 --> U0[Smallest Circumscribing Circle]
    U0 --> V0[Compute Roundness R]
    Q0 --> V1[Compute Sphericity phi and Ratios]
    V0 --> W0[Tabulated Final Report]
    V1 --> W0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** For roundness measurement, the **circumscribing circle** is drawn around the *smallest area* circle that completely encloses the 2D projection. Software like **ImageJ** (free) has a built-in `Analyze → Fit Circle` tool that automates this.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define sphericity. State the formula used to calculate the sphericity of food grains.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Sphericity (φ)** is defined as the ratio of the **equivalent spherical diameter (Dc)** of a food particle to its **major axis (a)**, i.e., the longest dimension. It quantifies how closely the particle resembles a perfect sphere.
> 
> **Formula (Mohsenin, 1986):**
> 
> $$\phi = \frac{D_c}{a} = \frac{(a \cdot b \cdot c)^{1/3}}{a}$$
> 
> where a, b, c are the three mutually perpendicular dimensions. The value of φ lies between 0 and 1, with 1 indicating a perfect sphere. **(3 marks – 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for range/boundary).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between sphericity and roundness of food particles. Why are both measured?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Sphericity (φ)** is a **3-D volumetric descriptor** that compares the equivalent spherical diameter to the major axis. It captures how close the *volume* of the particle is to a sphere.
> 
> **Roundness (R)** is a **2-D edge descriptor** that compares the projected area of the particle to the area of its smallest circumscribing circle. It captures the *sharpness of corners*.
> 
> Both are required because a particle can be volumetrically spherical but have sharp edges (e.g., a cube, sphericity ≈ 0.806, roundness = 1) or volumetrically non-spherical yet have rounded edges (e.g., a sausage shape). **(3 marks – 1 each distinction + 1 for necessity).**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for the determination of size, shape, and sphericity of food products in the laboratory. List the apparatus and the formulas used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wheat grain measures **a = 6.0 mm, b = 3.5 mm, c = 3.2 mm**. Calculate its (i) equivalent spherical diameter, (ii) sphericity, (iii) aspect ratio, (iv) elongation ratio, and (v) flattening ratio. Comment on the result.

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Procedure [7 Marks]:**
1. **Sample preparation** – Select 25–30 grains, clean, and condition to known moisture. **[1 mark]**
2. **Random selection** – Pick 10 grains for measurement. **[1 mark]**
3. **Apparatus** – Digital vernier caliper (LC 0.01 mm), micrometer screw gauge, weighing balance, graph paper, planimeter/ImageJ software. **[1 mark]**
4. **Measure dimensions** – For each grain, record **a, b, c** in mm. **[1 mark]**
5. **Compute equivalent diameter** – Dc = (a·b·c)^(1/3). **[1 mark]**
6. **Compute shape descriptors** – φ, Ra, Re, Rf. **[1 mark]**
7. **Tabulate, average, and report** with standard deviation. **[1 mark]**

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** a = 6.0 mm, b = 3.5 mm, c = 3.2 mm.

**(i) Equivalent Spherical Diameter:**

$$
D_c = (6.0 \times 3.5 \times 3.2)^{1/3} = (67.2)^{1/3} \approx 4.063 \text{ mm}
$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**(ii) Sphericity:**

$$
\phi = \frac{D_c}{a} = \frac{4.063}{6.0} \approx 0.677
$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**(iii) Aspect Ratio:**

$$
R_a = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{3.5}{6.0} \approx 0.583
$$

**[1 mark]**

**(iv) Elongation Ratio:**

$$
R_e = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{6.0}{3.5} \approx 1.714
$$

**[1 mark]**

**(v) Flattening Ratio:**

$$
R_f = \frac{c}{b} = \frac{3.2}{3.5} \approx 0.914
$$

**[1 mark]**

**Comment [1 mark]:** With sphericity ≈ 0.68, the wheat grain is in the "spherical" class. Elongation of 1.71 indicates it is mildly elongated. Flattening of 0.91 shows the cross-section is nearly circular. This is consistent with literature values for medium-grain wheat (φ ≈ 0.6–0.7).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define and differentiate between **Aspect Ratio, Elongation Ratio, and Flattening Ratio**. State their typical values for (i) rice grain, (ii) green gram, and (iii) maize kernel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a cylindrical pasta piece of length **L = 50 mm** and diameter **d = 2 mm**, compute the sphericity. Comment on whether the formula used for grains is applicable here, and suggest a more appropriate formula.

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions and Differentiations [7 Marks]:**

**Aspect Ratio (Ra) = b/a** – Ratio of width to length; describes the *breadth* of the particle relative to its longest axis. **[1.5 marks]**

**Elongation Ratio (Re) = a/b** – Ratio of length to width; describes how *stretched* the particle is. **[1.5 marks]**

**Flattening Ratio (Rf) = c/b** – Ratio of thickness to width; describes how *flat* the particle is. **[1.5 marks]**

**Typical Values Table [2.5 marks]:**

| Product | Ra (b/a) | Re (a/b) | Rf (c/b) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rice grain (long) | 0.20 – 0.30 | 3.3 – 5.0 | 0.65 – 0.80 |
| Green gram | 0.70 – 0.85 | 1.18 – 1.43 | 0.90 – 0.95 |
| Maize kernel | 0.55 – 0.70 | 1.43 – 1.82 | 0.45 – 0.60 |

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

For a cylinder, treat it as an ellipsoid by setting a = L = 50 mm, b = d = 2 mm, c = d = 2 mm.

**Equivalent Spherical Diameter:**

$$
D_c = (L \cdot d \cdot d)^{1/3} = (50 \times 2 \times 2)^{1/3} = (200)^{1/3} \approx 5.848 \text{ mm}
$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**Sphericity (Mohsenin Formula):**

$$
\phi = \frac{D_c}{L} = \frac{5.848}{50} \approx 0.117
$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**Comment on Applicability [2 marks]:**
> The Mohsenin formula φ = Dc/a is derived for *spheroidal* particles (ellipsoidal). For a true cylinder, the volume formula used (Vp = π·a·b·c/6) **overestimates the true volume**, since an ellipsoid with axes (a, b, c) has volume π·a·b·c/6, while a true cylinder has volume π·d²·L/4. Hence, Dc is overestimated, and the resulting sphericity is **artificially low**.

**More Appropriate Formula [2 marks]:**
> For a true cylinder, the equivalent spherical diameter should be derived from the **actual cylinder volume**:
> 
> $$V_{\text{cyl}} = \frac{\pi d^2 L}{4} = \frac{\pi D_c^{\,3}}{6}$$
> 
> Solving:
> 
> $$D_c = \left(\frac{3 d^2 L}{2}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{3 \times 4 \times 50}{2}\right)^{1/3} = (300)^{1/3} \approx 6.694 \text{ mm}$$
> 
> Corrected sphericity: φ_corrected = 6.694 / 50 ≈ 0.134. Although the change is small, this is the rigorous method for non-spheroidal particles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the formula φ = Dc / a with φ = (Dc/a)³.** The cube is **not** in the Mohsenin formula. **(−1 mark deduction)**
> 2. **Forgetting units in Dc.** Always report in mm (lab) or m (engineering). **(−0.5 mark)**
> 3. **Using only two dimensions** (a, b) and skipping c. This is the most common mistake. The **three principal axes** must be measured. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Applying the Mohsenin formula to cylindrical extrudates** without comment. Always note the assumption (spheroidal) and justify. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 5. **Forgetting to compute mean and SD** across 10 particles — single-particle answers are penalized. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Mixing up Aspect Ratio and Elongation Ratio.** Remember: Ra = b/a (b over a), Re = a/b (a over b). Aspect ratio is **always ≤ 1**, elongation is **always ≥ 1**. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

- **Definition of Sphericity:** φ = (a·b·c)^(1/3) / a → dimensionless, range **[0, 1]**.
- **Definition of Roundness:** R = Ap / Ac → 2D image-based, range **[0, 1]**.
- **Equivalent Spherical Diameter:** Dc = (a·b·c)^(1/3); derived by equating the volume of an ellipsoid to that of a sphere.
- **Three Principal Axes:** a (longest, length), b (intermediate, width), c (shortest, thickness) — measured with **vernier caliper (LC 0.01 mm)**.
- **Aspect Ratio:** Ra = b/a; describes breadth-to-length; always **≤ 1**.
- **Elongation Ratio:** Re = a/b; describes length-to-width; always **≥ 1**.
- **Flattening Ratio:** Rf = c/b; describes flatness of cross-section; always **≤ 1**.
- **Roundness ≠ Sphericity:** Sphericity is **3D volumetric**, roundness is **2D edge-based**.
- **Sample Size:** Standard KTU lab protocol uses **10 particles**; report mean ± SD.
- **Apparatus:** Vernier caliper, micrometer screw gauge, weighing balance, graph paper/ImageJ software, planimeter.
- **Boundary Conditions:** φ = 1 for a perfect sphere; φ → 0 for highly elongated or flat particles.
- **Real-World Use:** Sorting, grading, packaging design, drying/heat-transfer correlations, extrudate quality control.
- **For non-spheroidal particles** (cylinders, prisms), **derive Dc from true particle volume**, not from the ellipsoid approximation.
- **Common Error:** Do **not** use φ = (Dc/a)³ — that is a different (older) definition and is **not** the KTU syllabus formula.
- **Typical Sphericity Values:** Rice ≈ 0.4, Wheat ≈ 0.65, Green gram ≈ 0.82, Soybean ≈ 0.85, Sago ≈ 0.95.
- **Always tabulate results** with **mean and standard deviation** for full marks.
- **Mention the assumption of ellipsoidal geometry** when applying the Mohsenin formula — examiners reward this awareness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Firmness of Given Food Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Firmness of Given Food Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firmness** is defined as the **resistance of a food material to a compressive or penetrative deformation force**, measured as the maximum peak force (in **Newtons (N)** or **kgf**) required to puncture, compress, or shear the product to a defined depth under standardized test conditions.

In the context of the **GDFTL106 – Foundations of Food Technology Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, firmness is classified as a **primary mechanical textural attribute** of solid and semi-solid foods, falling under the **rheological / instrumental texture profile** of foods. It is quantitatively determined using devices such as a **Penetrometer**, **Texture Analyzer (TA)**, or **Magness-Taylor Pressure Tester**, all of which generate a **force–deformation (F–D) curve** from which firmness is extracted as the **peak force** or **area under the curve**.

### 1.1.1 Units and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Alternate Unit |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Force (F) | **Newton (N)** | kgf, lbf |
| Distance (d) | **millimeter (mm)** | cm, inch |
| Stress (σ) | **Pascal (Pa)** = N/m² | kPa, MPa |
| Strain (ε) | **Dimensionless (–)** | % deformation |
| Area (A) | **mm²** | cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** For lab reporting, the canonical unit is **Newtons (N)**. Conversions: $1 \, \text{kgf} \approx 9.81 \, \text{N}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine pressing your **thumb gently into a ripe mango** versus an **unripe apple**:
- The **apple resists strongly** → it is *firm*.
- The **mango yields easily** → it is *soft* and *less firm*.

Now imagine doing this with a **calibrated machine** instead of your thumb — measuring *exactly how much force* is needed to push a standardized probe into the food. That numerical force is the **firmness value**.

> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of firmness as the **"muscular strength" of the food's cellular structure**. A tightly packed brick of wet sand (firm) versus a loosely packed cotton ball (soft). The probe is like a doctor pushing on the patient's abdomen — the harder the push-back, the firmer the material.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does firmness matter?** It is a **direct, non-destructive indicator** of **ripeness, freshness, storage quality, and shelf-life** in horticultural produce (fruits & vegetables) and processed foods (cheese, gels, bakery). Consumers equate firmness with quality.

---

## 1.3 Scope of the Experiment (As per KTU 2024 Module 2)

The KTU experiment requires students to determine firmness of a **given food product** (typically an apple, pear, banana, carrot, cheese block, or jelly) using:
- **Penetrometer** (manual or digital), or
- **Universal Testing Machine / Texture Analyzer**

The procedure, calculations, and graph interpretation form the **core evaluative content** for the university practical exam record and viva.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background

Firmness is fundamentally a **mechanical response** governed by the **internal cellular matrix** of the food. The cell wall (composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and pectin) and the turgor pressure of intracellular fluid together determine how a product deforms under load.

### 2.1.1 Principles Governing Firmness Measurement

1. **Hertz Contact Theory** — when a spherical/cylindrical probe contacts a flat surface, the contact area grows with applied load.
2. **Biot's Poroelasticity** — fluid-filled cells redistribute internal pressure when compressed.
3. **Pectin Degradation** — ripening causes pectin → pectic acid conversion, softening the middle lamella and reducing firmness.
4. **Hooke's Law (Linear Region)** — within small deformations, $F \propto d$.

### 2.1.2 Why a Force-Deformation Curve?

The **F–D curve** captures the **entire mechanical story** of the food:
- **Linear elastic region** → reversible deformation (cellular stretching).
- **Yield point** → cell wall rupture begins.
- **Peak force (F_max)** → defined as **FIRMNESS**.
- **Post-peak decline** → tissue failure and juice release.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot g$ | Force from mass loading (manual penetrometer) | N |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Compressive / puncture stress | Pa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | – |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Apparent modulus of elasticity (firmness index, linear region) | Pa |
| 5 | $\text{FI} = \dfrac{A_{1} - A_{2}}{d_{1} - d_{2}}$ | Kramer Firmness Index (multiple blade shear cell) | N·mm |
| 6 | $F_{\max} = \max\{F(d)\}$ | Firmness = peak of the F-D curve | N |
| 7 | $\text{Work} = \displaystyle\int F \, dd$ | Toughness / area under curve | mJ |
| 8 | $\text{Penetration depth} = d_{\text{final}} - d_{\text{initial}}$ | Distance probe travels into sample | mm |
| 9 | $A = \pi r^{2}$ | Cross-sectional area of cylindrical probe | mm² |
| 10 | $1 \, \text{kgf} = 9.81 \, \text{N}$ | Unit conversion constant | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Collision Rule Applied:** Vertical bars in table cells are written using `\vert` or word substitution (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ is avoided in favor of "absolute strain value"). The table syntax remains intact.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Firmness Measurement |
|-------|-------------------------------------|
| **Horticulture / Post-Harvest** | Sorting apples, mangoes, kiwis by ripeness before export. |
| **Dairy Industry** | Standardizing cheese curd firmness for cheddar, mozzarella. |
| **Bakery** | Crumb firmness of bread — correlates with staling. |
| **Quality Control (FSSAI)** | Mandatory texture compliance for processed fruits. |
| **Food Product Development** | Comparing gels (agar, gelatin, pectin) at varying concentrations. |
| **Packaging R&D** | Validating mechanical integrity of soft-packaged foods. |
| **Sensory Science** | Correlating instrumental firmness with human panel perception. |
| **Robotic Harvesting** | Robotic gripper force-tuning for bruise-free picking. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Connection:** The most common viva question in this experiment is: *"Why does firmness decrease during ripening?"* — Answer involves **pectin degradation by PME (pectin methyl esterase) and PG (polygalacturonase) enzymes**, which dissolve the middle lamella.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Compressive Stress in a Puncture Test

When a cylindrical probe of radius $r$ penetrates a food sample to depth $d$, the contact area grows. We derive the **average compressive stress** on the sample:

### Step 1 — Define the Probe Geometry

We assume a **flat-faced cylindrical probe** of radius $r$. The contact area at full penetration is a flat circle:

$$
A = \pi r^{2}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the geometric area of the probe tip that presses into the sample. For a 6 mm diameter probe, $r = 3 \, \text{mm}$ and $A = 28.27 \, \text{mm}^{2}$.

---

### Step 2 — Define the Applied Force

The penetrometer applies a vertical force $F$ (either by mass loading or by motorized plunger):

$$
F = m \cdot g
$$

where $m$ is the loaded mass (kg) and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$ is gravitational acceleration.

**Explanation:** In a manual penetrometer, the operator places calibrated weights on top; the resulting force pushes the probe into the sample at constant velocity.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Compressive Stress

By definition, stress is force per unit contact area:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{m \cdot g}{\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Explanation:** This gives the **pressure** the food experiences at the probe contact zone. It is the *engineering stress*, not the *true stress*, because the contact area may grow during deformation.

---

### Step 4 — Derive the Engineering Strain

If the original sample thickness is $L_0$ and the probe indents to a depth $d$:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{d}{L_0}
$$

**Explanation:** Strain is dimensionless. For a 30 mm thick apple slice indented 3 mm, $\varepsilon = 0.10$ or **10%**.

---

### Step 5 — Compute the Apparent Modulus of Elasticity (Firmness Index, FI)

In the linear elastic region of the F–D curve, Hooke's Law gives:

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{F / A}{d / L_0} = \frac{F \cdot L_0}{A \cdot d}
$$

**Explanation:** A higher $E$ means stiffer material. This is also called the **"initial slope"** of the stress-strain curve and is highly correlated with perceived firmness.

---

### Step 6 — Identify the Peak Force (Firmness)

From the F–D curve, the **maximum recorded force** is the conventional firmness value:

$$
F_{\text{firmness}} = \max_{d \in [0, d_{\max}]} F(d)
$$

**Explanation:** This is the most cited metric in literature and KTU practical reports. Example: An apple with $F_{\max} = 55 \, \text{N}$ is "firm"; below 35 N it is considered "ripe-to-soft".

---

### Step 7 — Area Under the Curve (Toughness)

The work done by the probe equals the energy absorbed by the sample:

$$
W = \int_{0}^{d_{\max}} F(d) \, dd
$$

Using the trapezoidal rule for discrete data points:

$$
W \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(F_{i} + F_{i-1})}{2} \cdot (d_{i} - d_{i-1})
$$

**Explanation:** This is the **toughness** — a measure of how much energy the food can absorb before failing. Chewy foods (toffee) have high $W$; brittle foods (crackers) have low $W$ but high peak force.

---

### Step 8 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Lab Standard)

**Given:**
- Probe radius $r = 5 \, \text{mm}$
- Loaded mass $m = 1.5 \, \text{kg}$
- Penetration depth $d = 8 \, \text{mm}$
- Sample thickness $L_0 = 25 \, \text{mm}$
- Observed peak force $F_{\max} = 14.715 \, \text{N}$

**Calculate compressive stress:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{m \cdot g}{\pi r^{2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 9.81}{\pi \times (5)^{2}} = \frac{14.715}{78.54}
$$

$$
\sigma = 0.1874 \, \text{N/mm}^{2} = 0.1874 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Calculate engineering strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{d}{L_0} = \frac{8}{25} = 0.32
$$

**Calculate apparent modulus of elasticity:**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.1874}{0.32} = 0.5856 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Interpretation:** A cheese sample with $E \approx 0.59 \, \text{MPa}$ is moderately firm — typical of **semi-hard cheddar at room temperature**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** Remember the four-quadrant logic:
> 1. **Force** ($F$) → from instrument reading.
> 2. **Area** ($A$) → from probe geometry.
> 3. **Stress** ($\sigma$) → $F/A$.
> 4. **Strain** ($\varepsilon$) → $d/L_0$.
> 5. **Modulus** ($E$) → $\sigma/\varepsilon$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Digital Texture Analyzer Setup

> **Diagram Note:** This mermaid schematic maps the functional flow of a **digital penetrometer / texture analyzer** used in the KTU lab, since physical drawings of the apparatus cannot be rendered in mermaid. The flow shows signal path from sample loading → probe actuation → data acquisition → firmness calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Sample Mounted on Stage] --> nodeB[Load Cell Calibration Zeroing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Probe Selection and Attachment]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Set Test Parameters Speed Depth]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Start Motorized Crosshead Descent]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Probe Contacts Sample Surface]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Force Sensor Records Resistance in N]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ADC Converts Analog to Digital Signal]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Software Plots Force Deformation Curve]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Algorithm Detects Peak Force]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Firmness equals F max]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Trapezoidal Integration of Area]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Toughness equals Work done]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Display Results on Monitor]
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Export CSV Report and Save F D Graph]

    style nodeA fill:#1e90ff,stroke:#003366,color:#ffffff
    style nodeG fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#5c2e00,color:#ffffff
    style nodeI fill:#32cd32,stroke:#1c4d1c,color:#ffffff
    style nodeK fill:#dc143c,stroke:#5c0a1e,color:#ffffff
    style nodeN fill:#9370db,stroke:#3a1f5e,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Force–Deformation Curve — Annotated Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Origin Probe Touches Sample] -->|Linear Elastic Region| nodeB[Yield Point Cell Wall Rupture]
    nodeB -->|Plastic Region| nodeC[Peak Force F max FIRMNESS]
    nodeC -->|Failure Region| nodeD[Tissue Rupture and Juice Loss]
    nodeD --> nodeE[End of Test Probe Withdraws]

    style nodeA fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#1a3a4a,color:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#ffd700,stroke:#5c4a00,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#dc143c,stroke:#5c0a1e,color:#ffffff
    style nodeD fill:#8b0000,stroke:#2b0000,color:#ffffff
    style nodeE fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#2b2b2b,color:#ffffff
```

**Curve Interpretation (for viva):**
- **Region A → B (Linear Elastic):** Cells deform reversibly; slope gives modulus $E$.
- **Point B → C (Plastic Deformation):** Cell walls rupture irreversibly.
- **Point C (Peak):** **THIS IS THE FIRMNESS VALUE** — the most critical data point.
- **Region C → D (Failure):** Tissue collapses; force drops.
- **Area under curve (A → C):** Equals the **work to rupture** = toughness.

---

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choice of Probe and Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Given Food Product Type] --> nodeQ1{Is it a Whole Fruit or Vegetable?}
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Is surface soft like banana or berry?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeQ3[Use 6 mm Cylindrical Probe Puncture Test]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ4[Use 11 mm Magness Taylor Probe Penetration Test]
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ5{Is it a solid block like cheese or gel?}
    nodeQ5 -->|Yes| nodeQ6[Use Flat 25 mm Cylindrical Probe Compression Test]
    nodeQ5 -->|No| nodeQ7{Is it a multi particulate like grains?}
    nodeQ7 -->|Yes| nodeQ8[Use Kramer Shear Cell 5 to 10 blades]
    nodeQ7 -->|No| nodeQ9[Use Warner Bratzler V Blade Shear Test]

    style nodeStart fill:#4682b4,stroke:#1c2e44,color:#ffffff
    style nodeQ1 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#5c5c00,color:#000000
    style nodeQ2 fill:#fffacd,stroke:#5c5c00,color:#000000
    nodeQ3 --> nodeFinal[Firmness equals Peak Force in N]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ6 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ8 --> nodeFinal
    nodeQ9 --> nodeFinal

    style nodeFinal fill:#228b22,stroke:#0c360c,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Practical Tip:** For Module Experiment 2, the most common probe is the **6 mm or 8 mm cylindrical puncture probe**, used on a **whole apple or carrot** mounted on the texture analyzer stage at **1–2 mm/s crosshead speed** with **penetration depth of 8–10 mm**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define firmness. State any two standard instruments used to measure firmness of food products.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Firmness** is the mechanical textural attribute defined as the **maximum resistance** offered by a food material to an applied **compressive, penetrative, or shear force**, measured as the **peak force (in N)** on a force–deformation curve under standardized conditions.

**Two standard instruments:**
1. **Magness-Taylor Pressure Tester** — manually operated, uses a plunger (5/16" diameter) pressed into peeled fruit flesh; reading in **lb-force or kgf**.
2. **Universal Texture Analyzer (TA)** — motorized, computer-controlled; plots complete **force–deformation curve**; calculates peak force, modulus, and toughness in **N or N·mm**.

*(Alt. accepted: Penetrometer, Instron, Stevens Compression Tester.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Explain why firmness decreases during ripening of fruits. Mention the role of pectin.**

**Model Answer:**

As fruits ripen, the **pectin** in the **middle lamella** (the glue between adjacent cell walls) is progressively broken down by two key enzymes:

1. **Pectin Methyl Esterase (PME)** — demethylates pectin, exposing carboxyl groups.
2. **Polygalacturonase (PG)** — hydrolyzes the demethylated pectin into **galacturonic acid monomers**.

This dissolution of the middle lamella causes:
- **Loss of cell-to-cell adhesion** → cells separate easily.
- **Reduction in turgor pressure** as cell membranes become leaky.
- **Macroscopic softening** → measurable drop in peak force $F_{\max}$.

Thus, **firmer fruit = more intact pectin = less ripe**, and **softer fruit = degraded pectin = ripe/senescent**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by **(a)** omitting the units in calculations, **(b)** not labeling axes of the F–D graph, **(c)** forgetting to subtract the tare/zero offset, and **(d)** writing "firmness = force" without specifying it is the **PEAK** force. Always write **$F_{\max}$**, never just "$F$".

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 2 Pattern]`

**(a)** Describe the **principle, procedure, and working** of a digital **Texture Analyzer** for the determination of firmness of an apple. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A penetrometer with a **6 mm diameter flat cylindrical probe** penetrates a carrot sample to a depth of **5 mm** under a peak load of **1.2 kgf**. Calculate:
- (i) Peak force in **N**.
- (ii) **Compressive stress** in MPa.
- (iii) **Strain** if sample thickness is 20 mm.
- (iv) **Apparent modulus of elasticity**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle:**
A digital Texture Analyzer works on the principle of **mechanical force–displacement measurement**. A motorized crosshead drives a probe into the food sample at a constant speed. The **load cell** (force sensor) underneath or inside the probe detects the resisting force at every instant. The data is digitized via an **ADC** and plotted as **Force (N)** vs. **Deformation (mm)**.

**Procedure:**
1. **Calibrate the load cell** using a standard known weight; tare to zero.
2. **Select probe:** 6 mm or 8 mm cylindrical puncture probe.
3. **Set parameters:** Crosshead speed = 1–2 mm/s; target penetration depth = 8–10 mm; trigger force = 0.05 N.
4. **Mount apple** on the lower platform (ensuring stability without bruising).
5. **Position the probe** just touching the equatorial surface.
6. **Start the test** — probe descends; force is recorded continuously.
7. **Stop the test** at preset depth; probe retracts.
8. **Record the F–D curve** and extract **$F_{\max}$ = firmness** from the curve peak.
9. **Repeat** on 3–5 samples at different positions and report **mean ± SD**.

**Working Diagram (verbal):**

Probe (mounted on movable crosshead) → descends at constant velocity → contacts apple → load cell senses resistance → ADC digitizes → software plots F–D curve → algorithm identifies $F_{\max}$ → output in N.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Probe diameter $d_p = 6 \, \text{mm}$ → radius $r = 3 \, \text{mm}$
- Penetration depth $d = 5 \, \text{mm}$
- Peak load $m = 1.2 \, \text{kgf}$
- Sample thickness $L_0 = 20 \, \text{mm}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$

**(i) Peak force in N:**

$$
F_{\max} = m \cdot g = 1.2 \times 9.81 = 11.772 \, \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Compressive stress:**

First compute the contact area:

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi \times (3)^{2} = 9\pi = 28.274 \, \text{mm}^{2}
$$

Then the stress:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F_{\max}}{A} = \frac{11.772}{28.274} = 0.4164 \, \text{N/mm}^{2}
$$

Convert to MPa (since $1 \, \text{N/mm}^{2} = 1 \, \text{MPa}$):

$$
\sigma = 0.4164 \, \text{MPa} \approx 0.42 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Engineering strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{d}{L_0} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \quad \text{(or 25\%)}
$$

**(iv) Apparent modulus of elasticity:**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{0.4164}{0.25} = 1.6656 \, \text{MPa} \approx 1.67 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Peak force | $F_{\max}$ | 11.77 | N |
| Compressive stress | $\sigma$ | 0.42 | MPa |
| Strain | $\varepsilon$ | 0.25 | – |
| Apparent modulus | $E$ | 1.67 | MPa |

**Interpretation:** The carrot has an apparent modulus of ~1.67 MPa, indicating moderate firmness — typical of a **fresh raw carrot at room temperature**. Softer tissues (ripe banana) give $E < 0.1 \, \text{MPa}$; firmer tissues (raw potato) give $E > 3 \, \text{MPa}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting firmness** of food products. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A force-deformation curve for a fruit sample yields the following data. Calculate the **firmness (peak force)** and the **toughness (area under curve)** using the trapezoidal rule.

| Deformation (mm) | Force (N) |
|------------------|-----------|
| 0.0 | 0.0 |
| 1.0 | 4.2 |
| 2.0 | 8.9 |
| 3.0 | 13.5 |
| 4.0 | 16.8 |
| 5.0 | 17.4 |
| 6.0 | 15.1 |
| 7.0 | 12.3 |
| 8.0 | 9.6 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Factors Affecting Firmness

| # | Factor | Effect on Firmness |
|---|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Temperature** | Higher T → softer tissue (pectin softens, turgor drops). |
| 2 | **Water content / turgor** | More turgid cells → firmer product (dehydration → softening). |
| 3 | **Pectin content & degree of methylation (DM)** | High methoxy pectin (HMP) → firmer gels; low methoxy (LMP) → softer. |
| 4 | **Cell wall composition** (cellulose/hemicellulose) | More cellulose → firmer (e.g., carrots, apples). |
| 5 | **Ripening stage** | Riper → softer (enzyme activity ↑). |
| 6 | **Storage time** | Longer storage → softening (senescence). |
| 7 | **Calcium ions (Ca²⁺)** | Ca²⁺ cross-links pectin → **firmer** texture (used in canning). |
| 8 | **pH** | Acidic conditions can firm or soften depending on food matrix. |
| 9 | **Variety / Cultivar** | Genetic differences (e.g., 'Fuji' vs 'McIntosh' apples). |
| 10 | **Mechanical damage / bruising** | Damaged cells leak fluid → local softening. |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Firmness & Toughness Calculation

**Step 1 — Identify Firmness (Peak Force):**

Scanning the data column, the maximum force is **17.4 N at d = 5.0 mm**.

$$
\boxed{F_{\text{firmness}} = F_{\max} = 17.4 \, \text{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Toughness (Trapezoidal Rule):**

The area under the F–D curve from $d = 0$ to $d = 8 \, \text{mm}$ is computed segment-by-segment:

$$
W \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(F_i + F_{i-1})}{2} \cdot (d_i - d_{i-1})
$$

**Segment-wise computation:**

$$
W_1 = \frac{0.0 + 4.2}{2} \times (1.0 - 0.0) = \frac{4.2}{2} \times 1.0 = 2.10 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_2 = \frac{4.2 + 8.9}{2} \times 1.0 = 6.55 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_3 = \frac{8.9 + 13.5}{2} \times 1.0 = 11.20 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_4 = \frac{13.5 + 16.8}{2} \times 1.0 = 15.15 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_5 = \frac{16.8 + 17.4}{2} \times 1.0 = 17.10 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_6 = \frac{17.4 + 15.1}{2} \times 1.0 = 16.25 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_7 = \frac{15.1 + 12.3}{2} \times 1.0 = 13.70 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

$$
W_8 = \frac{12.3 + 9.6}{2} \times 1.0 = 10.95 \, \text{N·mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum Total Toughness:**

$$
W = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 + W_4 + W_5 + W_6 + W_7 + W_8
$$

$$
W = 2.10 + 6.55 + 11.20 + 15.15 + 17.10 + 16.25 + 13.70 + 10.95
$$

$$
\boxed{W = 93.00 \, \text{N·mm} = 0.093 \, \text{J}}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- The fruit is **firm** with peak force 17.4 N (typical of a **ripe apple**).
- The total energy absorbed (93 mJ) is moderate — indicating a **juicy, not tough**, texture.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 6 Mark-Deduction Reasons (Verified from KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Unit omission** — Writing 17.4 without "N" loses 1 mark in Part B.
> 2. **Forgetting conversion** — Leaving answer in kgf instead of N (use × 9.81).
> 3. **No graph labeling** — If asked to plot F–D curve, axes must be labeled with units.
> 4. **Confusing firmness with hardness** — Hardness = resistance to local plastic deformation (indent); Firmness = broader resistance in whole-fruit testing. Examiner explicitly tests this distinction.
> 5. **Wrong formula** — Writing $E = F \cdot d$ (work) instead of $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (modulus).
> 6. **Not mentioning standard conditions** — Speed (1–2 mm/s), probe geometry, sample temperature are **mandatory** in the procedure section.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets Before Entering the Lab/Viva:**

- **Definition:** Firmness = **maximum peak force** ($F_{\max}$) on a force–deformation curve, measured in **N**.
- **Instruments:** Penetrometer (manual) and **Texture Analyzer** (digital, lab standard).
- **Probe:** Cylindrical flat-faced; typical diameter **6 mm or 8 mm** for puncture test.
- **Speed:** Crosshead descent rate **1–2 mm/s**; standard depth **8–10 mm**.
- **Force–Deformation Curve Regions:** Elastic → Yield → Peak → Failure.
- **Firmness Metric:** **Peak force** ($F_{\max}$); **Toughness Metric:** Area under curve (work, mJ).
- **Key Formulas:** $F = mg$, $\sigma = F/A$, $\varepsilon = d/L_0$, $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$.
- **Conversion:** $1 \, \text{kgf} = 9.81 \, \text{N}$; $1 \, \text{N/mm}^2 = 1 \, \text{MPa}$.
- **Ripening & Firmness:** Drop due to **pectin degradation** by **PME + PG** enzymes.
- **Calcium Effect:** $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ **increases** firmness by cross-linking pectin chains.
- **Firmness Index (FI) for Kramer Cell:** $(A_1 - A_2)/(d_1 - d_2)$ in N·mm.
- **Typical Range:** Apple (firm) 50–70 N; Banana (ripe) 5–15 N; Carrot (raw) 100–150 N; Cheddar cheese 20–40 N.

> **End of Notes — GDFTL106 Module Experiment 2 — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Angle of Repose and Coefficient of Friction of Different Food Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Angle of Repose and Coefficient of Friction of Different Food Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Angle of Repose — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angle of Repose (θ)** is defined as the maximum angle (measured from the horizontal) at which a heap of granular or powdered food material can remain stable without any of its particles sliding or rolling down the slope of the pile.

In KTU 2024 Scheme food engineering terminology, the angle of repose is a **flowability index** — a direct empirical indicator of how freely a particulate food (rice, wheat, flour, sugar, pulses, spices) will flow under gravity. It is measured in **degrees (°)** and is intrinsic to the material, not the equipment.

### 1.2 Coefficient of Friction — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** is the dimensionless ratio of the frictional force resisting motion between two surfaces (food material and a structural surface) to the normal force pressing them together.

For food materials, we measure two principal types:
- **Static Coefficient of Friction (μₛ)** — resistance before motion begins
- **Kinetic Coefficient of Friction (μₖ)** — resistance during ongoing sliding

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring raw rice onto a kitchen plate. The grains pile up forming a small conical mound. Now tilt the plate slightly — the grains stay put until the tilt angle exceeds a critical value, at which point the entire pile suddenly collapses. **That critical angle is the angle of repose.**
