Now imagine pushing a wooden block on a steel surface — it resists until you apply a minimum force. That minimum force divided by the block's weight gives you the **coefficient of friction**. For food grains on a galvanized iron sheet (a common chute material in rice mills), this same principle applies.

> [!TIP]
> **Why it matters in food industry:** These two parameters decide the design of **storage silos, hoppers, conveyor chutes, and inclined belt conveyors**. If a mill designer ignores the angle of repose, grain will either jam (too steep) or fail to discharge (too shallow).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Angle of Repose unit: **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)**
- Coefficient of Friction: **dimensionless**
- Typical values for food grains: **20° – 45°**
- Typical μₛ for grains on mild steel: **0.20 – 0.60**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background of Angle of Repose

When a granular food material is poured onto a flat surface, particles slide outward and settle at an angle θ where the **gravitational force component along the slope** equals the **internal frictional resistance** between particles.

**Mechanism of Heap Formation (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Initial drop:** Particles fall vertically under gravity and accumulate at the apex.
2. **Spreading phase:** As height increases, the slope angle grows, forcing outward rolling.
3. **Equilibrium phase:** A steady-state angle is reached when the downward component of gravitational pull is balanced by inter-particle static friction.
4. **Cessation:** Additional particles roll off the surface; the cone height stabilizes.

### 2.2 Theoretical Background of Coefficient of Friction

When a food sample rests on an inclined plane of structural material, two force components act on it:
- **Normal force (N)** acting perpendicular to the inclined surface
- **Gravitational force (W = mg)** acting vertically downward

The sample begins to slide when the **tangential component** of weight exceeds the **maximum static friction force**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit / Description |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Angle of Repose (Funnel Method) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{h}{r}$ | h = height of cone (m); r = base radius (m) |
| 2 | Angle of Repose (Cylinder Method) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{h}{r}$ | Same; r = cylinder radius |
| 3 | Angle of Repose (Tilting Box) | $\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{L}\right)$ | h = lift height; L = box length |
| 4 | Static Coefficient of Friction | $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$ | α = angle of slide (deg) |
| 5 | Kinetic Coefficient of Friction | $\mu_k = \tan\beta$ | β = angle at constant velocity |
| 6 | Normal Force on Incline | $N = mg\cos\alpha$ | Newtons (N) |
| 7 | Frictional Force | $F_f = \mu N$ | Newtons (N) |
| 8 | Critical Slide Condition | $\tan\alpha = \mu_s$ | Dimensionless identity |
| 9 | Height of Cone | $h = r\tan\theta$ | metres (m) |
| 10 | Volume of Cone | $V = \dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^{2} h$ | cubic metres (m³) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition:** $\mu_s \geq \mu_k$ always. If measured values violate this, experimental error is present (usually due to inconsistent surface preparation or excessive sample loading).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Silo and hopper design:** The hopper wall angle must exceed the angle of repose of stored grain to ensure **mass flow discharge** (no arching or rat-holing).
- **Conveyor chute inclination:** Chute angle must lie between **θ + 5°** (minimum for flow) and the angle causing mechanical damage to seeds.
- **Packaging machinery:** Determines optimal fill spout angles in bagging machines.
- **Pneumatic conveying:** Affects particle suspension and saltation velocity calculations.

---

### 2.5 Methods of Determination (Theory Summary)

**A. Funnel Method (Free-Flowing Materials)**
- Material is poured through a funnel held at a fixed height above a base plate.
- Heap forms naturally; height (h) and base radius (r) are measured once the cone stabilises.

**B. Cylinder Method (Cohesive Powders)**
- A transparent cylinder filled with material is slowly raised vertically; the material flows out forming a heap.
- Cylinder radius fixes the base radius; height is measured.

**C. Tilting Box Method**
- A box with material is tilted gradually until the surface just begins to slip; the tilt angle is the angle of repose.

**D. Inclined Plane Method (for Coefficient of Friction)**
- Food sample placed on structural surface (mild steel, plywood, aluminium) on a tilting platform.
- Tilt angle at which sliding begins = static friction angle.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Angle of Repose from Cone Geometry

Consider a conical heap of food material of height $h$ and base radius $r$ resting on a horizontal plate. The slope of the cone makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal base.

**Step 1:** Identify the right triangle formed by the cone's axis (height $h$), the base radius ($r$), and the slant surface (hypotenuse).

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric definition of the tangent function.

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{\text{Opposite Side}}{\text{Adjacent Side}} = \frac{h}{r}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the angle of repose explicitly.

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{h}{r}\right)
$$

**Step 4 (Force balance justification):** On the slant surface, a particle of mass $m$ experiences:
- Component of weight along slope (downward): $mg\sin\theta$
- Frictional resistance along slope (upward): $f_s \leq \mu_s N = \mu_s mg\cos\theta$

At the **incipient sliding** condition on the cone surface, these balance:

$$
mg\sin\theta = \mu_s\, mg\cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $mg$ on both sides.

$$
\sin\theta = \mu_s \cos\theta
$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides by $\cos\theta$ (valid as $\theta < 90°$).

$$
\tan\theta = \mu_s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This derivation shows that the **angle of repose of a granular heap is numerically equal to the internal angle of friction** between particles. This is why both parameters are measured in food engineering and they are physically interlinked.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Coefficient of Friction from Inclined Plane

**Step 1:** Consider a food sample of mass $m$ placed on a structural surface inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal.

**Step 2:** Resolve gravitational force $W = mg$ into two components relative to the inclined plane:
- Perpendicular to the plane: $W_\perp = mg\cos\alpha$
- Parallel to the plane (down the slope): $W_\parallel = mg\sin\alpha$

**Step 3:** The normal force balances the perpendicular component (no motion in that direction).

$$
N = mg\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 4:** The frictional force opposes impending or actual motion along the plane.

$$
F_f = \mu_s N = \mu_s\, mg\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 5:** At the incipient sliding condition, the gravitational pull down the slope exactly equals the maximum static friction.

$$
mg\sin\alpha = \mu_s\, mg\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $\mu_s$.

$$
\mu_s = \frac{\sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = \tan\alpha
$$

**Step 7:** For kinetic friction, repeat the same logic at the constant-velocity sliding condition.

$$
\mu_k = \tan\beta
$$

where $\beta$ is the angle at which the block slides at **uniform velocity** (no acceleration).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem:** A heap of wheat grains is formed by the funnel method. The base radius is measured as $r = 4.5$ cm and the height of the cone is $h = 3.8$ cm. Determine the angle of repose and interpret the flowability.

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{h}{r}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values (ensuring consistent units — both in cm cancels).

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{3.8 \text{ cm}}{4.5 \text{ cm}} = 0.8444
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\theta$.

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.8444)
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate numerically.

$$
\theta \approx 40.2^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5 (Interpretation per KTU Standard Scale):**

| Angle of Repose Range | Flowability Classification |
|----------------------:|----------------------------|
| $< 30°$ | Very free-flowing |
| $30°-38°$ | Free-flowing |
| $38°-45°$ | Fair to passable flow |
| $45°-55°$ | Cohesive, poor flow |
| $> 55°$ | Very cohesive, non-flowing |

**Conclusion:** $\theta = 40.2°$ → **Fair to passable flow**, typical of cleaned, dry wheat.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example for Coefficient of Friction

> **Problem:** A sample of rice grains placed on a mild steel surface begins to slide when the platform is tilted to $\alpha = 28°$. Determine the static coefficient of friction.

**Step 1:** Apply the derivation result.

$$
\mu_s = \tan\alpha
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\mu_s = \tan(28^{\circ}) = 0.5317
$$

**Step 3:** Result.

$$
\boxed{\mu_s \approx 0.53}
$$

**Step 4 (Engineering interpretation):** This value indicates moderate friction — suitable for **gravity-flow chutes** in rice mills where some resistance prevents grain from rolling too fast and fragmenting.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Angle of Repose Determination

```mermaid

    A10[Compute Theta = arctan h over r]
    A11[Record Theta in Degrees]
    A12[Repeat for 3 Trials]

    A14[Report Angle of Repose Result]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2

```

```mermaid

    B6[Apply Mu Static = tan Alpha]

    B8[Continue Tilting Until Steady Sliding]
    B9[Record Tilt Angle Beta for Steady Motion]
    B10[Apply Mu Kinetic = tan Beta]
    B11[Compute Kinetic Coefficient of Friction]
    B12[Repeat for Multiple Surfaces]

    B14[Report Friction Coefficients]

    B0 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Force Balance Schematic on Inclined Plane (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Food Sample Mass m on Inclined Surface]
    C2[Weight W = mg Acts Vertically Downward]
    C3[Resolve W into Two Components]
    C4[Normal Component N = mg cos Alpha]
    C5[Parallel Component W parallel = mg sin Alpha]
    C6[Normal Force N from Surface on Sample]
    C7[Frictional Force Ff opposing Motion]
    C8[At Incipient Slide mg sin Alpha equals Ff]
    C9[Static Friction Fs equals Mu Static times N]
    C10[Solve and obtain Mu Static = tan Alpha]

    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C6
    C5 --> C8

    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C10
    C9 --> C10
```

### 4.4 Apparatus Layout Summary

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Funnel (with stand) | Free-flowing sample discharge |
| Base plate (graph paper beneath) | Visualises cone base diameter |
| Transparent cylinder | Alternative heap formation method |
| Tilting table with protractor | Inclined plane for friction test |
| Test surface coupons (MS, SS, GI, plywood, aluminium) | Simulate chute wall materials |
| Vernier caliper / ruler | Precise height and diameter reading |
| Food samples (rice, wheat, dal, sugar, turmeric) | Granular test materials |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define the term **angle of repose** and mention any two methods used for its determination for food materials.

**Model Answer:**
The angle of repose is the maximum angle (measured from the horizontal) that the free surface of a heap of granular food material makes with the horizontal plane when the heap is at the point of incipient sliding. It is a direct indicator of flowability.

Two methods used for food materials are:
1. **Funnel method** — sample is poured through a fixed funnel onto a base plate; cone height (h) and base radius (r) are measured; $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/r)$.
2. **Tilting box method** — a box of sample is gradually tilted until the top surface just begins to slip; the tilt angle equals the angle of repose.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What is the **coefficient of friction**? Differentiate between **static** and **kinetic** coefficient of friction as applied to food grains.

**Model Answer:**
The coefficient of friction ($\mu$) is the dimensionless ratio of the frictional force between two contacting surfaces to the normal force pressing them together: $\mu = F_f / N$.

| Aspect | Static ($\mu_s$) | Kinetic ($\mu_k$) |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Resistance before motion begins | Resistance during ongoing sliding |
| When measured | At the moment sliding is about to start | At uniform sliding velocity |
| Magnitude | Always $\geq$ corresponding $\mu_k$ | Always $\leq$ corresponding $\mu_s$ |
| Governing equation | $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$ (incipient slide angle) | $\mu_k = \tan\beta$ (steady motion angle) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression relating the **angle of repose** to the **internal angle of friction** for a granular food material. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heap of Bengal gram (chana dal) is formed using the funnel method. The measured values are:

| Trial | Base Diameter (cm) | Cone Height (cm) |
|------:|-------------------:|-----------------:|
| 1 | 9.0 | 4.0 |
| 2 | 9.4 | 4.1 |
| 3 | 8.8 | 3.9 |

Calculate the mean angle of repose and classify its flowability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- Heap shape is a perfect right circular cone.
- Particles are uniform in size and shape.
- Particles are cohesionless (no inter-particle adhesion).
- Surface is horizontal and non-deforming.
- Sliding occurs only along the slant surface.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a particle of mass $m$ resting on the slant surface of the cone at angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** Identify the forces:
- Weight $W = mg$ vertically downward
- Normal reaction $N$ perpendicular to slant
- Static friction $f_s$ along the slant (uphill)

**Step 3:** Resolve $W$ along and perpendicular to the slant.

$$
N = mg\cos\theta
$$

$$
F_{\text{driving}} = mg\sin\theta
$$

**Step 4:** At incipient sliding, $F_{\text{driving}} = f_s = \mu_s N$.

$$
mg\sin\theta = \mu_s\, mg\cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $mg$.

$$
\sin\theta = \mu_s \cos\theta
$$

**Step 6:** Divide by $\cos\theta$.

$$
\tan\theta = \mu_s
$$

**Step 7:** Conclude that the **angle of repose equals the angle of internal friction** for the granular material.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute base radius for each trial: $r = d/2$.

| Trial | $d$ (cm) | $r$ (cm) | $h$ (cm) | $\tan\theta = h/r$ | $\theta$ (°) |
|------:|---------:|---------:|---------:|-------------------:|-------------:|
| 1 | 9.0 | 4.50 | 4.0 | 0.8889 | 41.63 |
| 2 | 9.4 | 4.70 | 4.1 | 0.8723 | 41.10 |
| 3 | 8.8 | 4.40 | 3.9 | 0.8864 | 41.53 |

**Step 2 (Trial 1 detailed calculation):**

$$
\tan\theta_1 = \frac{4.0}{4.50} = 0.8889 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}(0.8889) \approx 41.63^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 (Trial 2 detailed calculation):**

$$
\tan\theta_2 = \frac{4.1}{4.70} = 0.8723 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = \tan^{-1}(0.8723) \approx 41.10^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 (Trial 3 detailed calculation):**

$$
\tan\theta_3 = \frac{3.9}{4.40} = 0.8864 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_3 = \tan^{-1}(0.8864) \approx 41.53^{\circ}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the mean.

$$
\bar{\theta} = \frac{41.63 + 41.10 + 41.53}{3} = \frac{124.26}{3} \approx 41.42^{\circ}
$$

**Step 6:** Classify flowability using KTU scale: $\theta = 41.42°$ lies in the **38°–45°** range → **Fair to passable flow** (typical of cleaned, dry legumes).

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **inclined plane method** for determining the **static and kinetic coefficient of friction** of a food sample on a mild steel surface. List the apparatus required and the procedural steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turmeric powder sample placed on a galvanised iron (GI) surface begins to slide at $\alpha = 32°$ and continues to slide uniformly at $\beta = 27°$. Calculate the static and kinetic coefficients of friction. Comment on the **design implications for hopper wall angle** in a turmeric processing plant. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Inclined Plane Method (7 marks):**

**Principle:** A food sample is placed on a structural test surface mounted on a tilting table. As the table is tilted gradually, the angle at which the sample begins to slide gives the static friction angle, and the angle at which it slides at constant velocity gives the kinetic friction angle.

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Tilting platform with calibrated protractor (0°–60°)
2. Test surface coupons: mild steel, GI, stainless steel, aluminium, plywood
3. Food sample container (small tray or block of sample)
4. Slow-release tilting mechanism / screw jack
5. Marker / protractor for angle reading

**Procedural Steps:**
1. Clean and dry the test surface; mount it horizontally.
2. Place the food sample (a small mass of grain or powder pressed into a sample holder) on the surface.
3. Slowly tilt the platform at a uniform rate (~1° per second).
4. Observe the moment the sample just begins to move — record this angle as $\alpha$.
5. Continue tilting; identify the angle $\beta$ at which the sample slides at **constant velocity** (use markers or stopwatch).
6. Repeat for 3 trials on each test surface; compute mean.
7. Compute $\mu_s = \tan\alpha$ and $\mu_k = \tan\beta$.

**Engineering significance:** Static $\mu$ governs the angle at which material starts to discharge (critical for hopper design), while kinetic $\mu$ governs the sustained flow rate (critical for chute design).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\alpha = 32°$ (incipient slide), $\beta = 27°$ (steady motion)

**Step 1:** Compute static coefficient of friction.

$$
\mu_s = \tan(32^{\circ}) = 0.6249 \approx 0.625
$$

**Step 2:** Compute kinetic coefficient of friction.

$$
\mu_k = \tan(27^{\circ}) = 0.5095 \approx 0.510
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the boundary condition: $\mu_s \geq \mu_k$.

$$
0.625 \geq 0.510 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 (Design implication):** 

The hopper wall angle must be **steeper than the angle of internal friction** for turmeric powder to flow reliably. For mass-flow hopper design:

- **Internal friction angle (≈ angle of repose)** must be measured separately using the funnel method.
- **Wall friction angle (≈ $\alpha$ at slide onset on the wall material)** here is 32°.
- The hopper valley angle should typically be designed as: $\theta_{\text{valley}} \geq \alpha + 5° \approx 37°$ from vertical (i.e., a steep conical hopper with half-angle $\leq 53°$ from horizontal) to ensure gravity-driven discharge of cohesive turmeric powder.

**Step 5:** For chute design in conveying turmeric, the chute inclination must lie between $\beta$ (minimum for sustained flow = 27°) and the damage-threshold angle for turmeric granules (~40°). This range ensures **flow without product damage**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m in $\tan\theta = h/r$. Always convert to a single unit; ratio is dimensionless so it cancels, but for clarity, KTU examiners prefer mm or cm uniformity.
> 2. **Forgetting the angle of internal friction identity:** Many students write $\theta$ and $\mu_s$ as separate unrelated quantities, missing the $\tan\theta = \mu_s$ derivation — a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss.
> 3. **Skipping the $mg$ cancellation step:** Examiners award step marks for the algebraic transition; never skip from $mg\sin\theta = \mu_s mg\cos\theta$ directly to the final result.
> 4. **Reporting angle of repose as a single trial:** KTU strictly requires **mean of three trials with standard deviation** — omitting trials loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing $\mu_s$ with $\mu_k$:** Static is always measured first (at the verge of motion), kinetic is at steady motion. Reversing the assignment loses full sub-part marks.
> 6. **Not commenting on design implications:** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect a final paragraph on **engineering application** (hopper angle, chute angle, silo design). Missing this is a 1–2 mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Angle of Repose (θ)** = maximum angle of granular pile from horizontal; measured in **degrees**; indicates **flowability**.
- **Funnel method formula:** $\tan\theta = h/r$ where $h$ = cone height, $r$ = base radius.
- **Tilting box method formula:** $\theta = \sin^{-1}(h/L)$ where $L$ = box length.
- **Cylinder method formula:** $\tan\theta = h/r$ where $r$ = cylinder radius (fixed).
- **Flowability scale:** $<30°$ very free; $30°$–$38°$ free; $38°$–$45°$ fair; $45°$–$55°$ cohesive; $>55°$ non-flowing.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ)** = dimensionless ratio of frictional force to normal force.
- **Static μₛ** = $\tan\alpha$, measured at incipient sliding.
- **Kinetic μₖ** = $\tan\beta$, measured at constant-velocity sliding.
- **Boundary rule:** $\mu_s \geq \mu_k$ — always.
- **Fundamental identity:** $\tan\theta = \mu_s$ (angle of repose equals angle of internal friction for cohesionless granular food).
- **Hopper design rule:** Wall angle from vertical $\geq$ wall friction angle $+ 5°$.
- **Chute design rule:** Inclination must be between kinetic friction angle and the damage threshold of the food product.
- **Standard test surfaces in food labs:** Mild steel, stainless steel, galvanised iron, aluminium, plywood, rubber.
- **Standard food samples tested:** Rice, wheat, maize, Bengal gram, tur dal, sugar, turmeric, coriander, milk powder.
- **Always report:** mean of **3 trials** with **standard deviation**.
- **Always state units** explicitly in the final answer — even though $\mu$ is dimensionless, convention requires the statement "dimensionless" or "no unit."
- **Always draw force diagrams** in 7-mark derivations — missing diagrams cost 1–2 marks.
- **Always link the result to an industry application** in 14-mark questions for full marks.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Foundations of Food Technology Lab (GDFTL106)**
> *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L3–L5: Apply, Analyze, Evaluate), and Course Outcomes CO1–CO3.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of True Density, Bulk Density, Porosity, Hausner Ratio, and Compressibility Index of Different Food Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of True Density, Bulk Density, Porosity, Hausner Ratio, and Compressibility Index of Different Food Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (GDFTL106 | Module 2):** This experiment falls under the **"Physical Properties of Foods"** cluster of the lab manual. The KTU Board Examiner expects you to (a) tabulate raw data, (b) state the governing formula *before* substituting values, and (c) interpret the result in terms of **flow behavior** of the powder/granular food.

## 1.1 What Are We Actually Measuring?

In a real food sample — say, a handful of wheat flour, sugar, coffee granules, or milk powder — the particles are **not** packed as a solid block of steel. They are a jumble of solid grains with **air gaps (voids)** trapped between them, and within each grain there may be **internal pores**. So when you weigh a sample and measure the volume it occupies, the answer you get depends on *which* volume you mean:

| Term | What It Includes | Engineering Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **True Volume (Vt)** | Only the solid skeleton — no inter-particle voids, no internal pores. | Density of defect-free crystal |
| **Bulk Volume (Vb)** | Solid + inter-particle air voids (as poured loosely) | "Loose" powder bed |
| **Tapped Volume (Vtap)** | Solid + reduced inter-particle voids (after mechanical tapping) | "Settled" powder bed |

### 1.2 Formal KTU Definitions

- **True Density (ρt):** The mass of the food material per unit *true* volume, i.e., the volume of the solid material alone, excluding all pores and inter-particle voids. **SI unit: kg/m³ (commonly g/cm³).**
- **Bulk Density (ρb):** The mass of the food material per unit *apparent* (bulk) volume, including the voids between particles. **SI unit: kg/m³ (commonly g/cm³).**
- **Porosity (ε):** The ratio of the volume of voids (inter-particle air spaces) to the total bulk volume of the food material, expressed as a fraction or percentage. **Unitless.**
- **Hausner Ratio (HR):** The ratio of tapped density to bulk density — a single number that summarizes how much a powder *settles* under vibration. **Unitless.**
- **Carr's Compressibility Index (CI):** The percentage difference between tapped and bulk density relative to the tapped density. **Unitless, expressed as %.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does KTU care?** These five numbers completely characterize how a powder *flows* out of a hopper, how it packs inside a sachet, how it dissolves in your mouth, and how it stores in a silo. They are the very first tests run during **product development** of any powdered food (spice mixes, instant coffee, protein supplements, salt, sugar, baby formula).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge in a Jar" Picture

Imagine you take a **sponge (the solid food)**, cut it into small cubes, and toss them loosely into a glass jar. The space taken up by the cubes in the jar includes (i) the sponge material itself, and (ii) all the air gaps between the cubes.

- The **mass of sponge** divided by the *volume of sponge material alone* (imagine melting the cubes back into one solid lump) = **True Density**.
- The **mass of sponge** divided by the *volume occupied in the jar* (sponge + air gaps) = **Bulk Density**.
- If you tap the jar on the table 100 times, the cubes settle, the air gaps shrink, and the volume goes down. The new mass-per-volume = **Tapped Density**.
- The **fraction of the jar that is empty air** = **Porosity**.
- How much the volume *shrank* from tapping tells you the **Hausner Ratio** and **Compressibility Index** — i.e., how "cooperative" the particles are in packing tightly.

A powder that barely changes volume on tapping (HR ≈ 1.0) flows like water. A powder that shrinks a lot (HR ≈ 1.5) is "sticky," bridges in hoppers, and gives inconsistent sachet weights — a nightmare for a packaging line.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Chain of Reasoning

Why do we measure all five parameters and not just one?

1. **Bulk density alone** tells you how heavy a finished package will be for a given volume — important for *logistics* and *pack size design*.
2. **True density** is an *intrinsic* material property — it lets you identify an adulterant (e.g., detecting starch in ground spices) and calculate the **porosity**, which is the single most important parameter governing *rehydration, oil absorption, and crispness* in dried foods.
3. **Hausner Ratio and Compressibility Index** are *derived* from bulk and tapped density — they are the **industry-standard go/no-go indicators** of powder flowability, used directly by QC labs.
4. **Porosity** determines *storage stability* — high porosity means more oxygen exposure, faster oxidative rancidity, and shorter shelf life in fat-rich powders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Saver Rule:** Always report bulk density and tapped density in the *same units*. Always state porosity as a *percentage* (not a fraction) unless the question specifically asks for the fraction. The relation ρtap ≥ ρb ≥ ρt is **never** true for the same powder — the correct hierarchy is **ρt ≥ ρtap ≥ ρb**.

## 2.2 The Governing Equations — Master Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Convention:** All masses are in **grams (g)**, all volumes in **cm³** (mL), and all densities in **g/cm³** unless stated otherwise. KTU exam answers consistently use **g/cm³** or **kg/m³** — convert carefully!

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1) Bulk Density:}\quad &\rho_{b} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{b}} \\
\textbf{(2) True Density:}\quad &\rho_{t} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{t}} \\
\textbf{(3) Tapped Density:}\quad &\rho_{tap} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{tap}} \\
\textbf{(4) Porosity (\%):}\quad &\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\rho_{t}-\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}} \times 100 \\
\textbf{(5) Hausner Ratio:}\quad &HR \;=\; \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{b}} \\
\textbf{(6) Compressibility Index (\%):}\quad &CI \;=\; \frac{\rho_{tap}-\rho_{b}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | SI Unit | Engineering Interpretation |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b$ | $m \div V_b$ | **kg/m³** | Mass per "as-poured" volume — used in packaging/silo design |
| 2 | True Density | $\rho_t$ | $m \div V_t$ | **kg/m³** | Intrinsic property — used in porosity & purity checks |
| 3 | Tapped Density | $\rho_{tap}$ | $m \div V_{tap}$ | **kg/m³** | Mass per "settled" volume — used in flowability |
| 4 | Porosity | $\varepsilon$ | $(1 - \rho_b/\rho_t) \times 100$ | **%** (or fraction) | Fraction of bulk volume that is empty air |
| 5 | Hausner Ratio | HR | $\rho_{tap} \div \rho_b$ | **dimensionless** | Settling tendency — 1.0 = no settling, >1.4 = poor flow |
| 6 | Compressibility Index | CI | $(1 - \rho_b/\rho_{tap}) \times 100$ | **%** | Industry-standard flowability index (Carr's scale) |

### 2.3 Why These Equations Make Physical Sense

- **Porosity** is "how empty the bulk is." If the bulk density equals the true density, the powder is a perfect solid block and porosity = 0. If the bulk is "half air," porosity = 50%. So porosity is the *fractional* difference between the two densities, normalized by the true density.
- **Hausner Ratio** is essentially "how much bigger is the tapped density compared to the bulk density." If they are equal (HR = 1.0), tapping did nothing — the powder was already perfectly packed (e.g., smooth glass beads). If HR is large, the powder was very loosely packed and had lots of room to settle.
- **CI** is just HR re-expressed as a percentage shrinkage. CI and HR carry the *same information* — they are mathematically interconvertible:

$$CI \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Students frequently write the porosity formula as $(\rho_b/\rho_t) \times 100$. This is the **relative density** (or *packing fraction*), not porosity. **Porosity = 1 − relative density = 1 − (ρb/ρt).** Examiners deduct full marks for this.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Parameter Used | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sachet / pouch packaging** | $\rho_b$ | Determines fill weight for a fixed-volume machine |
| **Hopper / silo design** | HR, CI | Predicts arching, rat-holing, and flow stoppages |
| **Spray-dried coffee/milk** | $\varepsilon$ | High porosity → fast rehydration; low porosity → dense, slow-dissolving |
| **Fried snacks (chips, namkeen)** | $\varepsilon$ | Oil absorption during frying is proportional to porosity |
| **Detecting adulteration** | $\rho_t$ | Pure substances have characteristic $\rho_t$; deviation flags adulterants |
| **Tableting / agglomeration** | $\rho_{tap}$ | Sets the die-fill volume in a tablet press |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Porosity Formula

Porosity is, by definition, the *fraction of the bulk volume that is occupied by voids*:

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{V_{voids}}{V_{bulk}}$$

We have one sample of mass *m* that occupies two volumes:
- $V_t = m / \rho_t$ (the volume of solid only)
- $V_b = m / \rho_b$ (the volume of solid + voids)

Therefore the void volume is the difference:

$$V_{voids} \;=\; V_{b} - V_{t} \;=\; \frac{m}{\rho_{b}} - \frac{m}{\rho_{t}}$$

Substituting into the definition of porosity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon \;&=\; \frac{V_{b} - V_{t}}{V_{b}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{V_{t}}{V_{b}} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; 1 - \frac{m/\rho_{t}}{m/\rho_{b}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$\varepsilon \,(\%) \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}}\right) \times 100 \;=\; \frac{\rho_{t} - \rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}} \times 100$$

This is the working formula used in the lab. The derivation is **exactly the type of 3-mark question** KTU sets in Part A.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Carr Compressibility Index Formula

CI is defined as the relative change in volume of a powder bed when it is tapped to a constant (minimum) volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CI \;&=\; \frac{V_{b} - V_{tap}}{V_{b}} \times 100 \quad \text{(volume-based definition)} \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \frac{(m/\rho_{b}) - (m/\rho_{tap})}{(m/\rho_{b})} \times 100 \\[4pt]
\;&=\; \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{tap}}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Note the *crucial identity* — the volume-based definition and the density-based definition are mathematically equivalent (because mass is constant), so examiners accept either form.

## 3.3 The Reference Standard — Carr's Flowability Scale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** It is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question in KTU exams and viva voce. The KTU Board Examiner expects you to **state the CI value, then *interpret* the flowability** in one sentence.

| Compressibility Index (%) | Flow Character | Hausner Ratio |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **≤ 10** | Excellent | **1.00 – 1.11** |
| **11 – 15** | Good | **1.12 – 1.18** |
| **16 – 20** | Fair | **1.19 – 1.25** |
| **21 – 25** | Passable | **1.26 – 1.32** |
| **26 – 31** | Poor | **1.33 – 1.45** |
| **32 – 37** | Very poor | **1.46 – 1.59** |
| **> 38** | Very, very poor | **> 1.60** |

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step Lab Procedure

### **Part A — Determination of Bulk Density and Tapped Density**

1. **Weigh** an empty, clean, dry **100 mL graduated cylinder** on an analytical balance. Record as $W_1$.
2. **Gently pour** the food sample (e.g., rice, sugar, wheat flour) into the cylinder through a funnel held at a fixed height (≈ 5 cm above the rim) until the cylinder is full to the 100 mL mark. **Do not tap the cylinder.**
3. **Weigh** the cylinder + sample. Record as $W_2$. Mass of sample $m = W_2 - W_1$.
4. Read the **bulk volume** $V_b$ directly from the cylinder (≈ 100 mL) or from the exact level.
5. **Bulk density** is computed as $\rho_b = m / V_b$.
6. **Tap the cylinder** mechanically (or manually — drop from a height of 2.5 cm onto a hard surface) **100 times**, then read the new volume.
7. Tap another 100 times. If the volume changes by less than 2 mL, take the reading as $V_{tap}$. Otherwise, repeat.
8. **Tapped density** is computed as $\rho_{tap} = m / V_{tap}$.

### **Part B — Determination of True Density (Pycnometer Method, Liquid Displacement)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU allows two methods — **liquid (pycnometer)** and **gas (air) pycnometer**. The pycnometer method is the most commonly asked in exams because it needs only a glass pycnometer, a balance, and a liquid (usually toluene or petroleum ether for fat-rich foods; water is *avoided* for water-soluble powders like sugar or salt).

1. **Weigh** the clean, dry, empty pycnometer. Record as $W_1$.
2. **Fill** the pycnometer with the food sample (about 1/3 full). Weigh and record as $W_2$. Mass of sample $m = W_2 - W_1$.
3. **Add** a known volume of liquid (e.g., toluene) sufficient to *just cover* the sample. **Degas** by gently swirling or by applying vacuum for 10 min so that air bubbles escape from the pores.
4. **Top up** the pycnometer with the same liquid to the calibration mark. Wipe the outside dry. Weigh and record as $W_3$.
5. **Empty** the pycnometer, clean it, **fill it with the same liquid alone** up to the mark, and weigh. Record as $W_4$.
6. The **volume of liquid displaced** (= true volume of the sample) is:

$$V_{t} \;=\; \frac{W_{4} - (W_{3} - W_{2})}{\rho_{liquid}}$$

where $\rho_{liquid}$ is the density of the displacement liquid at the working temperature.

7. Compute $\rho_t = m / V_t$.

### **Part C — Calculated Parameters**

Once $\rho_b$, $\rho_{tap}$, and $\rho_t$ are known, plug into the four derived formulas in §2.2 to obtain porosity, Hausner Ratio, and Compressibility Index.

## 3.5 Fully Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact style** of calculation KTU expects in Part B. Practice writing it in this 5-line format with the formula *before* substitution.

**Question.** A sample of **corn flour** has a mass of 40 g. It loosely fills a 100 mL graduated cylinder to the 80 mL mark. After 200 taps, the volume settles to 64 mL. A separate liquid-displacement pycnometer test gives a true volume of 27.0 cm³ for the same 40 g. Calculate $\rho_b$, $\rho_{tap}$, $\rho_t$, porosity, HR, and CI. Comment on the flowability.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Bulk Density.**

$$
\rho_{b} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{b}} \;=\; \frac{40 \text{ g}}{80 \text{ cm}^3} \;=\; 0.500 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Tapped Density.**

$$
\rho_{tap} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{tap}} \;=\; \frac{40}{64} \;=\; 0.625 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — True Density.**

$$
\rho_{t} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{t}} \;=\; \frac{40}{27.0} \;=\; 1.481 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Porosity.**

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{0.500}{1.481}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 66.24 \%
$$

**Step 5 — Hausner Ratio.**

$$
HR \;=\; \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{b}} \;=\; \frac{0.625}{0.500} \;=\; 1.25
$$

**Step 6 — Compressibility Index.**

$$
CI \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{0.500}{0.625}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 20.00 \%
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (Flowability).**

$$HR = 1.25 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Fair-to-passable flowability} \quad \text{---} \quad CI = 20\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Fair flow character (Carr's scale).}$$

The corn flour flows reasonably well, suitable for a standard hopper, but marginal for high-speed sachet filling without a vibratory feeder.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Food Materials

| Food Material | Typical $\rho_t$ (g/cm³) | Typical $\rho_b$ (g/cm³) | Typical $\varepsilon$ (%) | Typical HR | Typical CI (%) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Wheat flour | 1.45 | 0.45 – 0.55 | 60 – 70 | 1.20 – 1.30 | 17 – 23 |
| Corn flour | 1.48 | 0.50 – 0.60 | 60 – 66 | 1.20 – 1.28 | 17 – 22 |
| Granulated sugar | 1.55 | 0.80 – 0.90 | 42 – 48 | 1.05 – 1.15 | 5 – 13 |
| Salt (fine) | 2.16 | 1.10 – 1.30 | 40 – 50 | 1.15 – 1.30 | 13 – 23 |
| Instant coffee | 1.40 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 78 – 86 | 1.30 – 1.50 | 23 – 35 |
| Whole milk powder | 1.32 | 0.45 – 0.60 | 55 – 66 | 1.20 – 1.35 | 17 – 26 |
| Rice (long grain) | 1.45 | 0.75 – 0.85 | 41 – 48 | 1.05 – 1.12 | 5 – 11 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagrams below map the **functional workflow** of the experiment. They are designed for direct transcription into the KTU lab record and are also accepted as substitutes for hand-drawn apparatus sketches in the model answer sheet.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sample Preparation: Clean, dry, sieve food material through standard mesh"]
    B["Weigh empty graduated cylinder W1 on analytical balance"]
    C["Loosely pour sample into 100 mL cylinder via funnel"]
    D["Read bulk volume Vb and weigh cylinder with sample W2"]
    E["Compute mass m equals W2 minus W1 and bulk density equals m by Vb"]
    F["Mechanically tap cylinder 100 times and read new volume"]
    G["Repeat tapping until successive volume change is less than 2 mL to obtain Vtap"]
    H["Compute tapped density equals m by Vtap"]
    P["Parallel Track: Weigh empty pycnometer WP1"]
    Q["Add sample and weigh pycnometer plus sample WP2"]
    R["Add degassed toluene to cover sample and top up to mark WP3"]
    S["Refill clean pycnometer with toluene only and weigh WP4"]
    T["Compute true volume Vt equals WP4 minus WP3 plus WP2 divided by rho liquid"]
    U["Compute true density equals m by Vt"]
    V["Calculate porosity, Hausner ratio, Compressibility index from the three densities"]
    W["Interpret flowability using Carr scale and HR scale"]
    X["Tabulate results, plot CI versus material, write conclusion"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
    A --> P --> Q --> R --> S --> T --> U
    E --> V
    H --> V
    U --> V --> W --> X
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Interpreting CI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Compute Compressibility Index CI percent and Hausner Ratio HR from lab data"]
    N1{"Is CI less than or equal to 10 percent?"}
    N2{"Is CI between 11 and 15 percent?"}
    N3{"Is CI between 16 and 20 percent?"}
    N4{"Is CI between 21 and 25 percent?"}
    N5{"Is CI between 26 and 31 percent?"}
    N6{"Is CI between 32 and 37 percent?"}
    Y7["Is CI greater than 38 percent?"}

    Op1["Excellent flow, ideal for high speed packaging, HR 1.00 to 1.11"]
    Op2["Good flow, suitable for most hopper systems, HR 1.12 to 1.18"]
    Op3["Fair flow, may need vibratory assistance, HR 1.19 to 1.25"]
    Op4["Passable flow, requires flow aid or glidant, HR 1.26 to 1.32"]
    Op5["Poor flow, hopper bridging likely, HR 1.33 to 1.45"]
    Op6["Very poor flow, redesign particle size or use forced feed, HR 1.46 to 1.59"]
    Op7["Very very poor flow, material is cohesive, granulation or agglomeration needed, HR above 1.60"]

    Start --> N1 -- Yes --> Op1
    N1 -- No --> N2 -- Yes --> Op2
    N2 -- No --> N3 -- Yes --> Op3
    N3 -- No --> N4 -- Yes --> Op4
    N4 -- No --> N5 -- Yes --> Op5
    N5 -- No --> N6 -- Yes --> Op6
    N6 -- No --> Y7 -- Yes --> Op7
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Pycnometer Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pyc["Pycnometer glass vessel with capillary stopper and calibration mark"]
    Bal["Analytical balance reading to 0.0001 g"]
    Samp["Food powder sample held in pycnometer"]
    Liq["Displacement liquid: toluene or petroleum ether for fat samples, water for insoluble powders"]
    Vac["Vacuum desiccator or ultrasonic bath for degassing trapped air"]
    Calc["Computation of true volume from mass balance equations"]

    Samp --> Pyc
    Liq --> Pyc
    Vac --> Pyc
    Pyc --> Bal
    Bal --> Calc
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Penalty (–2 marks):** Mixing grams with cm³ in one equation and kg with m³ in another without explicit conversion. Stick to **g/cm³** throughout the calculation block.
> 2. **Porosity Formula Inversion (–3 marks):** Writing $\varepsilon = (\rho_b/\rho_t)\times 100$ instead of $\varepsilon = (1 - \rho_b/\rho_t)\times 100$. The former is the *relative density* (a fraction < 1), not porosity.
> 3. **Tapped Volume Logic (–2 marks):** Forgetting to state the tapping termination criterion ("until successive volume change < 2 mL or 2% of volume") — the KTU lab manual mandates this and the examiner will hunt for it.
> 4. **No Interpretation (–2 marks):** Computing HR and CI without classifying flowability on Carr's scale is treated as an incomplete answer.

---

## **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *True Density* and *Bulk Density*. State two differences between them.

**Model Answer.**

- **True Density (ρt):** The mass of the food material per unit volume of the solid material alone, excluding all inter-particle voids and internal pores. It is an intrinsic property of the material.
- **Bulk Density (ρb):** The mass of the food material per unit apparent volume of the powder bed as poured loosely into a container, including all the inter-particle air spaces.

| S. No. | True Density | Bulk Density |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Excludes voids and pores | Includes inter-particle voids |
| 2 | Measured by pycnometer / gas displacement | Measured by graduated cylinder |
| 3 | Always **higher** in numerical value | Always **lower** in numerical value |
| 4 | Independent of packing | Strongly dependent on packing method |

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the Carr Compressibility Index? Write its formula and state the flowability range corresponding to a "good" flowing powder.

**Model Answer.**

The **Carr Compressibility Index (CI)** is a numerical index, expressed as a percentage, that indicates the flowability of a powder based on its bulk and tapped densities. It is calculated as:

$$CI \;=\; \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{b}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$$

A "**good**" flowing powder has a Compressibility Index in the range of **11 % to 15 %**, with a corresponding Hausner Ratio of **1.12 to 1.18**. *(3 marks — definition, formula, range with HR value.)*

---

## **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)** — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe the principle, apparatus, and procedure for the determination of *true density* of a food powder using a liquid-displacement pycnometer. State the formula used.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

**Principle:** True density is determined by measuring the volume of liquid displaced by a known mass of the food powder. The powder is immersed in a liquid that does not dissolve or swell it (e.g., toluene for oily powders, petroleum ether for fats, water only for insoluble powders like starch). The displaced liquid volume equals the true volume of the solid material, as the liquid penetrates the inter-particle voids and the trapped air is removed by vacuum degassing.

**Apparatus Required:** Glass pycnometer (25 or 50 mL with capillary stopper), analytical balance (±0.0001 g), displacement liquid (toluene/petroleum ether), vacuum desiccator or ultrasonic bath, thermometer, tissue paper, dropper.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean and dry the pycnometer. Weigh empty with the stopper: $W_1$.
2. Fill the pycnometer to about one-third with the food sample. Weigh: $W_2$. Sample mass $m = W_2 - W_1$.
3. Add a small quantity of the displacement liquid to *just cover* the sample. Place in a vacuum desiccator for 10–15 minutes (or in an ultrasonic bath for 5 min) to **degas** — i.e., to remove trapped air from pores.
4. Top up the pycnometer with the same liquid exactly up to the calibration mark on the capillary stopper. Wipe dry externally. Weigh: $W_3$.
5. Empty and clean the pycnometer. Refill with the *same liquid only* up to the mark. Weigh: $W_4$.
6. Record the density of the displacement liquid $\rho_{liq}$ at the working temperature (from a standard table — for toluene at 25 °C, $\rho_{liq} \approx 0.866$ g/cm³).

**Formula:**

$$V_{t} \;=\; \frac{W_{4} - (W_{3} - W_{2})}{\rho_{liq}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_{t} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{t}} \;=\; \frac{(W_{2}-W_{1})\,\rho_{liq}}{W_{4} - W_{3} + W_{2}}$$

**(b) Define *Hausner Ratio* and *Compressibility Index*. Explain how they characterize the flow behavior of a food powder. Use a labeled table to classify flowability based on CI values.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

- **Hausner Ratio (HR):** It is the ratio of the tapped density of a powder to its bulk density, $HR = \rho_{tap}/\rho_b$. A value close to 1.0 indicates that the powder is already densely packed and shows little further volume reduction on tapping; higher values indicate poor packing and greater inter-particle friction.
- **Compressibility Index (CI):** It is the percentage decrease in the bulk volume of a powder on tapping, expressed in terms of density as $CI = (1 - \rho_b/\rho_{tap}) \times 100$.

These two indices are *mathematically equivalent* indicators of flowability: $CI = (1 - 1/HR)\times 100$. They reflect the **cohesiveness and inter-particle friction** of the powder. A powder that settles a lot on tapping has high HR/CI and poor flow — it will bridge in hoppers, rat-hole in silos, and give inconsistent fill weights. A powder that barely settles (low HR/CI) flows like a liquid and is preferred for high-speed packaging.

**Carr's Flowability Classification Table:**

| Compressibility Index (%) | Flow Character | Hausner Ratio |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| ≤ 10 | Excellent | 1.00 – 1.11 |
| 11 – 15 | Good | 1.12 – 1.18 |
| 16 – 20 | Fair | 1.19 – 1.25 |
| 21 – 25 | Passable | 1.26 – 1.32 |
| 26 – 31 | Poor | 1.33 – 1.45 |
| 32 – 37 | Very poor | 1.46 – 1.59 |
| > 38 | Very, very poor | > 1.60 |

A typical KTU viva question: *"If HR = 1.6, what would you do to improve flow?"* — Answer: add a **glidant** (e.g., 0.5% colloidal silica), **granulate** the powder to increase particle size, or **dry-blend** with a free-flowing carrier.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe the experimental procedure to determine the bulk density and tapped density of a granular food material. How are these used to compute the Hausner Ratio and the Compressibility Index? List the sources of experimental error.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

**Procedure for Bulk Density:**
1. Weigh an empty, dry 100 mL graduated cylinder: $W_1$.
2. Using a funnel held at a fixed height (≈ 5 cm above the rim) to avoid compaction, gently pour the food sample into the cylinder until the 100 mL mark is reached.
3. Weigh the cylinder with the sample: $W_2$. Sample mass $m = W_2 - W_1$.
4. Read the bulk volume $V_b$ (≈ 100 mL) directly.
5. Compute $\rho_b = m/V_b$.

**Procedure for Tapped Density:**
1. Mechanically tap the cylinder 100 times by raising it to a height of 2.5 cm and letting it drop, or by using a motorized tapper.
2. Read the new volume. Tap 100 more times. If the volume changes by **< 2 mL (or < 2%)**, accept it as the tapped volume $V_{tap}$. Otherwise, repeat.
3. Compute $\rho_{tap} = m/V_{tap}$.

**Derived Parameters:**

$$HR \;=\; \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{b}}, \qquad CI \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{tap}}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right)\times 100$$

**Sources of Error:**
- Variation in **pour height** and **funnel geometry** (alters bulk density).
- **Moisture content** of the sample (water acts as a binder and inflates volume).
- **Particle size distribution** — fines pack differently from coarse granules.
- **Operator dependence** in reading meniscus and tapping force.
- **Static charge** in dry powders (e.g., cocoa, milk powder) causing clinging to glass walls.
- Non-attainment of the **"constant volume"** criterion during tapping.

**(b) A 50 g sample of whole milk powder is loosely filled to 78 mL in a 100 mL graduated cylinder. After 200 taps, the volume reduces to 60 mL. A pycnometer test on the same 50 g gives a true volume of 36.0 cm³. Calculate the bulk density, tapped density, true density, porosity, Hausner ratio, and Compressibility index. Classify the flowability using Carr's scale.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution.**

**Given Data:** $m = 50$ g; $V_b = 78$ mL; $V_{tap} = 60$ mL; $V_t = 36.0$ cm³.

**Step 1 — Bulk Density:**

$$\rho_{b} \;=\; \frac{m}{V_{b}} \;=\; \frac{50}{78} \;=\; 0.641 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Tapped Density:**

$$\rho_{tap} \;=\; \frac{50}{60} \;=\; 0.833 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — True Density:**

$$\rho_{t} \;=\; \frac{50}{36.0} \;=\; 1.389 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Porosity:**

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{b}}{\rho_{t}}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{0.641}{1.389}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 53.85 \%$$

**Step 5 — Hausner Ratio:**

$$HR \;=\; \frac{0.833}{0.641} \;=\; 1.30$$

**Step 6 — Compressibility Index:**

$$CI \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right)\times 100 \;=\; \left(1 - \frac{0.641}{0.833}\right)\times 100 \;=\; 23.05 \%$$

**Step 7 — Flowability Classification:**

CI = 23.05 % falls in the range **21–25 %** → **"Passable"** flow. The corresponding HR = 1.30 also lies in the "Passable" range (1.26–1.32). The whole milk powder has moderate flow behavior; it will require a flow aid (e.g., 0.5–1 % colloidal silica) for trouble-free high-speed filling, but is acceptable for low-speed packing.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- [x] **Five numbers, one experiment:** Bulk density, tapped density, true density, porosity, and the flowability indices (HR & CI) are the complete output of this lab.
- [x] **Hierarchy of densities (always!):** $\rho_t > \rho_{tap} > \rho_b$. Never the reverse.
- [x] **Porosity formula trap:** $\varepsilon = (1 - \rho_b/\rho_t)\times 100$, **not** $(\rho_b/\rho_t)\times 100$.
- [x] **HR = 1.0 means no settling** (theoretically perfect packing). HR > 1.4 means poor flow.
- [x] **CI & HR are mathematically equivalent** — $CI = (1 - 1/HR)\times 100$ — but CI is the *industry* standard.
- [x] **Bulk density procedure:** pour through a fixed-height funnel, *do not tap*. Tapped density requires tapping until volume change is **< 2 mL or 2 %** between two consecutive 100-tap cycles.
- [x] **Pycnometer rule:** always use a liquid that does *not* dissolve or swell the food — toluene for fat-rich powders, water only for insoluble powders.
- [x] **Degas the sample** (vacuum or ultrasound) before topping up the pycnometer — trapped air gives a falsely high true volume and a falsely low true density.
- [x] **Interpret, don't just compute:** every CI/HR answer must end with a Carr-scale classification sentence.
- [x] **Use a single unit system** (g and cm³ preferred) throughout to avoid KTU's "unit inconsistency" penalty.
- [x] **Three common viva questions to brace for:**
   1. *"Why toluene and not water for oilseeds?"* — water is polar and would be absorbed; toluene is non-polar and inert.
   2. *"Why is porosity important in spray-dried coffee?"* — high porosity → fast rehydration and aroma release.
   3. *"If your tapped volume kept decreasing past 500 taps, what does that say?"* — the powder is cohesive / has not reached its steady-state packing; either the criterion is wrong or the material is unusually sticky.
- [x] **Examiner's pet line:** *"Carr's compressibility index is a one-number summary of how a powder will behave on a packaging line."* Memorize it verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Reynold's Number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Reynold's Number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reynold's Number (Re)** is a fundamental dimensionless number in fluid mechanics that quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces within a fluid. It is used to predict the flow regime — whether the flow will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent** — in a given physical situation.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the product of **density ($\rho$)**, **velocity ($v$)**, and **characteristic length ($D$)** to the **dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** of the fluid.

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment demonstrates the physical significance of the dimensionless number Re in determining the nature of fluid flow. It establishes a clear visual demarcation between **steady (streamline) flow** and **turbulent flow** using a dye tracer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sipping a thick milkshake through a straw versus gulping water through a garden hose.

- When you sip the milkshake **slowly**, the liquid moves in an orderly, parallel, layered fashion. This is **laminar flow**.
- When you turn on the garden hose at **full force**, the water gushes chaotically, mixing and swirling. This is **turbulent flow**.

Reynold's Number mathematically captures *which of these two scenarios* is happening inside a pipe, by comparing two opposing forces:

| Force Type | Origin | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Inertial Forces** | Fluid's own momentum (mass × velocity) | Promote turbulence |
| **Viscous Forces** | Internal fluid friction (viscosity) | Promote order/laminarity |

If **inertial forces dominate** (high Re) → flow becomes **turbulent**.
If **viscous forces dominate** (low Re) → flow remains **laminar**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Critical Values for Pipe Flow:**
> - **Re < 2000** → **Laminar (Streamline) Flow**
> - **2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000** → **Transitional Flow**
> - **Re > 4000** → **Turbulent Flow**

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard values are required during the experiment:

- **Density of water at 25 °C:** $\rho = 998 \, kg/m^3$ (approximated as $1000 \, kg/m^3$ in most lab settings)
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C:** $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \, Pa \cdot s$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at 25 °C:** $\nu = \mu/\rho = 0.89 \times 10^{-6} \, m^2/s$
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

> [!NOTE]
> Always note the **water temperature** during the experiment, as viscosity is highly temperature-dependent. Warmer water → lower viscosity → higher Re.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Osborne Reynolds (1883)

In **1883**, British scientist **Osborne Reynolds** conducted the classic experiment to visualize flow behaviour. The principle he established forms the cornerstone of modern fluid dynamics and is the basis for the experiment performed in this module.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

1. **Fluid is set in motion** through a transparent horizontal glass tube of known diameter $D$.
2. A **dye tracer** (commonly **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** or **methylene blue**) is injected at the entrance of the tube.
3. The **flow rate $Q$** is regulated using a control valve at the outlet.
4. As $Q$ increases gradually, the dye behaviour is observed:
   - At **low flow** → dye travels as a **straight, unbroken filament** (Laminar).
   - At **moderate flow** → dye **wavers, oscillates, and breaks up** (Transitional).
   - At **high flow** → dye **disperses rapidly** and mixes with the bulk fluid (Turbulent).
5. For each flow rate, Re is computed, and the **critical transition value (~2300)** is verified.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' of Each Variable

| Variable | Symbol | Role in Determining Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity | $v$ | Higher $v$ → higher kinetic energy → promotes turbulence |
| Pipe Diameter | $D$ | Larger $D$ → larger inertial volume → promotes turbulence |
| Density | $\rho$ | Heavier fluid → more inertia per unit volume → promotes turbulence |
| Viscosity | $\mu$ | Stiffer fluid resistance → suppresses turbulence |

> [!TIP]
> The Re equation can be memorised as **"Inertia / Viscous"** forces, where the numerator contains the inertial terms ($\rho v D$) and the denominator contains the viscous term ($\mu$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Primary definition (SI form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$ | Alternate form using kinematic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Volumetric flow rate (Volume per time) | $m^3/s$ |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of the pipe | $m^2$ |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Mean velocity of fluid in pipe | $m/s$ |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$ | Substitution form using $Q$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity relation | $m^2/s$ |
| 8 | $Re_{critical} = 2000$ | Upper limit of laminar flow | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re_{critical} = 4000$ | Lower limit of turbulent flow | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use the **diameter $D$** (not radius) as the characteristic length for pipe flow applications of Re.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food Technology

The determination of Re is critical in food processing industries for:

- **Designing pipe networks** for milk, juices, oils, and syrups in dairies and beverage plants.
- **Pump selection** for viscous fluids like honey, chocolate, and ketchup.
- Ensuring **sanitary cleaning-in-place (CIP)** flow regimes are turbulent (Re > 4000) for effective scouring.
- **Heat exchanger design** — flow regime drastically affects heat transfer coefficients.
- Predicting **fouling behaviour** and pressure drops in food pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Working Re Formula in Terms of Measurable Quantities

The Re formula is rewritten in terms of directly measurable laboratory quantities: **volumetric flow rate $Q$** and **pipe diameter $D$**.

### Step 1: Start with the foundational Re equation

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$$

### Step 2: Express mean velocity $v$ in terms of flow rate $Q$ and pipe area $A$

By continuity, the volume of fluid passing per unit time equals the velocity times the cross-sectional area.

$$Q = v \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the area of a circular pipe

The cross-section of the pipe is a circle of diameter $D$.

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 4: Combine the velocity and area expressions

$$v = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $v$ back into the Re equation

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right) \cdot D}{\mu}$$

### Step 6: Simplify the expression algebraically

Cancel one factor of $D$ in the numerator with the denominator's $D^2$.

$$Re = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$$

This is the **final working formula** used in the laboratory for calculating Re directly from the measured volume $V$ (over time $t$) and the known pipe diameter $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic Summary:** The substitution $v = 4Q/(\pi D^2)$ converts an abstract velocity term into measurable volumetric quantities, which is the practical engineering convention.

## 3.2 Analytical Solution Matrix — Sample Numerical Evaluation

**Given laboratory data:**

- Inner diameter of glass tube: $D = 15 \, mm = 0.015 \, m$
- Volume of water collected: $V = 500 \, mL = 500 \times 10^{-6} \, m^3 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$
- Time of collection: $t = 30 \, s$
- Water temperature: $25 \, °C$
- Density of water: $\rho = 998 \, kg/m^3 \approx 1000 \, kg/m^3$
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \, Pa \cdot s$

### Step 1: Compute volumetric flow rate

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3}{30 \, s} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \, m^3/s$$

### Step 2: Compute mean velocity

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.015)^2}$$

$$v = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0943 \, m/s$$

### Step 3: Compute Reynold's Number

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.0943 \times 0.015}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Re = \frac{1.4145}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = 1589$$

### Step 4: Classify the flow regime

Since $Re = 1589 < 2000$, the flow is **laminar (streamline flow)**.

> [!TIP]
> Always end a numerical problem with an **explicit classification statement** — this is a frequently-tested sub-part in KTU examinations.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example (Alternate — Turbulent Case)

**Given:** $D = 20 \, mm = 0.02 \, m$, $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3/s$, $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s$.

### Step 1: Substitute directly into the working Re formula

$$Re = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$$

### Step 2: Plug in numerical values

$$Re = \frac{4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 0.02 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 3: Compute numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.8$$

### Step 4: Compute denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = \pi \times 0.02 \times 0.001 = 6.2832 \times 10^{-5}$$

### Step 5: Final division

$$Re = \frac{0.8}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} = 12732$$

### Step 6: Classify

Since $Re = 12732 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynold's Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Water Tank] --> B[Control Valve Inlet]
    B --> C[Constant Head Reservoir]
    C --> D[Horiz Glass Test Tube D known]
    D --> E[Dye Injection Point KMnO4]
    E --> F[Flow Regulation Valve]
    F --> G[Measuring Cylinder]
    G --> H[Stopwatch Timing]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition V and t]
    I --> J[Compute Q and v]
    J --> K[Calculate Re]
    K --> L{Flow Regime?}
    L -->|Re less than 2000| M[LAMINAR FLOW]
    L -->|Re 2000 to 4000| N[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    L -->|Re greater than 4000| O[TURBULENT FLOW]
```

## 4.2 Flow Regime Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Experiment]) --> MeasureD[Measure Pipe Diameter D]
    MeasureD --> MeasureQ[Collect Volume V in time t]
    MeasureQ --> ComputeQ[Compute Q equals V over t]
    ComputeQ --> ComputeV[Compute v equals 4Q over piD squared]
    ComputeV --> ComputeRe[Compute Re equals rho v D over mu]
    ComputeRe --> Compare1{Re less than 2000?}
    Compare1 -->|Yes| L1[Streamline Flow Confirmed]
    Compare1 -->|No| Compare2{Re less than 4000?}
    Compare2 -->|Yes| L2[Transitional Flow]
    Compare2 -->|No| L3[Turbulent Flow Confirmed]
    L1 --> End([End])
    L2 --> End
    L3 --> End
```

## 4.3 Force-Interaction Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inertial Forces] -->|Dominates| B[High Re]
    A2[Viscous Forces] -->|Dominates| C[Low Re]
    B --> D[Turbulent Flow]
    C --> E[Laminar Flow]
    D --> F[Re greater than 4000]
    E --> G[Re less than 2000]
    F --> H[Food Application - CIP Cleaning]
    G --> I[Food Application - Viscous Syrup Pumping]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flow regime is fundamentally a contest between the fluid's **momentum** (inertia) and the fluid's **internal friction** (viscosity). The Re number is a quantitative scoreboard of this contest.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Reynold's Number. State its significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Reynold's Number is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a flowing fluid.

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

**Significance:**

- It predicts whether a fluid flow is **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.
- For pipe flow: **Re < 2000** → Laminar, **2000–4000** → Transitional, **Re > 4000** → Turbulent.
- It is a **similarity parameter**, allowing laboratory model results to be scaled up to industrial pipelines.

---

### Question 2: What is the physical significance of the critical Reynold's Number? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The critical Reynold's Number marks the threshold at which a **streamline (laminar) flow transitions into a turbulent flow**. Osborne Reynolds experimentally established that:

- For Re < **2000** → flow is fully laminar.
- For Re > **4000** → flow is fully turbulent.
- Between **2000 and 4000** → flow is unstable and transitional.

Physically, it represents the limit where viscous forces lose their ability to suppress the natural instability of the flowing fluid, allowing chaotic eddies to form.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Question Format:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into two 7-mark sub-parts. Students answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Describe with a neat sketch the experimental setup for the determination of Reynold's Number. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Reynold's Apparatus** consists of the following components:

1. **Overhead tank** – supplies water at constant head.
2. **Inlet control valve** – regulates entry flow.
3. **Constant-head reservoir** – maintains steady head via an overflow.
4. **Transparent horizontal glass tube** – of known inner diameter $D$, where flow visualisation occurs.
5. **Dye reservoir** – containing **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** solution, connected to a fine-bore injection tube entering the glass test section.
6. **Outlet flow-control valve** – varies the flow rate $Q$ to transition between flow regimes.
7. **Measuring cylinder and stopwatch** – for volumetric and temporal measurements.

**Working Principle:** Water flows through the glass tube. A thin stream of dye is injected at the entrance. The flow rate is gradually increased by opening the outlet valve. The dye's visual behaviour (straight line, oscillation, or dispersion) is correlated with the computed Re value, distinguishing laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow.

**(b) In a Reynold's experiment, water at 25 °C flows through a 20 mm diameter glass tube. The volume of water collected is 600 mL in 25 seconds. Calculate the Reynold's Number and identify the flow regime. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 20 \, mm = 0.02 \, m$
- $V = 600 \, mL = 6 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$
- $t = 25 \, s$
- $\rho = 1000 \, kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \, Pa \cdot s$

**Step 1: Compute flow rate**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{25} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \, m^3/s$$

**Step 2: Apply the working Re formula**

$$Re = \frac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$Re = \frac{4 \times 1000 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.02 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.096$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = \pi \times 0.02 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} = 5.5922 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 6: Final calculation**

$$Re = \frac{0.096}{5.5922 \times 10^{-5}} = 1717$$

**Step 7: Classification**

Since $Re = 1717 < 2000$, the flow is **Laminar (Streamline Flow)**.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the three different flow regimes observed in the Reynold's experiment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three flow regimes are:

**1. Laminar (Streamline) Flow — Re < 2000**
- The fluid moves in **parallel, non-intersecting layers**.
- The injected dye travels as a **distinct, straight, unbroken filament** along the tube axis.
- Viscous forces dominate; fluid particles move in an orderly fashion.
- Velocity at any cross-section follows a **parabolic profile** (Hagen-Poiseuille profile).

**2. Transitional Flow — 2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000**
- The dye filament begins to **oscillate and waver** laterally.
- The flow is **unstable** — minor disturbances cause fluctuations.
- Viscous and inertial forces are of **comparable magnitude**.
- Predicting exact behaviour is difficult; it can switch between laminar and turbulent.

**3. Turbulent Flow — Re > 4000**
- The dye **disperses rapidly and mixes completely** with the bulk fluid.
- Fluid particles move in **chaotic, irregular eddies** with cross-stream mixing.
- Inertial forces dominate; viscous effects are confined to a thin boundary layer.
- Velocity profile is **much flatter** than the parabolic laminar profile.

**(b) A fluid of density 850 kg/m³ and viscosity $1.5 \times 10^{-2}$ Pa·s flows through a pipe of 25 mm diameter at a velocity of 2 m/s. Determine Re and state the flow type. (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 850 \, kg/m^3$
- $\mu = 1.5 \times 10^{-2} \, Pa \cdot s$
- $D = 25 \, mm = 0.025 \, m$
- $v = 2 \, m/s$

**Step 1: Apply Re formula**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$Re = \frac{850 \times 2 \times 0.025}{1.5 \times 10^{-2}}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 850 \times 2 \times 0.025 = 42.5$$

**Step 4: Final calculation**

$$Re = \frac{42.5}{0.015} = 2833.33$$

**Step 5: Classification**

Since $2000 \leq Re \leq 4000$, the flow is **Transitional Flow**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diameter unit conversion** (mm → m) — costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Using radius $r$ instead of diameter $D$** as the characteristic length — strict examiner penalty.
> 3. **Omitting the final flow regime classification** — the statement "therefore the flow is laminar/turbulent" is mandatory for full marks.
> 4. **Swapping numerator and denominator** in the Re formula — a fatal arithmetic error worth 0 marks in the final calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting to state units of all measured quantities** at the start of numerical problems — partial mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ — dimensionless.
- **Experimenter:** Osborne Reynolds (1883).
- **Dye Used:** Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) or methylene blue.
- **Critical Values (pipe flow):** Laminar < 2000; Transitional 2000–4000; Turbulent > 4000.
- **Working formula in lab:** $Re = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu}$, where $Q = V/t$.
- **Characteristic length for pipe flow:** Diameter $D$ (not radius).
- **Practical implication:** Higher temperature → lower viscosity → higher Re → promotes turbulence.
- **Food industry relevance:** Used in pump sizing, CIP cleaning validation, heat exchanger design, and pasteurisation pipe networks.
- **Mnemonic:** "**Reynolds Ratio = Inertial / Viscous**" — numerator is $\rho v D$, denominator is $\mu$.
- **Always classify** the flow regime at the end of every numerical problem.
- **Units check:** $Re$ must be dimensionless — verify units cancel out in $\dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$:
  $\dfrac{(kg/m^3) \cdot (m/s) \cdot (m)}{Pa \cdot s} = \dfrac{kg/(m \cdot s)}{kg/(m \cdot s)} = 1$ ✓.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of the Rate of Flow of Fluid Using an Orifice Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of the Rate of Flow of Fluid Using an Orifice Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orifice Meter (Orifice Plate Flowmeter):** A classical **differential pressure type flow measuring device** used in the food processing industry to determine the rate of flow of an incompressible fluid (such as water, milk, fruit juice, edible oils, and sugar syrups) through a pipe. It operates by introducing a thin, flat, concentric circular plate with a precisely machined hole (the **orifice**) at the centre, which causes a localized constriction in the flow area, generating a measurable **pressure drop** that is directly related to the volumetric flow rate.

The standard terminology used in KTU laboratory manuals and process instrumentation texts:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Orifice Plate** | The thin metallic disc with a sharp-edged circular hole |
| **Upstream Tap (D/2 tap)** | Pressure tap located 1 pipe diameter before the orifice |
| **Downstream Tap (D/2 tap)** | Pressure tap located 1/2 pipe diameter after the orifice |
| **Differential Manometer** | U-tube manometer (mercury or water) to read pressure drop |
| **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** | Empirical correction factor accounting for vena contracta losses |
| **Vena Contracta** | Point of minimum cross-section of the jet, just downstream of the orifice |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Garden-Hose Analogy:** Imagine you are watering your garden by partially blocking the open end of a hose pipe with your thumb. The water exits at a much higher velocity, but the pressure inside the hose (just behind your thumb) drops significantly. By placing a small pressure gauge on either side of your thumb, you can relate the **pressure difference** to the **flow rate**. The orifice meter does exactly this — except instead of a thumb, it uses a precisely engineered sharp-edged plate, and instead of feeling the pressure by hand, it uses a calibrated manometer.

**A more relatable food-industry analogy:** When milk is forced through a narrow restriction in a homogenizer, the pressure upstream is high and downstream is low. The same pressure-flow relationship is exploited by the orifice meter.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and units are essential for KTU viva-voce and record-book entries:
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (Standard value used unless otherwise specified in the lab)
> - **Density of water at 25 °C:** $\rho_w = 1000 \; kg/m^3$
> - **Density of mercury (Hg) at 25 °C:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; kg/m^3$
> - **SI unit of volumetric flow rate:** $m^3/s$ (commonly expressed in **LPM** — Litres Per Minute in the lab)
> - **SI unit of pressure drop:** Pascal $(Pa)$ or metres of fluid head $(m)$

---

### 1.4 Why an Orifice Meter in a Food Technology Lab?

In food processing plants, the orifice meter is one of the **simplest, cheapest, and most robust** flow measuring instruments. It has no moving parts, is easy to sterilize (sanitary-grade orifice plates are used in dairy plants), and gives a reliable measurement of milk, juice, brewing wort, edible oil, and cleaning-in-place (CIP) solution flow rates. Understanding its working principle is therefore a **core competency** for any food technologist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The orifice meter works on the **principle of Bernoulli's equation** combined with the **continuity equation**, both of which are consequences of conservation of energy and mass for an incompressible, steady, frictionless flow.

The logical sequence is:

1. **Undisturbed flow** enters the pipe with cross-sectional area $A_1$ and velocity $V_1$.
2. The orifice plate forces the fluid to converge through a smaller opening of area $A_2$ (or $a$).
3. Due to the **continuity equation** ($A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$), the velocity must increase: $V_2 > V_1$.
4. Due to **Bernoulli's equation**, this kinetic-energy increase is compensated by a **decrease in static pressure**, producing a measurable pressure drop $P_1 - P_2$.
5. The fluid jet continues to contract for a short distance beyond the orifice to a minimum area $A_c$ (the **vena contracta**), then re-expands to fill the pipe.
6. A **differential manometer** reads the head difference $h$ (in metres of the manometer fluid).
7. Applying Bernoulli's equation between the upstream section (1) and the vena contracta (c) yields a **theoretical velocity**, which is then corrected by a dimensionless **coefficient of discharge $C_d$** to obtain the **actual flow rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind $C_d$:** Bernoulli's equation is derived assuming an **ideal, inviscid, frictionless** fluid. In reality, viscous losses, turbulence, and the contraction of the jet make the actual flow rate lower than the theoretical prediction. The empirical factor $C_d$ bundles all these real-world losses into a single correction factor, typically $C_d \approx 0.60 - 0.65$ for a standard sharp-edged orifice.

---

### 2.2 Constructional Features of the Orifice Plate
