A standard sharp-edged orifice plate consists of:

- A **flat, circular metallic disc** (stainless steel in food-grade applications).
- A **concentric circular opening** with a sharp, square upstream edge (the **knife-edge**).
- The downstream edge is slightly chamfered to prevent damage.
- **Pressure tap holes** drilled into the pipe flange, either at:
  * **Flange taps** — 1 inch upstream and 1 inch downstream of the plate.
  * **D-tap and D/2-tap** — 1 pipe diameter upstream and 1/2 pipe diameter downstream.
  * **Corner taps** — exactly at the plate surfaces.
- A **holder ring** (carrier ring) to clamp the plate between pipe flanges.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this entire table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge (ideal) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | $m^3/s$ | Bernoulli + continuity result |
| 2 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | $m^3/s$ | Final working equation |
| 3 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless | Determined experimentally |
| 4 | Manometer head difference (in metres of flowing fluid) | $h = \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right) x$ | $m$ | $x$ = manometer deflection in m |
| 5 | Differential pressure | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot x$ | $Pa$ | Used in electronic transmitters |
| 6 | Reynolds number at orifice | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Determines flow regime |
| 7 | Diameter ratio | $\beta = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1}$ | dimensionless | $\beta$ must be in $(0.2, 0.75)$ for $C_d$ validity |
| 8 | Volumetric flow in LPM | $Q_{LPM} = Q_{m^3/s} \times 60 \times 1000$ | $L/min$ | For lab-record conversion |

> **Note on the manometer formula:** The differential U-tube manometer (typically mercury-water) gives a deflection $x$ in metres. The equivalent head $h$ of the flowing fluid is **NOT** equal to $x$ — it must be multiplied by the factor $\left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right)$ because the manometer fluid (mercury) is denser than the flowing fluid (water).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** Sanitary orifice meters measure the flow of raw and processed milk, cream, and whey.
- **Beverage Industry:** Used to monitor flow of fruit juices, beer, and carbonated soft drinks in processing lines.
- **Edible Oil Refining:** Quantifies the throughput of crude and refined oil.
- **CIP (Clean-In-Place) Systems:** Ensures the correct volume of cleaning solution is circulated.
- **Sugar Industry:** Measures flow of cane juice, syrup, and molasses.
- **Water Treatment:** Monitors the inlet/outlet water flow in food-plant effluent treatment.

> **Engineering Insight:** Although modern plants prefer Coriolis, magnetic, or ultrasonic flowmeters for high-accuracy work, the orifice meter remains the **workhorse of process industries** because it is **calibration-stable, inexpensive, easy to replace, and works for any clean fluid** — making it a mandatory topic in any food-engineering curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Orifice Meter Discharge Equation

Let the following points be defined in the flow:

- **Section 1-1:** Upstream of the orifice plate, inside the pipe.
- **Section 2-2:** At the plane of the orifice (theoretical minimum area).
- **Section c-c:** At the **vena contracta** (actual minimum area of the jet).

Given quantities:

- $A_1$ = cross-sectional area of the pipe at section 1-1
- $A_2$ = cross-sectional area of the orifice
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area at the vena contracta
- $V_1, V_2, V_c$ = average fluid velocities at sections 1-1, 2-2, and c-c
- $P_1, P_2, P_c$ = static pressures at the three sections
- $h$ = differential head across the orifice (in metres of the flowing fluid)
- $C_c = \dfrac{A_c}{A_2}$ = **coefficient of contraction** (typically $C_c \approx 0.62$ for sharp-edged orifice)
- $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ = **coefficient of discharge**, where $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient accounting for viscous losses.

---

**Step 1: Apply the Continuity Equation**

For an incompressible fluid flowing steadily:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = A_c V_c
$$

This means as the area decreases, the velocity must increase.

---

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Section 1-1 and Section c-c**

Assuming an ideal, frictionless, incompressible flow along a streamline:

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_c}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_c^2}{2g} + z_c
$$

For a horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_c$, so the elevation terms cancel:

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} = \dfrac{P_c}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_c^2}{2g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{P_1 - P_c}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V_c^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Define the differential head:

$$
h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_c}{\rho g}
$$

Therefore:

$$
h = \dfrac{V_c^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

---

**Step 3: Use the Continuity Equation to Eliminate $V_1$**

From continuity:

$$
V_1 = \dfrac{A_c V_c}{A_1}
$$

Substituting into the energy equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 g h &= V_c^2 - \left( \dfrac{A_c V_c}{A_1} \right)^2 \\
2 g h &= V_c^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{A_c}{A_1} \right)^2 \right] \\
V_c^2 &= \dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_c / A_1)^2} \\
V_c &= \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_c / A_1)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Express the Theoretical Discharge**

The theoretical volumetric flow rate is the velocity at the vena contracta multiplied by its area:

$$
Q_{th} = A_c V_c = A_c \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_c / A_1)^2}}
$$

---

**Step 5: Introduce the Coefficient of Contraction $C_c$**

By definition:

$$
C_c = \dfrac{A_c}{A_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_c = C_c A_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{th} = C_c A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (C_c A_2 / A_1)^2}}
$$

Since $C_c$ also appears inside the square root, it is convenient to define a **single empirical coefficient $C_d$** that absorbs both $C_c$ and the velocity coefficient $C_v$:

$$
C_d = C_c \cdot C_v
$$

---

**Step 6: Final Working Equation for Actual Discharge**

$$
Q_{act} = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2 / A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

This is the **standard KTU equation** that must be used in all numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Question Type)

> **Problem Statement:**
> In a food-processing lab, an orifice meter with a sharp-edged concentric orifice of diameter $d_2 = 25 \; mm$ is fitted in a horizontal pipe of diameter $d_1 = 75 \; mm$ carrying water. A differential mercury-water U-tube manometer connected across the orifice shows a deflection of $x = 0.20 \; m$. The coefficient of discharge is $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate:
> 1. The differential head $h$ in metres of water.
> 2. The actual volumetric flow rate in $m^3/s$ and in **LPM** (litres per minute).

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1 &= 75 \; mm = 0.075 \; m \\
d_2 &= 25 \; mm = 0.025 \; m \\
x &= 0.20 \; m \\
C_d &= 0.62 \\
\rho_w &= 1000 \; kg/m^3 \\
\rho_{Hg} &= 13600 \; kg/m^3 \\
g &= 9.81 \; m/s^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 1: Calculate the Pipe and Orifice Areas**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2 \\
A_2 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \; m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2: Compute the Area Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} &= \left( \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{0.025}{0.075} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{1}{3} \right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{9} \\
\dfrac{A_2^2}{A_1^2} &= \dfrac{1}{81} = 0.01235
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Convert Manometer Reading to Differential Head $h$ in Metres of Water**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \left( \dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) x \\
h &= \left( \dfrac{13600}{1000} - 1 \right) \times 0.20 \\
h &= (13.6 - 1) \times 0.20 \\
h &= 12.6 \times 0.20 \\
h &= 2.52 \; m \text{ of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Compute the Velocity Term $\sqrt{2 g h}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{2 g h} &= \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} \\
&= \sqrt{49.4424} \\
&= 7.031 \; m/s
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Plug Into the Discharge Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h} \\
Q_{act} &= \dfrac{0.62 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.01235}} \times 7.031 \\
Q_{act} &= \dfrac{3.044 \times 10^{-4}}{0.9938} \times 7.031 \\
Q_{act} &= (3.063 \times 10^{-4}) \times 7.031 \\
Q_{act} &= 2.154 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 6: Convert to Litres Per Minute (LPM)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{LPM} &= Q_{m^3/s} \times 1000 \times 60 \\
Q_{LPM} &= 2.154 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 60 \\
Q_{LPM} &= 129.2 \; L/min
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Final Answer:**

- Differential head $h = 2.52 \; m$ of water
- Volumetric flow rate $Q = 2.154 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s = 129.2 \; LPM$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Orifice vs Other Meters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a common viva-voce and 14-mark question. **Memorize the trade-offs.**

| Feature | Orifice Meter | Venturi Meter | Rotameter | Pitot Tube |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure loss (permanent) | **High** (5–15 % of $\Delta P$) | **Low** (≈ 10 % of orifice) | **Negligible** | **Negligible** |
| Initial cost | **Low** | **High** (machined throat) | **Moderate** | **Low** |
| Accuracy | ± 1–2 % | ± 1 % | ± 2 % | ± 1–3 % |
| Space requirement | **Compact** | **Long** (3–4 pipe D) | **Compact** | **Compact** |
| Best suited for | Clean fluids, large pipelines | Large flow rates, low-loss | Local indication, transparent fluids | Point velocity measurement |
| Food-industry use | High (milk, juice lines) | Moderate (HTST pasteurizer feed) | Low (small batch) | Low (mostly lab use) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional flow** of the orifice-meter apparatus as assembled in a typical KTU food-technology laboratory. The diagram maps the major hardware components, fluid path, and measurement points to give a clear picture of how the experiment is wired up.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Overshead Tank] --> A2[Inlet Valve]
    A2 --> A3[Straight Calm Section 1.5m]
    A3 --> A4[Upstream Pressure Tap D-tap]
    A4 --> A5[Orifice Plate Assembly]
    A5 --> A6[Downstream Pressure Tap D by 2]
    A6 --> A7[Straight Calm Section 1m]
    A7 --> A8[Outlet Control Valve]
    A8 --> A9[Collection Tank with Volumetric Marking]
    A4 -. Hg-Water Manometer Limb L1 .-> B1[U Tube Manometer]
    A6 -. Hg-Water Manometer Limb L2 .-> B1
    B1 --> B2[Manometer Scale cm]
    A9 --> A3[Stopwatch and Measuring Cylinder]
    A3 --> A10[Data Tabulation Sheet]
    B2 --> A10
```

---

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Fluid Path and Measurement Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Experiment] --> P2[Open Inlet Valve Fully]
    P2 --> P3[Allow Steady Flow to Establish]
    P3 --> P4[Note Manometer Reading x in cm]
    P4 --> P5[Collect Water in Measuring Cylinder for Time t]
    P5 --> P6[Record Volume V in Litres]
    P6 --> P7{Vary Outlet Valve Position}
    P7 --> P8[Set 1 Small Opening]
    P7 --> P9[Set 2 Half Opening]
    P7 --> P10[Set 3 Full Opening]
    P8 --> P11[Record x V t for Set 1]
    P9 --> P12[Record x V t for Set 2]
    P10 --> P13[Record x V t for Set 3]
    P11 --> P14[Calculate Cd for Each Set]
    P12 --> P14
    P13 --> P14
    P14 --> P15[Calculate Mean Cd]
    P15 --> P16[Plot Calibration Curve Q vs sqrt h]
    P16 --> P17[End Experiment]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Description of the Orifice Plate Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Pipe of diameter D1] --> S2[Flange Joint 1]
    S2 --> S3[Orifice Plate with hole d2]
    S3 --> S4[Flange Joint 2]
    S4 --> S5[Pipe continues diameter D1]
    S3 --> S6[Sharp Upstream Edge]
    S3 --> S7[Chamfered Downstream Edge]
    S3 --> S8[Upstream Tap D]
    S3 --> S9[Downstream Tap D by 2]
```

> **Note to the student:** In your record book, you should draw a **neat, labelled cross-sectional view** of the orifice plate showing:
> - The pipe wall (hatched)
> - The orifice plate clamped between two flanges
> - The sharp upstream edge of the orifice
> - The two pressure-tap locations
> - The vena contracta (a dashed line showing the jet's minimum area)
> - The re-expanded jet downstream
> - The differential manometer connected to the two taps

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
> *Define the coefficient of discharge of an orifice meter. What is its typical range for a sharp-edged concentric orifice?*

**Model Answer:**

> The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of an orifice meter is defined as the **ratio of the actual volumetric flow rate to the theoretical (ideal) flow rate** computed from Bernoulli's equation. Mathematically,
> $$ C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} $$
> It is a dimensionless empirical correction factor that accounts for the contraction of the jet (vena contracta) and the viscous/turbulent energy losses. For a **sharp-edged concentric orifice plate**, the typical value of $C_d$ lies in the range **0.60 to 0.65**, with $C_d \approx 0.62$ being a commonly used average for water at moderate Reynolds numbers.

---

> **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
> *What is vena contracta? Why does it form downstream of an orifice?*

**Model Answer:**

> The **vena contracta** is the location along the jet, just downstream of the orifice, where the cross-sectional area of the flowing stream reaches its **minimum value** before re-expanding to fill the pipe. It forms because the fluid streamlines, upon approaching the orifice, **cannot make a sharp 90° turn** to enter the smaller opening abruptly. Instead, they continue to converge in a curved path for a short distance beyond the plate, reaching the minimum cross-section at the vena contracta. The ratio of the vena contracta area to the orifice area is the **coefficient of contraction $C_c$**, typically about **0.62** for a sharp-edged orifice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Question B — Choice A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]:**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle** of an orifice meter used in food processing industries. **\[7 Marks\]**
> **(b)** Derive the **equation for the actual discharge** through an orifice meter, clearly stating all assumptions. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working:**

The orifice meter assembly consists of:

1. **Orifice plate** — a flat circular stainless-steel disc with a sharp-edged concentric circular hole (the **orifice**) of diameter $d_2$, mounted between two pipe flanges. The upstream edge of the hole is machined to a **sharp square edge** to ensure a reproducible vena contracta; the downstream edge is slightly chamfered.

2. **Pressure tappings** — two small holes drilled into the pipe wall, one located at a distance of **1 pipe diameter ($D$)** upstream of the orifice and the other at **$D/2$** downstream. These connect the pipe interior to the differential manometer. (Other standard tap locations — flange, corner — are also acceptable.)

3. **Differential manometer** — a U-tube manometer containing mercury (for water flow) or carbon tetrachloride (for air flow) connects the two pressure taps. The manometer deflection $x$ is read on a graduated scale.

4. **Supporting pipework** — straight calming sections of length **$5D$ to $10D$** upstream and downstream of the orifice to ensure a fully developed velocity profile.

**Working:** As the fluid flows through the pipe, it is forced to converge through the smaller orifice area. By continuity, velocity increases; by Bernoulli's principle, pressure drops. The differential manometer records this pressure drop, which is uniquely related to the flow rate via the discharge equation.

> **Diagram (must be drawn in the answer book):** A neat, labelled line diagram of the orifice plate, pipe, two pressure taps (D and D/2 locations), and the U-tube manometer.

**(b) Derivation of the Discharge Equation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1 of these notes** for the complete derivation. The key steps are:

1. Apply **continuity equation**: $A_1 V_1 = A_c V_c$.
2. Apply **Bernoulli's equation** between the upstream section and the vena contracta.
3. Eliminate $V_1$ using continuity to obtain the theoretical velocity $V_c$.
4. Multiply by the vena contracta area $A_c$ to get the theoretical discharge.
5. Introduce the **coefficient of discharge $C_d$** to obtain the final working equation:

$$
Q_{act} = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2 / A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Assumptions clearly stated in the answer:**

- The fluid is **incompressible** and of constant density.
- The flow is **steady**, **uniform**, and **horizontal**.
- The fluid is **ideal** (Bernoulli applies) — actual losses are absorbed into $C_d$.
- The pressure taps are correctly positioned to capture the static pressure.
- The manometer fluid is **immiscible** with the flowing fluid and the manometer limbs are of equal diameter (so no correction for meniscus asymmetry is needed).

---

> **Question B — Choice B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]:**
> **(a)** A horizontal pipe of diameter **$D_1 = 100 \; mm$** carrying water is fitted with a sharp-edged orifice of diameter **$D_2 = 50 \; mm$**. A differential mercury manometer reads a deflection of **$x = 100 \; mm$**. If the coefficient of discharge is **$C_d = 0.62$**, calculate the **actual flow rate in LPM** and the **differential head in metres of water**. **\[7 Marks\]**
> **(b)** Describe the **experimental procedure** to determine the coefficient of discharge of an orifice meter in the food technology lab. List **five sources of error**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $D_1 = 0.100 \; m$, $D_2 = 0.050 \; m$, $x = 0.100 \; m$, $C_d = 0.62$, $\rho_w = 1000 \; kg/m^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$.

**Step 1 — Differential head in metres of water:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \left( \dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) x \\
h &= (13.6 - 1)(0.100) = 1.26 \; m \text{ of water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2 \\
A_2 &= \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity term:**

$$
\sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26} = \sqrt{24.7212} = 4.972 \; m/s
$$

**Step 4 — Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= \dfrac{0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1 - (0.5)^4}} \times 4.972 \\
&= \dfrac{1.217 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} \times 4.972 \\
&= \dfrac{1.217 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{0.9375}} \times 4.972 \\
&= \dfrac{1.217 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9683} \times 4.972 \\
&= (1.257 \times 10^{-3}) \times 4.972 \\
&= 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to LPM:**

$$
Q_{LPM} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 60 = 375 \; L/min
$$

**Final Answers:** $h = 1.26 \; m$ of water; $Q = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s = 375 \; LPM$.

**(b) Experimental Procedure:**

1. **Apparatus setup:** Connect the overhead tank to the test pipe fitted with the orifice plate. Ensure the pipe is horizontal and the manometer is properly primed with mercury.
2. **Leak check:** Run water through the system for a few minutes; verify there are no leaks at the flanges, manometer, or joints.
3. **Steady-state condition:** Open the inlet valve fully and partially close the outlet valve to set a **steady flow rate**.
4. **Manometer reading:** Once the manometer levels stabilise, read the deflection $x$ in cm from the scale.
5. **Volumetric collection:** Simultaneously start a stopwatch and collect water at the outlet in a **measuring cylinder** for a fixed time $t$ (typically 30–60 s).
6. **Volume measurement:** Record the collected volume $V$ in litres.
7. **Repeat for different flow rates:** Vary the outlet valve to obtain **at least 5–6 different flow rates**, repeating the $x, V, t$ measurement for each.
8. **Calculation of $C_d$:** For each setting, compute the **actual flow rate** $Q_{act} = V / t$ and the **theoretical flow rate** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$, where $h$ is computed from $x$. The coefficient of discharge is $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$.
9. **Report the mean $C_d$** and plot a calibration graph of $Q$ vs $\sqrt{h}$, which should be a straight line passing through the origin.

**Five Sources of Error:**

1. **Parallax error** while reading the mercury meniscus in the manometer.
2. **Fluctuations in the manometer** due to unsteady flow caused by pump surges or partially closed valves.
3. **Air bubbles trapped** in the manometer tubing, causing false readings.
4. **Inaccurate volume or time measurement** in the volumetric collection.
5. **Improper tap location** or manufacturing defect in the orifice plate (non-sharp edge, eccentricity).
6. (Bonus) Temperature variation affecting fluid density, and fouling of the orifice edge.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in the university exam:**
> 1. **Manometer-head conversion (–2 marks):** Students frequently substitute $h = x$ directly into the discharge equation, forgetting that the manometer reads the differential pressure in terms of the *manometer fluid* (mercury), not the *flowing fluid* (water). Always convert using $h = \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right) x$.
> 2. **Confusion between $C_c$, $C_v$, and $C_d$ (–1 mark):** Clearly define each term. $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ — students often write this relation incorrectly.
> 3. **Missing assumptions in derivation (–2 marks):** A derivation without the explicit statement *"fluid is incompressible, steady, and the flow is horizontal"* is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Unit-conversion mistakes (–1 mark):** Forgetting to convert mm to m, or forgetting to multiply by 60 × 1000 to convert $m^3/s$ to LPM.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark descriptive questions (–1 to –2 marks):** A neat, labelled diagram of the orifice plate and manometer is **mandatory**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must be memorized before the exam):**

- **Orifice meter** = differential-pressure flowmeter using a thin sharp-edged plate.
- **Principle:** Bernoulli + continuity.
- **Vena contracta** = minimum area of jet, located at $C_c \approx 0.62$ of the orifice.
- **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d \approx 0.60$ – $0.65$; computed as $Q_{act} / Q_{th}$.
- **Master equation:** $\;Q = \dfrac{C_d \, A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}\;$
- **Manometer-to-head conversion:** $\;h = \left( \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1 \right) x\;$ — never use $h = x$ directly.
- **Standard pressure tap locations:** Flange, D and D/2, or corner.
- **Permanent pressure loss** of an orifice meter is **10 – 15 %** of the differential pressure (one of its main disadvantages).
- **Diameter ratio** $\beta = d_2 / d_1$ should be in the range **0.2 – 0.75** for the standard $C_d$ correlations to apply.
- **For a horizontal pipe**, elevation terms in Bernoulli's equation cancel; for an inclined pipe, retain the $z$ terms.
- **Calibration graph** of $Q$ vs $\sqrt{h}$ is a **straight line through the origin** — a useful sanity check in the lab.
- **Food-industry relevance:** Used for milk, juice, beer, edible oil, and CIP solution flow monitoring.
- **Always mention assumptions** in any derivation question: incompressible, steady, uniform, inviscid (losses bundled into $C_d$).
- **Units to memorise:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $\rho_w = 1000 \; kg/m^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \; kg/m^3$, 1 LPM $= 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3/s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of the Rate of Flow of Fluid Using a Venturi Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of the Rate of Flow of Fluid Using a Venturi Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Venturi Meter** is a precision-built differential pressure flow-measuring device used to determine the rate of fluid flow (liquid or gas) flowing through a closed conduit (pipe). It operates on the principle of the **Venturi Effect**, where a constriction in the flow path causes a localized drop in static pressure that is directly proportional to the fluid's velocity.

Geometrically, it consists of three distinct sections:
1. **Converging (Inlet) Cone** — gradually narrows from the full pipe diameter to the throat.
2. **Throat** — the narrowest cross-section of the meter (constant diameter over a small length).
3. **Diverging (Outlet) Cone** — gradually expands back to the original pipe diameter to recover pressure.

Tappings (piezometer or differential manometer connections) are placed at the inlet and the throat to capture the pressure difference $(\Delta P = P_1 - P_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For GDFTL106, the Venturi meter experiment tests two key Course Outcomes — the student's ability to *operate fluid-flow measurement instruments* and to *compute discharge (Q) using the continuity equation, Bernoulli's equation, and the coefficient of discharge (Cd)*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a wide highway that suddenly narrows to a single-lane bridge.

- To keep the **same volume of cars passing per second**, every car must **speed up** through the narrow bridge.
- The faster the cars move, the more the air pressure on the side windows drops (you can almost feel your ears "pop").

Now apply the same idea to a fluid (milk, water, fruit juice, oil) in a pipe:
- **Wide part of pipe** → fluid moves slowly → static pressure is **high**.
- **Narrow throat** → fluid must speed up → static pressure is **low**.
- The **difference in pressure** between these two points tells us *how fast* the fluid is moving — and therefore, **how much fluid is flowing per second (the discharge, Q).**

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the Venturi meter as a "speedometer" for fluids. It does not measure speed directly; it infers speed from a pressure drop, just like a pilot tube, but with **far less energy loss** because the diverging section gradually recovers the pressure.

## 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics (Bolded)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **g** | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s²** |
| **ρ** | Density of fluid | **kg/m³** (water ≈ 1000 kg/m³) |
| **A₁, A₂** | Cross-sectional areas at inlet and throat | **m²** |
| **d₁, d₂** | Diameters at inlet and throat | **m** |
| **h** | Differential pressure head | **m of fluid** |
| **Cd** | Coefficient of discharge (Venturi) | **0.95 to 0.99** (dimensionless) |
| **Q** | Volumetric flow rate (discharge) | **m³/s** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Physical Principles

The Venturi meter derivation rests on **three sacred pillars** of fluid mechanics. Every KTU examiner expects you to name them before writing the formula:

### Pillar 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an incompressible fluid in a steady, streamlined flow:
$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

This means the mass entering per second equals the mass leaving per second.

### Pillar 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
For an ideal (frictionless, incompressible) fluid:
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Each term represents a **head** (energy per unit weight):
- $P/\rho g$ = **Pressure head**
- $V^2/2g$ = **Velocity head (kinetic energy)**
- $z$ = **Elevation head (potential energy)**

### Pillar 3 — Discharge Coefficient (Reality Check)
In the real world, friction and turbulence cause energy losses. We patch this by introducing:
$$Q_{actual} = C_d \times Q_{theoretical}$$

Where $C_d$ (typically **0.95–0.99** for a Venturi) is determined experimentally.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Why is there a converging section?**
- A sudden contraction would cause flow separation, eddies, and large friction losses.
- A *gradual* converging cone (angle ≈ 20°–30°) keeps the streamlines smooth, ensuring **laminar/turbulent-but-attached** flow.

**Why is there a diverging section?**
- A sudden expansion back to pipe diameter would cause the fluid to "detach" and waste 80% of the recovered pressure.
- A *gradual* diverging cone (angle ≈ 5°–7°) allows the kinetic energy gained at the throat to be **reconverted into pressure energy**, recovering up to 90% of the pressure drop — a phenomenon called **pressure recovery**.

**Why is the Venturi meter preferred over an orifice meter in food industries?**
- Lower permanent pressure loss → **less pumping energy cost**.
- No sharp edges → **no stagnation of viscous food fluids** (sauces, syrups, dairy).
- Self-cleaning geometry → **hygienic** for food processing (CIP-friendly).
- Higher accuracy and higher $C_d$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim. Every KTU board problem on this experiment revolves around these five equations.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | m³/s | Incompressible flow |
| 2 | Bernoulli (horizontal) | $\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$ | m of fluid | $z_1 = z_2$ for horizontal pipe |
| 3 | Differential Head | $h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$ | m | Read from manometer |
| 4 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | m³/s | Substitute $V_2$ from Bernoulli |
| 5 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | m³/s | **This is the final answer** |
| 6 | Area Relation | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | m² | Use when diameters are given |
| 7 | Manometer Head (mercury) | $h = \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_f} - 1\right) x$ | m of fluid | $x$ = manometer reading |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Food & Allied Engineering

- **Dairy plants:** Measuring flow of milk, cream, and whey through pasteurizers.
- **Beverage industry:** Monitoring juice, beer, and soft-drink flow on bottling lines.
- **Sugar refineries:** Quantifying syrup and molasses flow.
- **Bioprocess plants:** Sanitary measurement of broth and fermentation media (Venturi is **CIP-compatible**).
- **Water treatment:** Dosing chemicals at precise flow rates.
- **Oil & fats:** Edible oil pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Venturi Meter Discharge Equation

We derive the discharge formula by combining Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation, then introduce $C_d$ to account for real-world losses.

### STEP 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Inlet (1) and Throat (2)

The general form is:
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

### STEP 2 — Assume Horizontal Installation (The KTU Standard Case)

Most lab Venturi meters are mounted horizontally, so $z_1 = z_2$. The $z$ terms cancel out:
$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

### STEP 3 — Rearrange to Isolate the Pressure Head Difference

Move the velocity terms to one side and the pressure terms to the other:
$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

Define the differential pressure head as $h$:
$$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

### STEP 4 — Apply the Continuity Equation to Eliminate $V_1$

From $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, solve for $V_1$:
$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

### STEP 5 — Substitute $V_1$ Into the Bernoulli Result

Replace $V_1$ in the equation from Step 3:
$$h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}$$

### STEP 6 — Factor Out $V_2^2$

$$h = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$

### STEP 7 — Solve for $V_2$ (Throat Velocity)

Multiply both sides by $2g$:
$$2gh = V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

Divide both sides by the bracket:
$$V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}$$

Take the square root:
$$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

### STEP 8 — Compute the Theoretical Discharge

Discharge is area × velocity at the throat:
$$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

### STEP 9 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge (Reality Correction)

Real fluids experience friction and turbulence, so the actual discharge is **less** than theoretical. We multiply by $C_d$:
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master equation.** Every KTU 14-mark question on this experiment ends with this expression. Learn it as a single block.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice)

**Given:**
- Inlet diameter $d_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Throat diameter $d_2 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Differential manometer reading $h = 0.20 \text{ m of water}$
- Coefficient of discharge $C_d = 0.98$

**Find:** Actual discharge $Q_{act}$ in L/s.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional areas.**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the area ratio squared.**
$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{0.025}{0.05}\right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator term.**
$$1 - (A_2/A_1)^2 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$
$$\sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9682$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator term.**
$$\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20} = \sqrt{3.924} = 1.981 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute theoretical discharge.**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.981}{0.9682}$$
$$Q_{th} = 1.0042 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the coefficient of discharge.**
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 1.0042 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$Q_{act} = 9.841 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to L/s.**
$$Q_{act} = 9.841 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.9841 \text{ L/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The fluid is flowing at approximately **0.984 L/s** through the Venturi meter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Venturi Meter Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of fluid, energy, and measurement signals through a Venturi meter. This is the **safe, render-friendly substitute** for a physical cross-sectional drawing, designed to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, no markdown inside quoted labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start of Experiment]) --> pipeInletNode[/Pipe Inlet Section/]
    pipeInletNode --> measureInletNode[/Measure Diameter D1 at Inlet/]
    measureInletNode --> convergingConeNode[Converging Cone Section]
    convergingConeNode --> throatNode[Throat Section Narrowest Part]
    throatNode --> measureThroatNode[/Measure Diameter D2 at Throat/]
    measureThroatNode --> divergingConeNode[Diverging Cone Section]
    divergingConeNode --> pipeOutletNode[/Pipe Outlet Section/]
    pipeOutletNode --> endNode([Discharge Outlets into Collection Tank])

    tapInletNode[Tap 1 at Inlet] -.Pressure P1.-> manometerLeftNode
    tapThroatNode[Tap 2 at Throat] -.Pressure P2.-> manometerRightNode
    manometerLeftNode --> uTubeManometerNode[U Tube Differential Manometer]
    manometerRightNode --> uTubeManometerNode
    uTubeManometerNode --> readManometerNode[/Read Differential Head h/]

    measureInletNode --> calcArea1Node[Compute Area A1 equals pi by 4 times D1 squared]
    measureThroatNode --> calcArea2Node[Compute Area A2 equals pi by 4 times D2 squared]
    readManometerNode --> applyBernoulliNode[Apply Bernoulli Equation Between 1 and 2]
    applyBernoulliNode --> applyContinuityNode[Apply Continuity Equation]
    applyContinuityNode --> deriveV2Node[Derive Throat Velocity V2]
    calcArea2Node --> deriveV2Node
    deriveV2Node --> calcQthNode[Calculate Theoretical Discharge Qth]
    calcArea1Node --> calcQthNode
    readManometerNode --> calcQthNode
    calcQthNode --> applyCdNode[Multiply by Coefficient of Discharge Cd]
    applyCdNode --> finalQNode[Final Result Actual Discharge Qact in L per s]

    finalQNode --> plotGraphNode[Plot Qact versus sqrt h Calibration Curve]
    finalQNode --> reportNode[Tabulate Results and Compute Percentage Error]

    style startNode fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000
    style endNode fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000000
    style throatNode fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000000
    style finalQNode fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000000
    style plotGraphNode fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000000
    style reportNode fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Variables and Their Inter-Relations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[D1 Inlet Diameter] --> A1[A1 Inlet Area]
    D2[D2 Throat Diameter] --> A2[A2 Throat Area]
    A2 --> V2[V2 Throat Velocity]
    A1 --> V2
    A1 --> V1[V1 Inlet Velocity]
    A2 --> V1
    V2 --> DP[Pressure Drop P1 minus P2]
    V1 --> DP
    DP --> h[Differential Head h]
    h --> Qth[Theoretical Discharge]
    A2 --> Qth
    V2 --> Qth
    Qth --> Qact[Actual Discharge]
    Cd[Cd Coefficient of Discharge] --> Qact
    Qact --> Lps[Convert to L per s]
    Qact --> Plot[Qact versus sqrt h Graph]
    Plot --> Slope[Slope gives Cd experimentally]

    style V2 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000000
    style Qact fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    style Slope fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagram

- The **green nodes** (`startNode`, `endNode`) mark the start and end of the experiment.
- The **orange node** (`throatNode`) is the critical constriction — pressure is lowest here.
- The **blue node** (`finalQNode`) is the answer you report in the lab record.
- The **pink nodes** (`plotGraphNode`, `reportNode`) represent the post-experiment analysis steps.
- The **yellow node** (`V2`) is the variable that connects geometry to dynamics.
- The **red node** (`Slope`) is how $C_d$ is determined experimentally from the calibration plot.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Bernoulli's theorem and list the three assumptions under which it is valid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bernoulli's theorem states that *for an ideal, incompressible fluid undergoing steady, streamline flow, the total energy (sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head) at any point along a streamline remains constant.*

Mathematically:
$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Assumptions (1 mark each):**
1. Fluid is **ideal** (inviscid, no friction).
2. Flow is **steady** (velocity at a point does not change with time).
3. Fluid is **incompressible** (density is constant).
4. Flow is **along a streamline** (irrotational or along one streamline).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the coefficient of discharge (Cd) of a Venturi meter. What is its typical range?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The coefficient of discharge is defined as the **ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge** through a flow-measuring device:
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It is a **dimensionless number** that accounts for energy losses due to friction, turbulence, and flow separation that the ideal Bernoulli equation neglects.

**Typical range for a Venturi meter: $C_d = 0.95$ to $0.99$** (much higher than an orifice meter, which has $C_d \approx 0.60$–$0.65$).

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Both questions carry 7 + 7 = 14 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a Venturi meter. List **two advantages** of a Venturi meter over an orifice meter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A Venturi meter consists of three sections arranged in series inside a pipe:
1. **Converging (inlet) cone** — angle of convergence ≈ 20°–30°, gradually reducing pipe diameter $d_1$ to throat diameter $d_2$.
2. **Throat** — a short parallel section of minimum constant diameter $d_2$ where velocity is maximum and pressure is minimum.
3. **Diverging (outlet) cone** — angle of divergence ≈ 5°–7°, gradually enlarging back to original pipe diameter.

Two pressure tappings are provided — one at the inlet (before the cone) and one at the throat — connected to a U-tube differential manometer.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When fluid flows through the constriction, by the **continuity equation** the velocity increases. By **Bernoulli's equation**, this increase in velocity head must be compensated by a decrease in pressure head. The differential pressure head $h$ measured by the manometer is then used in the discharge formula to compute $Q_{act}$.

**Two Advantages over Orifice Meter (2 marks):**
1. **Higher $C_d$ (0.95–0.99 vs 0.6–0.65)** → more accurate measurement.
2. **Lower permanent pressure loss** → saves pumping energy.
3. **No sharp edges** → no stagnation of viscous food fluids, suitable for sanitary applications (CIP-friendly).
4. **Self-cleaning geometry** → ideal for food and dairy industries.

---

**Q. (b)** A Venturi meter has an inlet diameter of **150 mm** and a throat diameter of **75 mm**. It is fitted in a vertical pipe with flow upwards. The pressure at inlet is **120 kPa** and at the throat is **90 kPa**. The differential manometer uses mercury (Sg = 13.6) and reads **150 mm** of Hg. The fluid flowing is water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). Determine the **actual discharge** in **L/s** if $C_d = 0.98$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the cross-sectional areas.**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the differential head $h$ in metres of water.**
For a mercury manometer, the equivalent head of water is:
$$h = x \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.150 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.150 \times 12.6$$
$$h = 1.89 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the area ratio squared.**
$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{75}{150}\right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**
$$1 - (A_2/A_1)^2 = 0.9375$$
$$\sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9682$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator.**
$$\sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89} = \sqrt{37.08} = 6.089 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute theoretical discharge.**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \frac{4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.089}{0.9682}$$
$$Q_{th} = 2.779 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Compute actual discharge.**
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 2.779 \times 10^{-2} = 2.723 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to L/s.**
$$Q_{act} = 27.23 \text{ L/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{act} = 27.23 \text{ L/s}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the terms: **(i)** Coefficient of discharge, **(ii)** Coefficient of velocity, **(iii)** Coefficient of contraction. How are they related to each other for a Venturi meter? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Coefficient of discharge (Cd) (2 marks):**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$
It is the ratio of the actual mass/volume flow rate to the theoretical flow rate predicted by Bernoulli's equation. It accounts for **all** real-world losses (friction, turbulence, contraction).

**(ii) Coefficient of velocity (Cv) (2 marks):**
$$C_v = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$$
It is the ratio of the actual velocity at the vena contracta (or throat) to the theoretical velocity predicted by Bernoulli's equation. For a Venturi meter, $C_v \approx 0.98$–$0.99$.

**(iii) Coefficient of contraction (Cc) (1 mark):**
$$C_c = \frac{A_{actual}}{A_{theoretical}}$$
It is the ratio of the actual area of the jet (at the vena contracta) to the area of the orifice/throat. For a Venturi meter, the throat is a **machined geometry**, not a natural contraction, so $C_c \approx 1.0$ (or very close to 1).

**Relation (2 marks):**
The three coefficients are linked by:
$$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$
For a Venturi meter, since $C_c \approx 1.0$, the discharge coefficient $C_d$ is essentially equal to $C_v$. This is the *primary reason* why the Venturi meter's $C_d$ is so high (0.95–0.99) compared to an orifice meter (0.60–0.65), where the vena contracta is unpredictable.

---

**Q. (b)** A horizontal Venturi meter fitted in a pipeline carrying oil (specific gravity 0.8) has an inlet diameter of **200 mm** and a throat diameter of **100 mm**. The differential U-tube mercury manometer shows a deflection of **250 mm**. If the coefficient of discharge is **0.97**, calculate the **mass flow rate** of oil in **kg/s**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the cross-sectional areas.**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.200)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the differential head in metres of oil.**
The manometer fluid is mercury (Sg = 13.6), and the flowing fluid is oil (Sg = 0.8).
$$h_{oil} = x \left(\frac{S_{Hg}}{S_{oil}} - 1\right) = 0.250 \times \left(\frac{13.6}{0.8} - 1\right)$$
$$h_{oil} = 0.250 \times (17 - 1) = 0.250 \times 16 = 4.0 \text{ m of oil}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the area ratio squared.**
$$\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.100}{0.200}\right)^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**
$$\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9682$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator.**
$$\sqrt{2 g h} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 4.0} = \sqrt{78.48} = 8.859 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute actual volumetric discharge.**
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = 0.97 \times \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.859}{0.9682}$$
$$Q_{act} = 0.97 \times 7.188 \times 10^{-2} = 6.972 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the mass flow rate.**
Density of oil $\rho = 0.8 \times 1000 = 800 \text{ kg/m}^3$
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{act} = 800 \times 6.972 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$\dot{m} = 55.78 \text{ kg/s}$$

> **Final Answer:** Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 55.78 \text{ kg/s}$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students lose marks on Venturi meter questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the manometer fluid conversion** — When the manometer uses mercury and the flow fluid is water/oil, you MUST convert the manometer reading $x$ to an equivalent head of the *flowing fluid* using $h = x(S_m/S_f - 1)$. Skipping this gives 0 marks for the head.
> 2. **Mixing up inlet and throat diameters** — The throat is ALWAYS the smaller diameter. Check $d_2 < d_1$ before substituting.
> 3. **Using $C_d$ incorrectly** — $C_d$ multiplies the **theoretical** discharge, not the velocity. Don't write $V_{act} = C_d \cdot V_2$ unless the question is about a single coefficient.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Diameters must be in **metres** (not mm) before computing areas in m². $h$ must be in **metres of the flowing fluid** before substitution.
> 5. **Skipping Bernoulli and continuity citations** — Even in numerical problems, the KTU examiner awards 2 marks for *stating the two equations used*. Do not jump straight to the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Venturi Meter (Module Experiment 2)**

- ✅ A Venturi meter measures **volumetric flow rate (Q)** using the **pressure drop** between a wide section and a narrow throat.
- ✅ Construction: **Converging cone (20°–30°) → Throat → Diverging cone (5°–7°)**.
- ✅ Governing equations: **Bernoulli's equation** + **Continuity equation**.
- ✅ **Master formula:**
$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$
- ✅ **Typical $C_d$ for Venturi = 0.95 to 0.99** (much higher than orifice).
- ✅ **Manometer head conversion** (mercury–water): $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_{water}} - 1\right)$.
- ✅ **Manometer head conversion** (mercury–oil): $h = x \left(\dfrac{S_{Hg}}{S_{oil}} - 1\right)$.
- ✅ **Area in terms of diameter:** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$.
- ✅ **For horizontal pipe:** $z_1 = z_2$ (elevation heads cancel).
- ✅ **For vertical pipe:** include the $\pm z$ term in Bernoulli (sign depends on flow direction).
- ✅ **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{act}$.
- ✅ **Advantages over orifice meter:** higher $C_d$, lower pressure loss, hygienic, CIP-friendly — **essential for food technology labs**.
- ✅ **Plot $Q_{act}$ vs $\sqrt{h}$** — the slope of this straight line gives $C_d$ experimentally.
- ✅ **Permanent pressure loss** in a Venturi is only ~10–15% of $\Delta P$ (vs ~50–60% in an orifice).
- ✅ **Units rule:** lengths in **m**, area in **m²**, $g$ in **m/s²**, $\rho$ in **kg/m³**, $Q$ in **m³/s** (convert to L/s at the end by multiplying by 1000).
- ✅ **Three assumptions of Bernoulli:** steady flow, ideal (inviscid) fluid, incompressible fluid, along a streamline.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 exam focus:** numerical problems with mercury manometer, derivation of the discharge formula, comparison with orifice meter, and identification of parts in a diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demonstration of Different Types of Pumps
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstration of Different Types of Pumps

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump (KTU 2024 Definition):** A pump is a mechanical device that uses kinetic energy, centrifugal force, or displacement action to move fluids (liquids, slurries, or gases) by mechanical action, converting rotational kinetic energy into hydrodynamic energy of the fluid mass. In the food processing industry, pumps are classified as **sanitary/hygienic pumps** that comply with **3-A Sanitary Standards** and **EHEDG (European Hygienic Engineering & Design Group)** guidelines.

A pump, in the context of **Food Technology**, is a critical unit operation equipment used to transfer raw materials, intermediate products, and finished food items through various processing stages such as cleaning, pasteurization, homogenization, filling, and packaging.

### 1.1.1 Primary Functional Role in Food Processing

| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| **Fluid Transfer** | Moves raw milk, fruit juices, oils, sauces, and beverages between processing stages |
| **Pressure Generation** | Provides required pressure for downstream processes like filtration, pasteurization, and homogenization |
| **Metering / Dosing** | Delivers precise volumes of ingredients (sugar syrups, flavors) in batching operations |
| **CIP (Clean-In-Place) Support** | Circulates cleaning chemicals through processing lines at defined velocities |
| **Vacuum Generation** | Indirect function via suction side; used in de-aeration, vacuum concentration |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a pump as the "HEART" of a food processing plant.**

Just as the human heart continuously pumps blood through arteries and veins to deliver oxygen and nutrients, a pump circulates food materials through the network of pipes, heat exchangers, separators, and packaging machines. The heart has different chambers and valves (analogous to **impellers** and **check valves** in pumps), and it works in cycles (similar to **reciprocating pumps**) or in continuous rotation (similar to **centrifugal pumps**).

A simpler analogy is a **bicycle air pump** — when you push the handle down, a piston compresses air and forces it through the nozzle. This is essentially a **reciprocating positive displacement pump**. Replace the manual piston with a rotating screw, and you get a **rotary positive displacement pump**. Replace the piston with a spinning impeller, and you get a **centrifugal pump**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Units)

- **Flow Rate (Q):** Volume of fluid delivered per unit time, measured in **m³/h** or **L/s** or **GPM (Gallons Per Minute)**.
- **Head (H):** Energy imparted per unit weight of fluid, measured in **metres (m)** of fluid column.
- **Power (P):** Energy consumed per unit time, measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)** or **Horsepower (HP)**.
  * **1 HP = 745.7 W ≈ 0.746 kW**
- **Efficiency (η):** Ratio of fluid power output to mechanical/electrical power input (dimensionless, expressed as %).
- **NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head):** Margin above fluid vapor pressure at the pump suction port to prevent **cavitation**, measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Specific Speed (Ns):** Characteristic dimensionless number classifying pump type, defined mathematically in Section 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GDFTL106):** Students must be able to **identify, classify, and demonstrate** the working of at least **three major pump types** commonly used in food processing laboratories and industries, namely Centrifugal, Reciprocating, and Rotary pumps.

## 1.4 General Classification Overview

Pumps are broadly divided into two fundamental categories based on the energy-transfer mechanism:

1. **Centrifugal (Dynamic / Kinetic) Pumps** — Use rotational kinetic energy of an impeller to impart velocity to the fluid, which is then converted to pressure in the volute casing.
2. **Positive Displacement (PD) Pumps** — Use mechanical action to displace a fixed volume of fluid per cycle, regardless of system pressure (within design limits).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Centrifugal = Continuous Spin; Positive Displacement = Fixed Volume Trapped and Pushed."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Pumps Used in Food Technology

### 2.1.1 Centrifugal Pumps (Dynamic Pumps)

**Operating Principle:** Fluid enters axially at the impeller eye (suction inlet) and is flung radially outward by the high-speed rotation of the curved impeller vanes. The kinetic energy thus gained is converted into pressure energy in the surrounding volute or diffuser casing.

**Sub-types Encountered in Food Labs:**

| Sub-type | Construction Feature | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|
| **End-suction Centrifugal** | Single impeller, axial suction, radial discharge | Water transfer, milk reception, CIP return |
| **Close-coupled / Sanitary Centrifugal** | Impeller mounted directly on motor shaft, polished SS-316 surfaces | Dairy, beverage, pharmaceutical |
| **Self-priming Centrifugal** | Built-in air-evacuation chamber | Suction-line de-aeration in breweries |
| **Multistage Centrifugal** | 2 or more impellers in series | High-pressure cleaning (CIP), RO feed |

**Why Centrifugal Pumps Dominate in Food Industries:**
- Simple construction, fewer moving parts → easy to clean (CIP-compatible).
- Smooth, pulseless flow → ideal for **shear-sensitive** fluids like cream, cultured dairy products.
- Handles liquids with low to moderate viscosity efficiently.

**Limitation:** Efficiency drops sharply for **high-viscosity** foods (honey, ketchup, chocolate) and for fluids containing delicate solids.

### 2.1.2 Reciprocating Pumps (Positive Displacement)

**Operating Principle:** A piston or plunger moves back and forth inside a cylinder. During the **suction stroke**, a low-pressure region draws fluid in through a one-way **suction valve**. During the **delivery stroke**, the fluid is forced out through a one-way **delivery valve**.

**Sub-types:**

- **Piston Pump** — Piston reciprocates in a cylinder; common in batching applications.
- **Plunger Pump** — Plunger (longer and sealed) reciprocates in a packing gland; used for high-pressure duties like homogenization feed.
- **Diaphragm Pump** — A flexible elastomeric diaphragm replaces the piston, eliminating leakage and contamination; **widely used for sanitary food applications**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanitary Diaphragm Pumps** are the workhorse of the food industry for handling viscous, particulate-laden, or foaming products (e.g., tomato concentrate, fruit pulps, mayonnaise) because the product only contacts the diaphragm and the wetted casing — no rotating seals.

### 2.1.3 Rotary Pumps (Positive Displacement)

**Operating Principle:** Rotating elements (gears, lobes, screws, vanes, rollers) trap a fixed volume of fluid between the rotor and casing and push it from suction to discharge.

| Type | Rotating Element | Food Industry Use |
|---|---|---|
| **External Gear Pump** | Two meshing external gears | Oils, fats, chocolate, sugar syrup |
| **Internal Gear Pump** | Outer rotor with internal teeth + inner rotor | High-viscosity sauces, peanut butter |
| **Lobe Pump** | Two or three bi-lobe/tri-lobe rotors | Yogurt with fruit pieces, curd, ice cream mix |
| **Progressive Cavity (Mono) Pump** | Helical rotor in a double-thread stator | Highly viscous, shear-sensitive foods (mustard, hummus) |
| **Vane Pump** | Sliding vanes in an eccentric rotor | Solvents, light oils (less common in foods) |
| **Peristaltic (Tube) Pump** | Rollers compress a flexible tube | Sterile, aseptic dosing in pharmaceuticals & food |

### 2.1.4 Special Sanitary Pumps (Industry-Specific)

- **Magnetic Drive Pumps** — Sealless; torque transmitted via permanent magnets; **zero leakage**, used for sterile and toxic fluids.
- **Air-Operated Double Diaphragm (AODD) Pumps** — Pneumatic drive; ideal for flammable, viscous, or abrasive food slurries.
- **Piston Pumps with Piggable Piping** — Allow in-place cleaning without disassembly.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Why and How Each Pump Works

### 2.2.1 Centrifugal Pump — The "Why"

- **Why use an impeller?** Rotational motion is the most energy-efficient way to accelerate a fluid continuously without reciprocating masses (lower inertia, no valve wear).
- **How does the head develop?** The Euler equation (Section 3) shows that head $H$ is proportional to $u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta} - u_1 \cdot c_{1\theta}$, where $u$ is blade tip speed and $c_\theta$ is the tangential component of absolute velocity.

### 2.2.2 Reciprocating Pump — The "Why"

- **Why use reciprocating action?** It can generate very high pressures independent of rotational speed — perfect for discharge heads exceeding 100 m.
- **How does displacement work?** Each stroke traps a volume $V_s$ and displaces it, giving **theoretical discharge** $Q = N \cdot V_s \cdot n$ where $N$ is number of cylinders and $n$ is strokes per second.

### 2.2.3 Rotary Pump — The "Why"

- **Why are rotary PD pumps popular in food?** They combine the **pulsation-free flow** of centrifugal pumps with the **high-pressure, viscosity-handling capability** of reciprocating pumps, while remaining CIP-friendly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL — Exam Must-Knows)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All equations below are listed in their final, examiner-friendly form. Memorize the variable meanings and unit conventions.**

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$ = discharge, $A$ = pipe area, $v$ = velocity | m³/s ; m² ; m/s | General volumetric flow |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{P}{\rho g} + z + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $H$ = total head, $P$ = pressure, $\rho$ = density, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $z$ = elevation | m | Bernoulli-based total head |
| 3 | $P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Fluid (water) power delivered by pump | W (kg·m²/s³) | Pump power output |
| 4 | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} \times 100$ | Pump efficiency, $P_{shaft}$ = mechanical input | % | Efficiency calculation |
| 5 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | Required shaft power (input) | W | Motor selection |
| 6 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | Motor input power | W | Brake power sizing |
| 7 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed, $N$ = rpm, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m | dimensionless | Pump type selection |
| 8 | $Slip \% = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | Slip = leakage loss in PD pumps | % | PD pump performance |
| 9 | $Q_{th} = A_{piston} \cdot L \cdot N \cdot n$ | Theoretical discharge of reciprocating pump | m³/s | Reciprocating pump |
| 10 | $V_{r} = \pi \cdot B \cdot (R_o^2 - R_i^2)$ | Volume per revolution of a gear pump | m³/rev | Gear pump sizing |
| 11 | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} \pm z_s - h_f$ | Available NPSH; $h_f$ = friction loss | m | Cavitation prevention |
| 12 | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Peripheral (blade tip) velocity, $D$ = impeller dia | m/s | Centrifugal pump Euler eqn |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** In the formula table, students often write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in LaTeX to avoid markdown table-breaking issues (as mandated by the engine protocol).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** Centrifugal pumps for raw milk reception; lobe pumps for curd; piston pumps for butter churns.
- **Beverage Industry:** Peristaltic pumps for aseptic filling; centrifugal for clear juice transfer; mono pumps for fruit concentrate.
- **Bakery & Confectionery:** Gear pumps for chocolate and fat transfer; diaphragm pumps for sugar paste.
- **Brewery:** Centrifugal pumps for wort transfer; progressive cavity pumps for spent grain slurries.
- **Meat & Seafood:** AODD pumps for viscous slurries; magnetic drive pumps for sterile broths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Euler's Equation for Centrifugal Pump Head

The Euler turbo-machinery equation relates the energy transferred per unit weight of fluid to the change in angular momentum of the fluid as it passes through the impeller.

### Step 1: Define the System and Forces

Consider an ideal impeller rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). Fluid enters at the impeller eye (radius $r_1$, absolute velocity $c_1$, blade velocity $u_1$) and exits at the tip (radius $r_2$, absolute velocity $c_2$, blade velocity $u_2$).

The torque $T$ exerted on the fluid by the impeller is given by the rate of change of angular momentum:

$$T = \dot{m} \cdot (c_{2\theta} \cdot r_2 - c_{1\theta} \cdot r_1)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate (kg/s) and $c_{1\theta}, c_{2\theta}$ are the tangential components of absolute velocity.

### Step 2: Relate Torque to Power

The mechanical power supplied to the fluid is:

$$P_{shaft} = T \cdot \omega = \dot{m} \cdot \omega \cdot (c_{2\theta} \cdot r_2 - c_{1\theta} \cdot r_1)$$

### Step 3: Express Blade Velocities

The peripheral (blade) velocity at any radius $r$ is:

$$u = \omega \cdot r = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$$

where $D$ is the impeller diameter and $N$ is the rotational speed in rpm.

### Step 4: Substitute $u = \omega r$ into the Power Equation

$$P_{shaft} = \dot{m} \cdot (u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta} - u_1 \cdot c_{1\theta})$$

### Step 5: Convert Power to Head

The fluid power can also be written as $P_{water} = \rho g Q H$. Equating and using $\dot{m} = \rho Q$:

$$\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = \rho \cdot Q \cdot (u_2 c_{2\theta} - u_1 c_{1\theta})$$

Cancel $\rho \cdot Q$ from both sides:

$$g \cdot H = u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta} - u_1 \cdot c_{1\theta}$$

### Step 6: Final Euler Head Equation

$$\boxed{H = \frac{u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta} - u_1 \cdot c_{1\theta}}{g}}$$

This is the **fundamental theoretical head equation** for any turbo-machine. For a **radial-flow centrifugal pump** with no pre-swirl (which is the standard assumption for food-industry sanitary pumps), $c_{1\theta} \approx 0$, and the equation simplifies to:

$$H = \frac{u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta}}{g}$$

This simplified form is what is evaluated in the numerical example below.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Discharge of a Reciprocating Pump

### Step 1: Define Geometric Parameters

- $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston/plunger (m²)
- $L$ = length of stroke (m)
- $N$ = number of cylinders
- $n$ = number of strokes per second (for double-acting: $n$ counts both directions)

### Step 2: Volume Displaced Per Stroke

For a single-acting, single-cylinder pump, one complete cycle displaces exactly one cylinder volume:

$$V_s = A \cdot L \quad (\text{m}^3 \text{ per stroke})$$

### Step 3: Number of Deliveries Per Second

In one second, the pump completes $n$ cycles, and each cycle delivers fluid once. For $N$ identical cylinders working in parallel (in phase or out of phase):

$$\text{Deliveries per second} = N \cdot n$$

### Step 4: Theoretical Discharge

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot n \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})}$$

### Step 5: Account for Slip (Real Pump)

In practice, a fraction of the fluid leaks back through valves and clearances. The slip is:

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{actual}$$

And the volumetric efficiency is:

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Slip}}{Q_{th}}\right) \times 100$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Speed and Pump Selection

### Step 1: Why a New Parameter?

Different pump types perform best at different combinations of flow rate, head, and speed. A single dimensionless number, the **specific speed** $N_s$, classifies pumps by their geometry.

### Step 2: Dimensional Analysis Basis

By eliminating the rotational speed dependence between geometrically similar pumps, one obtains:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in **rpm**
- $Q$ = flow rate in **m³/s** (or GPM in US units)
- $H$ = head per stage in **m** (or ft in US units)

### Step 3: Interpretation Table — Selection Guide

| $N_s$ Range | Recommended Pump Type |
|---|---|
| $N_s < 500$ | **Radial-flow centrifugal** (Francis-type, low $Q$, high $H$) |
| $500 \le N_s \le 4000$ | **Mixed-flow centrifugal** (medium $Q$, medium $H$) |
| $4000 \le N_s \le 10000$ | **Axial-flow centrifugal** (high $Q$, low $H$) |
| $N_s > 10000$ | **Positive displacement** (reciprocating/rotary) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Low $N_s$ = Radial, High $N_s$ = Axial / PD."*

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Typical 7-Mark KTU Pattern):** A centrifugal pump delivers **0.025 m³/s** of water against a head of **30 m**. The impeller diameter is **300 mm** and runs at **1450 rpm**. The tangential component of absolute velocity at the exit is **8 m/s**. Calculate: (a) Blade tip velocity, (b) Theoretical head developed, (c) Shaft power if overall efficiency is **70%**.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 0.025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H = 30 \text{ m}$
- $D = 0.300 \text{ m}$
- $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$
- $c_{2\theta} = 8 \text{ m/s}$
- $\eta = 70\% = 0.70$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### Part (a): Blade Tip Velocity $u_2$

Using the relation $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$:

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.300 \times 1450}{60}$$

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 435}{60} = \frac{1366.89}{60}$$

$$\boxed{u_2 = 22.78 \text{ m/s}}$$

### Part (b): Theoretical Head $H_{th}$

Assuming no pre-swirl ($c_{1\theta} = 0$) — standard assumption for sanitary centrifugal pumps:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta}}{g} = \frac{22.78 \times 8}{9.81}$$

$$H_{th} = \frac{182.24}{9.81} = 18.58 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{th} = 18.58 \text{ m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The theoretical head (18.58 m) is **less than** the actual delivered head (30 m) because the simplified Euler equation neglects the volute and diffuser pressure-recovery contributions. In KTU exams, this comparison itself can be a 2-mark sub-question.

### Part (c): Shaft Power $P_{shaft}$

Fluid (water) power output:

$$P_{water} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 30$$

$$P_{water} = 7357.5 \text{ W} = 7.36 \text{ kW}$$

Shaft power required (input):

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta} = \frac{7357.5}{0.70}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = 10510.7 \text{ W} \approx 10.51 \text{ kW} \approx 14.1 \text{ HP}}$$

### Conversion to Horsepower (for completeness)

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{10510.7}{745.7} = 14.10 \text{ HP}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Exam Ready)

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump | Rotary (Lobe/Gear) Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow Type** | Continuous, pulseless | Pulsating, cyclic | Continuous, near-pulseless |
| **Pressure Capability** | Low–Medium (up to ~15 bar) | Very High (up to ~700 bar) | Medium–High (up to ~30 bar) |
| **Viscosity Suitability** | Low to moderate (< 500 cP) | Low to very high | Moderate to very high (up to 100,000 cP) |
| **Solid Handling** | Limited (clog risk) | Limited | Good (lobe pumps) |
| **Shear to Product** | Moderate | Low (diaphragm type) | Low–Moderate |
| **CIP Compatibility** | Excellent | Good (diaphragm type) | Excellent |
| **Self-Priming** | Poor (needs priming) | Good | Good |
| **Flow vs. Pressure Relation** | $Q$ decreases as $H$ increases | $Q$ nearly constant regardless of $H$ | $Q$ nearly constant regardless of $H$ |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | N/A (kinetic) | 85–98% | 80–95% |
| **Typical Food Use** | Milk, water, clear juice | CIP, high-pressure dosing | Chocolate, sauce, yogurt |
| **Cost (per kW)** | Lowest | Highest | Medium |

---

## 3.6 Demonstration Protocol — Lab Procedure Outline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Evaluation Note (GDFTL106):** During the viva, students are expected to describe the **dismantled components**, the **direction of fluid flow**, and the **operational sequence** of each pump shown.

**Standard 5-Step Demonstration Sequence:**

1. **Identification** — Name the pump type, manufacturer, model, and rated specifications from the nameplate.
2. **Component Listing** — Enumerate all wetted and non-wetted parts (impeller/rotor, casing, shaft, seals, bearings, valves, motor).
3. **Flow Path Tracing** — Manually trace the fluid route from suction inlet → pumping element → discharge outlet.
4. **Principle Explanation** — State whether it is dynamic or PD, and identify the energy-transfer mechanism.
5. **Start-Stop & Performance Check** — Energize the motor, observe flow and pressure gauge readings, and record the steady-state operating point on the **H-Q curve**.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Shortcut:** If the examiner asks *"Why is this pump suitable for food applications?"* — answer with three keywords: **CIP-compatible**, **sanitary (SS-316) construction**, and **EHEDG-compliant design**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Master Classification of Pumps in Food Technology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PUMPS FOR FOOD TECHNOLOGY"] --> B["CENTRIFUGAL DYNAMIC PUMPS"]
    A --> C["POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT PUMPS"]
    A --> D["SPECIAL SANITARY PUMPS"]

    B --> B1["END SUCTION CENTRIFUGAL"]
    B --> B2["SANITARY CLOSE COUPLED"]
    B --> B3["SELF PRIMING"]
    B --> B4["MULTISTAGE CENTRIFUGAL"]

    C --> C1["RECIPROCATING PD PUMPS"]
    C --> C2["ROTARY PD PUMPS"]

    C1 --> C1a["PISTON PUMP"]
    C1 --> C1b["PLUNGER PUMP"]
    C1 --> C1c["DIAPHRAGM PUMP"]

    C2 --> C2a["EXTERNAL GEAR PUMP"]
    C2 --> C2b["INTERNAL GEAR PUMP"]
    C2 --> C2c["LOBE PUMP"]
    C2 --> C2d["PROGRESSIVE CAVITY PUMP"]
    C2 --> C2e["PERISTALTIC PUMP"]

    D --> D1["MAGNETIC DRIVE PUMP"]
    D --> D2["AODD PNEUMATIC PUMP"]
    D --> D3["SEALLESS PIGGABLE PUMP"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `B1`, `C2d`), no reserved keywords used, and labels contain raw uppercase text only (no markdown bold or HTML inside quoted labels).

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SUCTION TANK"] -- SUCTION LINE --> I1["IMPELLER EYE R1"]
    I1 -- ROTATIONAL ACCELERATION --> I2["IMPELLER TIP R2"]
    I2 -- KINETIC ENERGY --> V1["VOLUTE / DIFFUSER CASING"]
    V1 -- PRESSURE CONVERSION --> D1["DISCHARGE OUTLET"]
    M1["ELECTRIC MOTOR"] -- SHAFT POWER --> I1
    V1 -- VELOCITY DECREASE --> C1["CIRCULATION BACK TO IMPELLER EYE SLIP"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow Diagram — Reciprocating Pump Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["PISTON AT TOP DEAD CENTRE"] -- SUCTION STROKE --> P2["PISTON MOVES DOWNWARD"]
    P2 -- LOW PRESSURE CAVITY --> P3["SUCTION VALVE OPENS"]
    P3 --> P4["FLUID ENTERS CYLINDER"]
    P4 -- PISTON AT BOTTOM DEAD CENTRE --> P5["DELIVERY STROKE BEGINS"]
    P5 -- PISTON MOVES UPWARD --> P6["DELIVERY VALVE OPENS"]
    P6 --> P7["FLUID DISCHARGED TO OUTLET"]
    P7 -- PISTON BACK TO TDC --> P1
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow Diagram — Rotary (Lobe) Pump Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["INLET SUCTION PORT"] --> L1["TWO LOBED ROTORS MESHING"]
    L1 -- ROTATION 90 DEG --> L2["FLUID TRAPPED BETWEEN LOBE AND CASING"]
    L2 -- ROTATION 180 DEG --> L3["FLUID CARRIED TO DISCHARGE SIDE"]
    L3 -- ROTATION 270 DEG --> L4["MESHING LOBES COMPRESS FLUID"]
    L4 --> O1["FLUID FORCED OUT DISCHARGE PORT"]
    O1 --> O2["OUTLET TO PROCESS LINE"]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["WHAT IS THE FLUID VISCOSITY?"]
    Q1 -- LOW UNDER 500 cP --> P1["CENTRIFUGAL PUMP"]
    Q1 -- MEDIUM 500 to 5000 cP --> P2["LOBE OR GEAR PUMP"]
    Q1 -- HIGH ABOVE 5000 cP --> P3["PROGRESSIVE CAVITY PUMP"]
    P1 --> CHK1["NEED HIGH PRESSURE?"]
    CHK1 -- NO --> SEL1["END SUCTION CENTRIFUGAL"]
    CHK1 -- YES --> SEL2["MULTISTAGE CENTRIFUGAL OR PLUNGER PUMP"]
    P2 --> SEL3["LOBE PUMP FOR SOLIDS BEARING FLUIDS"]
    P2 --> SEL4["GEAR PUMP FOR OILY NON ABRASIVE FLUIDS"]
    P3 --> SEL5["MONO SCREW PROGRESSIVE CAVITY PUMP"]
```

## 4.6 Component Anatomy — Sanitary Centrifugal Pump (Cross-Sectional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["MOTOR COVER TOP"] --> A2["MOTOR WINDING"]
    A2 --> A3["MOTOR SHAFT"]
    A3 --> A4["MECHANICAL SEAL OR MAGNETIC COUPLING"]
    A4 --> A5["IMPELLER SS 316 L POLISHED"]
    A5 --> A6["PUMP CASING VOLUTE SS 316 L"]
    A6 --> A7["SUCTION PORT SANITARY TRI CLAMP"]
    A6 --> A8["DISCHARGE PORT SANITARY TRI CLAMP"]
    A8 --> A9["FOOT MOUNTING BASE PLATE"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why SS-316L?** The "L" denotes low-carbon grade, which prevents chromium carbide precipitation at weld joints — critical for food contact and CIP chemical resistance.
