**(b)** 0.75 g of an oxalic acid dihydrate sample (H₂C₂O₄·2H₂O, M = 126 g/mol) is dissolved in 500 mL of distilled water. 25 mL of this solution required 22.5 mL of NaOH for complete neutralization using phenolphthalein indicator. Calculate the normality and the strength (in g/L) of the NaOH solution. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Standardization (1 mark):** Standardization is the process of determining the exact concentration of a solution (secondary standard) by titrating it against a solution whose concentration is precisely known (primary standard).

**Principle (2 marks):** Acid-base titration is based on the neutralization reaction between an acid and a base:

$$\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The titration curve plots pH (y-axis) vs. volume of titrant added (x-axis). For a strong acid (HCl) titrated with a strong base (NaOH), the curve shows:
- A gradual rise in pH in the buffer region (initial flat portion).
- A **sharp vertical rise** near the equivalence point (pH ≈ 7).
- A plateau after the equivalence point as excess NaOH is added.

**Role of Indicator (2 marks):** The indicator is a weak organic acid or base that exhibits a sharp color change within a narrow pH range. For strong acid vs strong base titration, indicators like bromothymol blue (pH 6.0–7.6) or phenolphthalein (pH 8.2–10.0) are suitable because their transition ranges overlap with the steep portion of the curve. The indicator signals the end point, which closely approximates the equivalence point.

**Titration Curve Description (2 marks):** At half-equivalence, pH = pKa. Near equivalence, even a single drop of titrant causes a pH jump of 4–6 units. This steep jump is what allows the indicator to function accurately.

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Mass of oxalic acid dihydrate = 0.75 g
- Formula mass of H₂C₂O₄·2H₂O = 126 g/mol
- n-factor of oxalic acid = 2
- Equivalent mass $E = \dfrac{126}{2} = 63$ g/eq
- Volume of oxalic acid solution = 500 mL

**Step 1: Calculate Normality of Oxalic Acid Solution (2 marks)**

Applying $N = \dfrac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{mL}}$:

$$N_{\text{acid}} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 1000}{63 \times 500}$$

$$N_{\text{acid}} = \dfrac{750}{31500}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{acid}} = 0.0238 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Normality Equation (3 marks)**

Let subscript 1 = NaOH (titrant), subscript 2 = Oxalic acid (analyte).

$$N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$$

$$N_{\text{NaOH}} \times 22.5 = 0.0238 \times 25$$

$$N_{\text{NaOH}} = \dfrac{0.0238 \times 25}{22.5}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.0264 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3: Convert NaOH Normality to Strength in g/L (2 marks)**

Equivalent mass of NaOH = 40 g/eq.

$$\text{Strength} = N \times E$$

$$\text{Strength} = 0.0264 \times 40$$

$$\boxed{\text{Strength of NaOH} = 1.058 \text{ g/L}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe in detail the procedure for the standardization of NaOH solution using oxalic acid dihydrate as a primary standard. Mention the apparatus, chemicals, indicator, and the calculations involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** 1.325 g of anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃, M = 106 g/mol) is dissolved in 250 mL of water. 20 mL of this solution required 19.8 mL of HCl for complete neutralization using methyl orange indicator. Calculate the normality and the strength (in g/L) of the HCl solution. State the type of reaction and the equivalence point pH. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Model Answer (7 Marks)

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):** Burette (50 mL), pipette (20 mL), volumetric flask (250 mL), conical flask (250 mL), analytical balance, wash bottle, funnel, stand with clamp.

**Chemicals Required (1 mark):**
- Oxalic acid dihydrate (H₂C₂O₄·2H₂O) — primary standard
- Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets — secondary standard to be standardized
- Phenolphthalein indicator solution
- CO₂-free distilled water

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Accurately weigh **1.575 g of oxalic acid dihydrate** (to make ~0.1 N in 250 mL) on an analytical balance.
2. Dissolve in CO₂-free distilled water and transfer quantitatively into a 250 mL volumetric flask. Make up to the mark.
3. Pipette out 20 mL of this oxalic acid solution into a clean conical flask.
4. Add 2–3 drops of phenolphthalein indicator. The solution remains **colorless** (acidic).
5. Rinse and fill the burette with the prepared NaOH solution. Note the initial reading.
6. Titrate the oxalic acid against NaOH slowly, with continuous swirling, until a **faint pink color** appears and persists for at least 30 seconds.
7. Note the final burette reading. The volume of NaOH used = Final − Initial.
8. Repeat the titration to obtain **at least three concordant readings** (within ±0.1 mL).
9. Calculate the normality of NaOH using $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ (where subscript 1 = NaOH, subscript 2 = oxalic acid).

**Calculations Involved (1 mark):**

$$N_{\text{NaOH}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{oxalic acid}} \times V_{\text{oxalic acid}}}{V_{\text{NaOH}}}$$

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Mass of Na₂CO₃ = 1.325 g
- Molar mass of Na₂CO₃ = 106 g/mol
- n-factor of Na₂CO₃ = 2 (since it reacts with 2 H⁺ ions)
- Equivalent mass $E = \dfrac{106}{2} = 53$ g/eq
- Volume of Na₂CO₃ solution = 250 mL

**Step 1: Calculate Normality of Na₂CO₃ Solution (2 marks)**

$$N_{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3} = \dfrac{1.325 \times 1000}{53 \times 250}$$

$$N_{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3} = \dfrac{1325}{13250}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3} = 0.1 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Normality Equation (2 marks)**

$$N_{\text{HCl}} \times 19.8 = 0.1 \times 20$$

$$N_{\text{HCl}} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 20}{19.8}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{HCl}} = 0.101 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Strength of HCl in g/L (2 marks)**

Equivalent mass of HCl = 36.5 g/eq.

$$\text{Strength} = 0.101 \times 36.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{Strength of HCl} = 3.686 \text{ g/L}}$$

**Step 4: Type of Reaction and Equivalence pH (1 mark):**
- Reaction type: **Strong acid–weak base neutralization** (HCl vs. Na₂CO₃ hydrolyzes to form H₂CO₃/CO₂).
- Equivalence point pH: **< 7 (acidic, approximately pH 3.7–4.0)**, hence methyl orange indicator is appropriate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Error in Normality:** Students frequently write normality without units (eq/L) or simply write "N" without specifying the volume basis. Always write: $\text{0.0444 N (or eq/L)}$.
> 2. **Confusion of n-factor for Na₂CO₃:** Many students forget that Na₂CO₃ has n-factor = 2 (because it reacts with 2 moles of HCl). Using E = 106 instead of 53 loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Indicator Mismatch:** Using phenolphthalein for Na₂CO₃–HCl gives an erratic end point due to CO₂ liberation. KTU examiners specifically test indicator knowledge.
> 4. **Final Answer Without Significant Figures:** Carrying only 2 sig figs through the calculation loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Burette Reading Errors:** Initial reading must be subtracted from final reading correctly. Reading from top to bottom of meniscus convention must be followed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Standardization = Determination of exact concentration of a secondary standard solution using a primary standard.**
- **Primary Standard Requirements:** High purity ✓, Stability ✓, Non-hygroscopic ✓, High molar/equivalent mass ✓, Known stoichiometry ✓, Solubility ✓.
- **Oxalic acid dihydrate** is the classic primary standard for bases (NaOH, KOH); **anhydrous Na₂CO₃** is the classic primary standard for acids (HCl, H₂SO₄).
- **Master Equation:** $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ — applicable **only at the equivalence point**.
- **Equivalent Mass Formula:** $E = \dfrac{\text{Molar Mass}}{n\text{-factor}}$, where n-factor = basicity for acids, acidity for bases.
- **Phenolphthalein** (colorless → pink, pH 8.2–10.0) for weak acid–strong base titrations.
- **Methyl orange** (red → yellow, pH 3.1–4.4) for strong acid–weak base titrations.
- **Concordant Readings:** At least three readings should agree within ±0.1 mL.
- **Burette Reading Convention:** Always read the bottom of the meniscus at eye level.
- **Titration Curve:** Sharp vertical jump near equivalence point allows visual detection.
- **Strength in g/L** = Normality × Equivalent mass.
- **Practical Tip:** Always use CO₂-free distilled water for NaOH preparation to prevent carbonate formation.
- **Real-World Food Lab Application:** Standardized NaOH is used to determine **% titratable acidity** of milk, vinegar, fruit juices, and wine — making this the foundational skill for all food quality testing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Specific Gravity and Viscosity of the Given Sample
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GDFTL106 - FOUNDATIONS OF FOOD TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Specific Gravity and Viscosity of the Given Sample

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Determination of Specific Gravity and Viscosity of the Given Sample

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Specific Gravity (Relative Density)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Specific Gravity (SG), also known as **Relative Density (RD)**, is defined as the ratio of the density of a given substance to the density of a reference substance, measured under standard conditions. For liquids, the reference substance is **pure water at 4 °C (277.15 K)**, where water has its maximum density of **1.000 g/cm³** (or 1000 kg/m³).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *Specific Gravity is a dimensionless quantity (no units) that expresses how many times a substance is heavier or lighter than an equal volume of water at 4 °C.*

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{substance}}{\rho_{water \ at \ 4°C}}$$

Where:
- $\rho_{substance}$ = Density of the given sample (in g/cm³ or kg/m³)
- $\rho_{water \ at \ 4°C}$ = Density of water at 4 °C = **1.000 g/cm³** = **1000 kg/m³**

### 1.2 Viscosity

**Formal Definition:**
Viscosity is the internal friction or **resistance of a fluid to deformation or flow**. It quantifies the internal resistive force per unit area when one layer of fluid moves relative to another adjacent layer under an applied shear stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Types of Viscosity – Must Remember:**
> 1. **Dynamic Viscosity (Absolute Viscosity, μ):** Measures the fluid's internal resistance to flow. Unit: **Pa·s** (SI) or **Poise (P)**; 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.
> 2. **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density. Unit: **m²/s** (SI) or **Stokes (St)**; 1 St = 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 – Specific Gravity:**
Imagine you have two identical buckets. Fill one with water and the other with honey. Both buckets have the same volume, but the bucket with honey is noticeably heavier. **Specific Gravity simply tells you "how many buckets of water" the honey weighs.** If honey's SG is 1.42, it means the honey bucket weighs 1.42 times more than the water bucket.

**Analogy 2 – Viscosity:**
Think of two liquids: water and cold honey. Pour both from the same height:
- Water flows **fast and freely** → Low viscosity
- Honey flows **slowly and thickly** → High viscosity

Viscosity is essentially the **"thickness" or "stickiness"** of a fluid. The thicker the fluid, the harder it is for its molecules to slide past one another, and hence the higher the viscosity.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Food Tech Examples:**
> - **Edible Oils:** SG ≈ 0.91–0.93 (lighter than water, hence oil floats on water)
> - **Honey:** SG ≈ 1.42, Viscosity ≈ 10,000 cP (very thick)
> - **Milk:** SG ≈ 1.028–1.035
> - **Tomato Ketchup:** Viscosity ≈ 50,000–100,000 cP (extremely thick)

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Density of water at 4 °C | **1.000 g/cm³** or **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of water at 20 °C | **0.9982 g/cm³** |
| Standard temperature for SG | 25 °C or 4 °C |
| 1 Poise (P) | **0.1 Pa·s** |
| 1 centipoise (cP) | **0.001 Pa·s** = 1 mPa·s |
| 1 Stokes (St) | **1 cm²/s** = 10⁻⁴ m²/s |
| 1 centistoke (cSt) | 0.01 St = 1 mm²/s |
| Standard gravity (g) | 9.81 m/s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Specific Gravity & Viscosity Matter in Food Technology:**
> These two parameters are **Quality Control Indicators** in food processing. They are used to:
> 1. Check the **purity and adulteration** of edible oils (SG deviation = adulteration).
> 2. Determine the **flow behavior** of liquid foods in pipelines (Pumps, Pipes, Heat Exchangers).
> 3. Calculate **Reynolds Number** for designing food processing equipment.
> 4. Validate **Brix and concentration** in syrups, juices, and concentrates.
> 5. Predict the **shelf-life and texture** of sauces, dressings, and beverages.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Determination of Specific Gravity — Three Standard Methods

### Method 1: Pycnometer (Specific Gravity Bottle) — *Most Common in KTU Labs*

The pycnometer is a **glass bottle of known fixed volume** (commonly 25 mL or 50 mL) with a ground-glass stopper that has a fine capillary bore. This allows excess liquid to escape and ensures a precise, reproducible volume.

**Procedure Logic:**

1. Weigh the **empty, clean, dry pycnometer** with the stopper → $W_1$
2. Fill with **distilled water**, insert stopper, wipe dry, weigh → $W_2$
3. Empty, dry, fill with the **given sample** (oil/syrup/milk), insert stopper, wipe dry, weigh → $W_3$

> [!NOTE]
> The capillary in the stopper ensures that exactly the same volume of water and sample are measured, eliminating the need to know the exact pycnometer volume.

### Method 2: Hydrometer (Less Precise, Industry Use)

A hydrometer is a **calibrated glass float** with a weighted bulb at the bottom and a graduated stem at the top. The depth to which it sinks is **inversely proportional to the liquid's density**. It is commonly used in dairies, breweries, and sugar industries.

### Method 3: Falling Ball / Falling Sphere Viscometer-Density Method

By measuring the terminal velocity of a sphere falling through a fluid, both viscosity and density can be determined. Used in research-grade applications.

## 2.2 Determination of Viscosity — Ostwald Viscometer Method

### 2.2.1 Instrument Principle

The **Ostwald Viscometer** (also called the U-tube viscometer) works on **Poiseuille's Law**. The time taken by a fixed volume of liquid to flow through a calibrated capillary tube under the influence of gravity is measured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poiseuille's Law (The Foundation Equation):**
> For a liquid flowing through a capillary under laminar flow conditions, the volume flow rate $Q$ is:
>
> $$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$
>
> Where $r$ = capillary radius, $L$ = capillary length, $\Delta P$ = pressure difference, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity.

### 2.2.2 Derivation of the Working Formula

For a fixed volume $V$ flowing through a capillary in time $t$ under gravity ($\Delta P = \rho g h$):

$$\eta_1 = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \rho_1 \cdot g \cdot h \cdot t_1}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$

For the same viscometer and volume, $\frac{\pi r^4 g h}{8 L V}$ is **constant**. Hence the ratio of viscosities of two liquids (sample vs. water) becomes:

$$\boxed{\frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}}$$

Where:
- $\eta_1, \rho_1, t_1$ = Viscosity, density, flow time of **sample**
- $\eta_2, \rho_2, t_2$ = Viscosity, density, flow time of **water** (reference)
- **Note:** Subscript 1 = Sample, Subscript 2 = Water (standard convention)

### 2.2.3 Procedure for Ostwald Viscometer

1. Clean the viscometer thoroughly with chromic acid, then distilled water, then acetone. Dry in an oven.
2. Introduce a known volume of **distilled water** using a pipette through the wider arm.
3. Place the viscometer vertically in a **thermostatic water bath at 25 °C** for 10–15 minutes for thermal equilibrium.
4. Use a rubber bulb to suck the water up above the **upper mark A** in the capillary.
5. Release and measure the time taken for the meniscus to fall from mark A to mark B → $t_{water}$.
6. Repeat the procedure with the **sample liquid** (oil/syrup/etc.) → $t_{sample}$.
7. Calculate viscosity using the working formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{sample}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific Gravity (fundamental) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $SG = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$ | Pycnometer method (practical) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density definition | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 4 | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | Dynamic vs. Kinematic viscosity relation | Pa·s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}$ | Ostwald viscometer working equation | Dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | $\eta_1 = \eta_{water} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}$ | Sample viscosity (solved) | cP or mPa·s |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity from dynamic | m²/s or cSt |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Poiseuille's Law (volume flow rate) | m³/s |
| 9 | $F_{viscous} = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' Law (falling ball) | N |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (uses viscosity) | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Shortcuts for KTU Exams:**
> - SG × 1000 = Density in **kg/m³** (if water density = 1000)
> - **1 cP = 1 mPa·s** (numerically equal to water at 20 °C)
> - Viscosity of water at **25 °C = 0.8937 cP** (often rounded to 0.89 cP)
> - Viscosity of water at **20 °C = 1.002 cP** (≈ 1 cP)

## 2.4 Why These Methods Are Used — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Method | Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|
| **Pycnometer** | Lab-scale precise measurement for quality control in oil refineries, dairy labs |
| **Hydrometer** | Quick field measurement in dairy (lactometer), sugar industry (Brix), alcohol % |
| **Ostwald Viscometer** | Comparative viscosity measurement in research labs, low-viscosity liquids |
| **Brookfield Viscometer** | Industrial viscosity of thick fluids (ketchup, paste, cream) |
| **Redwood Viscometer** | Petroleum and oil industries (high-temperature viscosity) |
| **Falling Ball** | Non-Newtonian, opaque, or highly viscous fluids (chocolate, jam) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Specific Gravity from Pycnometer Weights

**Setup:** Let $W_1$ = weight of empty pycnometer, $W_2$ = weight of pycnometer + water, $W_3$ = weight of pycnometer + sample.

**Step 1: Weight of water alone in the pycnometer**

$$W_{water} = W_2 - W_1$$

**Step 2: Weight of sample alone in the pycnometer**

$$W_{sample} = W_3 - W_1$$

**Step 3: Density of water (mass per unit volume V of pycnometer)**

$$\rho_{water} = \frac{W_{water}}{V} = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{V}$$

**Step 4: Density of sample (same volume V)**

$$\rho_{sample} = \frac{W_{sample}}{V} = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{V}$$

**Step 5: Specific Gravity = ratio of densities**

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{sample}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{\dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{V}}{\dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{V}}$$

**Step 6: Volume $V$ cancels out (the elegant trick)**

$$\boxed{SG = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The actual volume of the pycnometer need NOT be known. The ratio eliminates it. This is why the pycnometer method is so popular — we only need three weighings.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Ostwald Viscometer Working Equation

**Starting Principle:** Poiseuille's Law for laminar flow through a capillary.

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{8 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$$

Solving for viscosity:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$

**For the sample liquid (subscript 1):**

$$\eta_1 = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P_1 \cdot t_1}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$

**Substitute $\Delta P_1 = \rho_1 \cdot g \cdot h$ (hydrostatic head):**

$$\eta_1 = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \rho_1 \cdot g \cdot h \cdot t_1}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$

**For the reference liquid (water, subscript 2):**

$$\eta_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \rho_2 \cdot g \cdot h \cdot t_2}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}$$

**Take the ratio $\eta_1 / \eta_2$:**

$$\frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}$$

> The factors $\pi r^4 g h$, $8 L V$ all **cancel** because they are properties of the viscometer, not the liquids.

**Solving for the sample viscosity:**

$$\boxed{\eta_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: Specific Gravity of Edible Oil (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 25 mL pycnometer weighs 18.42 g when empty. When filled with distilled water at 25 °C, it weighs 43.35 g. When filled with groundnut oil, it weighs 41.95 g. Calculate the specific gravity of the oil.

**Given Data:**

$$W_1 = 18.42 \text{ g (empty pycnometer)}$$
$$W_2 = 43.35 \text{ g (pycnometer + water)}$$
$$W_3 = 41.95 \text{ g (pycnometer + oil)}$$

**Step 1: Weight of water alone**

$$W_{water} = W_2 - W_1 = 43.35 - 18.42 = 24.93 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Weight of oil alone**

$$W_{oil} = W_3 - W_1 = 41.95 - 18.42 = 23.53 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Apply the specific gravity formula**

$$SG = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1} = \frac{23.53}{24.93}$$

**Step 4: Calculate final numerical value**

$$SG = 0.9438 \approx 0.944$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The groundnut oil has SG = 0.944 (dimensionless). Since SG < 1, the oil is **lighter than water** and will float — this matches real-world observation and confirms the experiment is correct.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: Viscosity of Sugar Syrup (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
The time of flow of water through an Ostwald viscometer at 25 °C is 48.6 seconds. For a sugar syrup sample, the flow time is 285.3 seconds. The density of water is 0.997 g/cm³ and the density of the syrup is 1.32 g/cm³. The viscosity of water at 25 °C is 0.8937 cP. Calculate the viscosity of the sugar syrup in cP.

**Given Data:**

$$t_2 = 48.6 \text{ s (water)}$$
$$t_1 = 285.3 \text{ s (syrup)}$$
$$\rho_2 = 0.997 \text{ g/cm}^3 \text{ (water)}$$
$$\rho_1 = 1.32 \text{ g/cm}^3 \text{ (syrup)}$$
$$\eta_2 = 0.8937 \text{ cP (water at 25 °C)}$$

**Step 1: Apply the Ostwald viscometer equation**

$$\eta_1 = \eta_2 \cdot \frac{\rho_1 \cdot t_1}{\rho_2 \cdot t_2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values**

$$\eta_1 = 0.8937 \cdot \frac{1.32 \times 285.3}{0.997 \times 48.6}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the numerator**

$$1.32 \times 285.3 = 376.596$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the denominator**

$$0.997 \times 48.6 = 48.4542$$

**Step 5: Compute the ratio**

$$\frac{376.596}{48.4542} = 7.7723$$

**Step 6: Multiply by $\eta_2$**

$$\eta_1 = 0.8937 \times 7.7723 = 6.945 \text{ cP}$$

**Step 7: Final answer with units**

$$\boxed{\eta_{syrup} = 6.95 \text{ cP} \approx 6.95 \text{ mPa.s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The sugar syrup is approximately **7.78 times more viscous than water** at 25 °C. This thick, viscous behavior is expected for concentrated sugar solutions and is critical for designing syrup-handling pipelines and pumps in food processing plants.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Traps

| Trap | Correct Approach |
|---|---|
| Forgetting to subtract empty weight | Always use $W_3 - W_1$ and $W_2 - W_1$, NOT $W_3$ and $W_2$ |
| Using wrong temperature viscosity of water | At 25 °C: $\eta_{water}$ = 0.8937 cP; At 20 °C: $\eta_{water}$ = 1.002 cP |
| Mixing up subscripts | Convention: Subscript 1 = Sample, Subscript 2 = Reference (water) |
| Confusing cP with cSt | cP is dynamic; cSt is kinematic. cSt = cP / (g/cm³) |
| Reporting SG with units | SG is **dimensionless**, no units. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Complete Experimental Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Sample Received] --> cleanA[Step 1: Clean and Dry Pycnometer]
    cleanA --> weighEmptyA[Step 2: Weigh Empty Pycnometer W1]
    weighEmptyA --> fillWaterA[Step 3: Fill with Distilled Water]
    fillWaterA --> weighWaterA[Step 4: Weigh Pycnometer Plus Water W2]
    weighWaterA --> emptyDryA[Step 5: Empty and Dry Pycnometer]
    emptyDryA --> fillSampleA[Step 6: Fill with Given Sample]
    fillSampleA --> weighSampleA[Step 7: Weigh Pycnometer Plus Sample W3]
    weighSampleA --> calcSG[Step 8: Calculate SG equals W3 minus W1 over W2 minus W1]
    calcSG --> sgResult[Step 9: Record Specific Gravity Result]

    startB[Start: Sample Received] --> cleanB[Step 1: Clean Ostwald Viscometer]
    cleanB --> mountB[Step 2: Mount Vertically in Water Bath 25C]
    mountB --> fillWaterB[Step 3: Pipette Distilled Water]
    fillWaterB --> thermEqB[Step 4: Wait 10 to 15 Minutes for Thermal Equilibrium]
    thermEqB --> suckWaterB[Step 5: Suck Water Above Upper Mark A]
    suckWaterB --> timeWaterB[Step 6: Measure Flow Time A to B equals t water]
    timeWaterB --> dryB[Step 7: Rinse Dry and Fill with Sample]
    dryB --> thermEqB2[Step 8: Re equilibrate 10 to 15 Minutes]
    thermEqB2 --> suckSampleB[Step 9: Suck Sample Above Mark A]
    suckSampleB --> timeSampleB[Step 10: Measure Flow Time equals t sample]
    timeSampleB --> calcVisc[Step 11: Calculate eta sample using Ostwald Equation]
    calcVisc --> viscResult[Step 12: Record Viscosity Result in cP]

    sgResult --> finalReport[Final Lab Report: SG and Viscosity Both Determined]
    viscResult --> finalReport
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Ostwald Viscometer Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upper Reservoir Arm A] --> B[Capillary Tube Section L]
    B --> C[Bulb of Volume V]
    C --> D[Lower Reservoir Arm]
    D --> E[Fill Here via Pipette]
    F[Mark M1 Upper] --> G[Mark M2 Lower]
    G --> H[Measure Flow Time Between M1 and M2]
```

## 4.3 Pycnometer (Specific Gravity Bottle) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Glass Bottle of Known Volume] --> P2[Ground Glass Stopper]
    P2 --> P3[Fine Capillary Bore in Stopper]
    P3 --> P4[Allows Excess Liquid to Escape]
    P4 --> P5[Ensures Fixed Accurate Volume V]
    P5 --> P6[Weigh in Three States: Empty, Plus Water, Plus Sample]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: Which Method to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the Food Sample?] --> Q2{Is it a Liquid?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Need High Precision?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4SG[Use Pycnometer Method for SG]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5SG[Use Hydrometer Method]
    Q2 -- No --> Q6SG[Use Density Bottle for Solids or Powders]

    Q1 --> Q7{Viscosity Needed?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8{Low to Medium Viscosity?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9V[Use Ostwald Viscometer]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10V{Transparent Liquid?}
    Q10V -- Yes --> Q11V[Use Falling Ball Viscometer]
    Q10V -- No --> Q12V[Use Brookfield Rotational Viscometer]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1. Define Specific Gravity. Why is it a dimensionless quantity? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Specific Gravity is the ratio of the density of a substance to the density of a reference substance (water at 4 °C for liquids). Mathematically, $SG = \rho_{substance} / \rho_{water}$. It is a dimensionless (unitless) quantity because it is a ratio of two similar physical quantities (both densities) — the units cancel out, leaving a pure number.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2. State the formula for determining viscosity using an Ostwald viscometer and explain the role of water as the reference liquid. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The Ostwald viscometer formula is:

$$\frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \frac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_2 t_2}$$

Water is used as the reference liquid because its viscosity at various temperatures is **accurately tabulated** and well-established (e.g., 0.8937 cP at 25 °C, 1.002 cP at 20 °C). The instrument-dependent constants (capillary radius, volume, length) cancel out when the ratio is taken, allowing us to determine the sample viscosity relative to a known standard.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
> **Q3 (a) A 50 mL pycnometer weighs 32.50 g when empty, 82.45 g when filled with distilled water at 25 °C, and 78.92 g when filled with coconut oil at 25 °C. Calculate the specific gravity and density of the coconut oil. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $W_1 = 32.50$ g (empty pycnometer)
- $W_2 = 82.45$ g (pycnometer + water)
- $W_3 = 78.92$ g (pycnometer + coconut oil)
- Volume of pycnometer = 50 mL (Note: not actually needed for SG)

**Step 1: Weight of water alone**

$$W_{water} = W_2 - W_1 = 82.45 - 32.50 = 49.95 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Weight of coconut oil alone**

$$W_{oil} = W_3 - W_1 = 78.92 - 32.50 = 46.42 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Specific Gravity calculation**

$$SG = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1} = \frac{46.42}{49.95}$$

$$SG = 0.9293 \approx 0.929$$

**Step 4: Density of coconut oil**

Since $\rho_{water}$ at 25 °C = 0.997 g/cm³:

$$\rho_{oil} = SG \times \rho_{water} = 0.9293 \times 0.997 = 0.9265 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{SG_{oil} = 0.929, \quad \rho_{oil} = 0.927 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Result Interpretation:** The coconut oil is **lighter than water** (SG < 1), confirming the well-known fact that cooking oils float on water. This value matches the standard range (0.92–0.93) for pure coconut oil.

---

> **Q3 (b) The flow time of water through an Ostwald viscometer at 25 °C is 52.4 s. For a sample of honey solution, the flow time is 412.8 s. Given the density of water = 0.997 g/cm³, density of honey solution = 1.35 g/cm³, and viscosity of water at 25 °C = 0.8937 cP. Calculate the viscosity of the honey solution. Also comment on the flow behavior. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $t_2 = 52.4$ s (water), $t_1 = 412.8$ s (honey solution)
- $\rho_2 = 0.997$ g/cm³, $\rho_1 = 1.35$ g/cm³
- $\eta_2 = 0.8937$ cP

**Step 1: Apply the Ostwald viscometer equation**

$$\eta_1 = \eta_2 \times \frac{\rho_1 \times t_1}{\rho_2 \times t_2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$\eta_1 = 0.8937 \times \frac{1.35 \times 412.8}{0.997 \times 52.4}$$

**Step 3: Numerator**

$$1.35 \times 412.8 = 557.28$$

**Step 4: Denominator**

$$0.997 \times 52.4 = 52.2428$$

**Step 5: Ratio**

$$\frac{557.28}{52.2428} = 10.667$$

**Step 6: Final viscosity**

$$\eta_1 = 0.8937 \times 10.667 = 9.533 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{honey} = 9.53 \text{ cP}}$$

**Step 7: Flow Behavior Comment:**
The honey solution has a viscosity approximately **10.67 times that of water**. This is consistent with the high sugar content of honey — it flows much more slowly than water, exhibits resistance to flow, and would require larger pumping power in industrial pipelines. The high viscosity also contributes to honey's characteristic **slow pouring behavior** and mouthfeel in food applications.

## 5.3 Alternative Choice: Question 4 (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Alternative]**
> **Q4 (a) Explain the principle and procedure for determining the specific gravity of a liquid food sample using a pycnometer. List the precautions to be taken. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
The specific gravity bottle (pycnometer) is a glass vessel of fixed, known volume fitted with a ground-glass stopper having a fine capillary bore. By weighing the pycnometer empty, with water, and with the sample, and taking the ratio of (sample weight) to (water weight) for the same volume, the specific gravity is determined.

**Working Formula:**

$$SG = \frac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the pycnometer with chromic acid, rinse with distilled water, and dry in a hot air oven.
2. Weigh the empty pycnometer with stopper (W₁).
3. Fill with distilled water (free of air bubbles), insert stopper, wipe dry, and weigh (W₂).
4. Empty, rinse with sample, fill with the given food sample, insert stopper, wipe dry, and weigh (W₃).
5. Calculate SG using the formula above.

**Precautions:**
1. Pycnometer must be **completely dry** before weighing empty.
2. **No air bubbles** should be trapped when filling.
3. **Same temperature** must be maintained for water and sample.
4. The stopper must be inserted firmly; excess liquid must **overflow through the capillary** to ensure constant volume.
5. The pycnometer must be **wiped clean and dry externally** before each weighing.
6. Use the **same balance** throughout the experiment.

---

> **Q4 (b) Describe the Ostwald viscometer method for determining viscosity. State the assumptions made in deriving the formula. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Description:**
The Ostwald viscometer is a U-shaped glass tube with a capillary section and two bulbs. A known volume of liquid is introduced, and the time taken for the liquid meniscus to fall between two calibrated marks (M₁ to M₂) under gravity is measured using a stopwatch. The viscometer is immersed in a constant-temperature water bath (typically 25 °C) to eliminate temperature variations.

**Working Equation:**

$$\frac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \frac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_2 t_2}$$

**Assumptions in Derivation:**
1. **Laminar flow** through the capillary (Reynolds number < 2100).
2. **Steady-state flow** — no acceleration of fluid.
3. The liquid is **Newtonian** (viscosity is independent of shear rate).
4. The viscometer is held **perfectly vertical**.
5. **Constant temperature** throughout the experiment (no thermal expansion).
6. The volume of liquid measured between the two marks is **fixed and identical** for both liquids.
7. **No-slip condition** at the capillary walls.

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks — KTU Board Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract empty weight (W₁):** Writing SG = W₃ / W₂ is WRONG. Always use (W₃ − W₁) / (W₂ − W₁). **Lose 2–3 marks** if this is missed.
>
> 2. **Using wrong viscosity of water:** Students often assume $\eta_{water} = 1.0$ cP universally. But it is 1.002 cP at 20 °C and 0.8937 cP at 25 °C. The temperature must be specified. **Lose 1 mark** for wrong constant.
>
> 3. **Mixing up subscripts:** Subscript 1 = sample, Subscript 2 = water. Reversing this gives a wrong answer. **Lose 2 marks** for reversal.
>
> 4. **Reporting SG with units:** SG is dimensionless. Writing "SG = 0.93 g/cm³" is wrong. Write "SG = 0.93 (dimensionless)" or just "0.93".
>
> 5. **Not stating the temperature:** Always mention the temperature of measurement (e.g., 25 °C) in the final answer.
>
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always write the viscosity with proper units (cP, mPa·s, or Pa·s).
>
> 7. **Forgetting to explain precautions:** In 7-mark procedure questions, listing 3–4 key precautions earns the full mark. Skipping them loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Specific Gravity Definition:** Ratio of density of substance to density of water at 4 °C. **Dimensionless**, no units.
- ☐ **Pycnometer Formula:** $SG = \dfrac{W_3 - W_1}{W_2 - W_1}$ — the **workhorse** formula for SG experiments.
- ☐ **Ostwald Formula:** $\dfrac{\eta_1}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{\rho_1 t_1}{\rho_2 t_2}$ — used for **relative viscosity** measurement.
- ☐ **Viscosity of Water:** 0.8937 cP at **25 °C**, 1.002 cP at **20 °C** — must be known.
- ☐ **Density of Water:** 1.000 g/cm³ at **4 °C** (reference), 0.997 g/cm³ at **25 °C**.
- ☐ **Unit Conversions:** 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s = 100 cP; 1 St = 1 cm²/s = 100 cSt.
- ☐ **Dynamic vs. Kinematic:** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — cP is dynamic, cSt is kinematic.
- ☐ **Poiseuille's Law:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ — the theoretical foundation.
- ☐ **Subscript Convention:** 1 = Sample, 2 = Reference (water). **NEVER reverse.**
- ☐ **Real-world SG values:** Oils (0.91–0.93), Milk (1.028–1.035), Honey (1.42), Water (1.00).
- ☐ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ — uses viscosity; determines flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent).
- ☐ **Newtonian fluids:** Viscosity is constant (water, milk, oils). Non-Newtonian (ketchup, blood) require Brookfield viscometer.
- ☐ **Temperature control is critical** — viscosity decreases with temperature for most liquids.
- ☐ **Always state temperature** in the final answer (commonly 25 °C).
- ☐ **Precautions to list in procedure:** No air bubbles, vertical mounting, thermostatic bath, clean dry apparatus, same balance.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In numerical problems, **always show all steps** (given, formula, substitution, calculation, result). The KTU board awards step-marks. Even if the final answer has a calculation error, you will earn partial credit for correct method and substitution.
